title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: networks created equal. Many real-world networks bipartite, meaning two distinct types nodes, ties form types, never within . Students enroll courses. Countries sign treaties. Legislators join committees. Users purchase products. case, adjacency matrix rectangular rather square, row column nodes play fundamentally different roles. lame package supports bipartite networks ame() (cross-sectional) lame() (longitudinal). key difference unipartite case model uses separate latent spaces two node types, connected interaction matrix GG captures row-node column-node characteristics relate .

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: bipartite network, probability tie row node ii column node jj modeled : yij=β′xij+ai+bj+ui′Gvj+ϵijy_{ij} = \beta'x_{ij} + a_i + b_j + u_i'Gv_j + \epsilon_{ij} additive effects work just unipartite case: aia_i captures “active” row node ii (e.g., student takes many courses), bjb_j captures “popular” column node jj (e.g., course high enrollment). multiplicative term ui′Gvju_i'Gv_j different unipartite ui′vju_i'v_j. Row column nodes live potentially different-dimensional latent spaces (RrowR_\text{row} RcolR_\text{col}), GG Rrow×RcolR_\text{row} \times R_\text{col} interaction matrix. GG handled differs cross-sectional longitudinal entry points: ame() (cross-sectional bipartite), GG initialised rank-truncated scaled identity held fixed MCMC. rotation scaling interaction absorbed UU VV, GG estimated; identified overall multiplicative term UGV′U G V'. lame() (longitudinal bipartite), default MCMC estimator samples GG iteration conditional Gaussian via bipartite GG-sampler. dynamic_G = FALSE single GG shared across periods; dynamic_G = TRUE separate GtG_t drawn per period returned fit$G_cube (see section dynamic_G ). fast ALS path, lame(..., method = "als"), also supports dynamic_G = TRUE normal, binary, Poisson bipartite panels, including named panels row column actors enter exit; estimates penalized point path GtG_t rather sampling posterior draws. either case, bipartite parameterisation lets row-side latent positions (UU, shape nrow×Rrown_\text{row} \times R_\text{row}) column-side latent positions (VV, shape ncol×Rcoln_\text{col} \times R_\text{col}) chosen different ranks, substantive flexibility users get. terms absent bipartite setting. dyadic correlation parameter ρ\rho, ties inherently directional row column nodes (can’t reciprocal tie student–course network). additive effects aa bb also independent variances rather joint covariance, since describe fundamentally different types actors.

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: Every family works square network also works rectangular one. rectangular Z-samplers live R/rZ_bipartite.R treat cell independent given linear predictor (reciprocal-cell coupling bipartite graph). supported set: table applies lame() bipartite mode. dynamic_G = TRUE supported lame(). default MCMC path runs Carter-Kohn FFBS vec(G_t) AR(1) state-space prior returns posterior summaries plus rotation-drift diagnostic. ALS path estimates point path G_t normal, binary, Poisson families, honors separate R_row R_col ranks, aligns named changing-composition panels union row column actor sets. Use MCMC need posterior draws rank/censored families. demonstrate MCMC dynamic_G fit later vignette.

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: Bipartite tobit right choice outcome continuous quantity censored known boundary, trade volume country IGO records “exchange” zero rather missing, patient’s reported dose drug prescribed (also zero). Z-sampler treats observed zeros left-censored draws underlying latent intensity propagates censoring additive / multiplicative updates. Set family = "tobit" supply matrix recorded values (zeros allowed). Bipartite rrl (row-ranked likelihood) supported ame() lame(): rectangular Z-sampler rZ_rrl_bip_fc R/rZ_bipartite.R enforces per-row rank cone (Y_ij > Y_ik implies Z_ij > Z_ik within row ) without zero threshold without odmax. sampler scales linearly row length, long rows (nB = 50 larger) routine.

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: data lives long-format tibble (patient_id, drug, prescribed), pivot matrix calling ame(): matrix ame(..., mode = "bipartite") consumes. Rownames / colnames carry fit$APM (one number per patient) fit$BPM (one number per drug) downstream.
code: library(tidyr); library(tibble) Y_df <- rx %>%                                        # long tibble     pivot_wider(names_from = drug, values_from = prescribed) Y <- as.matrix(Y_df[, -1])                            # numeric matrix rownames(Y) <- Y_df$patient_id                        # row actor names # colnames(Y) are already the drug names from pivot_wider

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: illustrate workflow, simulate bipartite network 30 students 20 courses. student 2-dimensional latent position captures academic preferences, course 2-dimensional latent position captures kind student attracts. interaction matrix GG determines latent dimensions combine predict enrollment.
code: # simulate a student-course enrollment network n_students <- 30 n_courses <- 20  # true latent positions (unobserved in practice) U_true <- matrix(rnorm(n_students * 2, 0, 0.8), n_students, 2) V_true <- matrix(rnorm(n_courses * 2, 0, 0.8), n_courses, 2)  # interaction matrix: dimension 1 has positive affinity, # dimension 2 has negative (students high on dim 2 avoid courses high on dim 2) G_true <- matrix(c(1, 0.5, 0.5, -1), 2, 2)  # generate enrollment probabilities and binary outcomes # negative intercept keeps enrollment rate realistic (~25-30%) eta <- -0.8 + U_true %*% G_true %*% t(V_true) prob <- pnorm(eta) Y_bipartite <- matrix(rbinom(n_students * n_courses, 1, prob),                                             n_students, n_courses)  rownames(Y_bipartite) <- paste0("Student", 1:n_students) colnames(Y_bipartite) <- paste0("Course", 1:n_courses)  cat("Network dimensions:", dim(Y_bipartite), "\n") #> Network dimensions: 30 20 cat("Enrollment rate:", round(mean(Y_bipartite), 2), "\n") #> Enrollment rate: 0.29

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: Fitting bipartite model requires setting mode = "bipartite" specifying latent dimensions node type separately via R_row R_col. summary shows regression coefficient (just intercept , since included covariates) variance components. fitted object also stores student latent positions (U), course latent positions (V), (fixed) interaction matrix (G), additive effects (APM student activity, BPM course popularity), ’ll visualize next.
code: # burn and nscan are small so the vignette builds quickly # for real analyses, use burn >= 1000 and nscan >= 5000. fit_cross <- ame(     Y = Y_bipartite,     mode = "bipartite",     R_row = 2,              # latent dimensions for students     R_col = 2,              # latent dimensions for courses     family = "binary",     burn = 100,     nscan = 500,     odens = 5,     verbose = FALSE )  summary(fit_cross) #>  #> === AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> [1] "Y ~ a[i] + b[j] + U[i,1:2] %*% G %*% V[j,1:2]', family = 'binary'" #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>           Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper    #> intercept   -0.597    0.195  -3.062   0.002   -0.963   -0.221 ** #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: stars are a visual hint from posterior mean / SD only; for inference use the credible intervals. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.309    0.120 #> cab    0.000    0.000 #> vb     0.401    0.135 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #> rho    0.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) #>   Note: bipartite model (rho fixed to 0, cab fixed to 0)

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: uv_plot function layout = "biplot" places row column nodes latent space, making easy see students cluster near courses. Students courses appear close together plot higher predicted probability tie.  interaction matrix GG mediates latent dimensions students courses combine predict ties. ame() (cross-sectional path used fit) GG held fixed rank-truncated scaled identity: rotation scaling interaction absorbed UU VV, GG estimated entries carry information. estimated overall multiplicative term UGV′U G V', captures latent structure network. (lame() GG sampled rather fixed; see next section.) visualise fitted latent structure UGV′U G V' directly:
code: uv_plot(fit_cross, layout = "biplot") +     ggtitle("Bipartite Latent Space: Students and Courses") G_mult <- fit_cross$U %*% fit_cross$G %*% t(fit_cross$V) G_df <- data.frame(     Student = rownames(G_mult)[row(G_mult)],     Course = colnames(G_mult)[col(G_mult)],     Fitted = as.vector(G_mult) ) # rdbu reversed (direction = -1): positive => red, negative => blue, # zero => white. By default scale_fill_distiller() centres white at the # midpoint of the DATA range, not at zero -- so we pass symmetric limits # c(-mx, mx) to force the white midpoint onto zero, which is the right # choice for a signed effect. RdBu is colour-blind-safe per ColorBrewer's # protanopia / deuteranopia checks. mx <- max(abs(G_df$Fitted), na.rm = TRUE) ggplot(G_df, aes(x = Course, y = Student, fill = Fitted)) +     geom_tile() +     scale_fill_distiller(palette = "RdBu", direction = -1, limits = c(-mx, mx)) +     labs(title = "Fitted Multiplicative Structure (UGV')",             subtitle = "Red = positive latent affinity, Blue = negative, near-white = ~0",             x = "Course", y = "Student",             fill = "U G V'") +     theme_bw() +     theme(panel.border = element_blank())

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: bipartite networks observed multiple time periods (say, student enrollments across semesters, country–treaty memberships across decades), lame() lets pool information across time optionally allowing latent structure evolve.

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: simulate panel user–item interaction networks 5 periods latent positions drift via AR(1) process ρ=0.9\rho = 0.9. smooth, mean-reverting evolution: period’s latent position mostly previous one plus small innovation. simulation chunk uses stationary-variance parameterisation (Ut=ρUt−1+1−ρ2ϵU_t = \rho\,U_{t-1} + \sqrt{1 - \rho^2}\,\epsilon ϵ∼N(0,)\epsilon \sim N(0, ), marginal variance UtU_t stays equal 1 every tt). MCMC sampler internally uses innovation-variance parameterisation (Ut=ρUt−1+ϵU_t = \rho\,U_{t-1} + \epsilon ϵ∼N(0,σuv2)\epsilon \sim N(0, \sigma_{uv}^2)); two process σuv2=1−ρ2\sigma_{uv}^2 = 1 - \rho^2, variance lives different objects, don’t mix writing simulations. data-generating interaction matrix GG combines UU VV held constant across periods, fit lame() dynamic_G = FALSE model’s static GG matched truth; show time-varying alternative (dynamic_G = TRUE) .
code:
n_periods <- 5 n_users <- 20 n_items <- 15  true_rho <- 0.9 G_long <- matrix(c(1, 0.3, 0.3, -0.8), 2, 2)  U_t <- vector("list", n_periods) V_t <- vector("list", n_periods) U_t[[1]] <- matrix(rnorm(n_users * 2), n_users, 2) V_t[[1]] <- matrix(rnorm(n_items * 2), n_items, 2) for(t in 2:n_periods) {     U_t[[t]] <- true_rho * U_t[[t-1]] +         sqrt(1 - true_rho^2) * matrix(rnorm(n_users * 2), n_users, 2)     V_t[[t]] <- true_rho * V_t[[t-1]] +         sqrt(1 - true_rho^2) * matrix(rnorm(n_items * 2), n_items, 2) }  Y_list <- list() for(t in 1:n_periods) {     eta_t <- U_t[[t]] %*% G_long %*% t(V_t[[t]])     prob_t <- pnorm(eta_t)     Y_list[[t]] <- matrix(rbinom(n_users * n_items, 1, prob_t),                                                 n_users, n_items)     rownames(Y_list[[t]]) <- paste0("User", 1:n_users)     colnames(Y_list[[t]]) <- paste0("Item", 1:n_items) } names(Y_list) <- paste0("T", 1:n_periods)  cat("Time periods:", length(Y_list), "
") #> Time periods: 5 cat("Dimensions per period:", dim(Y_list[[1]]), "
") #> Dimensions per period: 20 15 cat("Average density:", round(mean(sapply(Y_list, mean)), 2), "
") #> Average density: 0.51 cat("True latent AR(1) coefficient:", true_rho, "
") #> True latent AR(1) coefficient: 0.9

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: simplest longitudinal model assumes latent positions additive effects constant time. pools time periods together get single estimate actor’s latent position, useful believe underlying structure stable just want data estimate precisely.
code: # iterations are small so the vignette builds quickly; use burn >= 1000 # and nscan >= 5000 for real analyses. fit_static <- lame(     Y = Y_list,     mode = "bipartite",     R_row = 2,     R_col = 2,     family = "binary",     dynamic_uv = FALSE,     dynamic_ab = FALSE,     burn = 100,     nscan = 500,     odens = 5,     verbose = FALSE,     plot = FALSE )  summary(fit_static) #>  #> === Longitudinal AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> [1] "Y ~ a[i] + b[j] + U[i,1:2] %*% G %*% V[j,1:2]', family = 'binary'" #>  #> Time periods: 5  #> Family: binary  #> Mode: bipartite  #>  #> Note: STATIC fit pooled across 5 time periods -- #>   U, V, a, b are time-invariant; per-period predictions vary #>   only through per-period covariates. For time-varying effects, #>   refit with dynamic_uv = TRUE and/or dynamic_ab = TRUE. #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>           Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper   #> intercept    0.008    0.236   0.034   0.973   -0.461    0.421   #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: stars are a visual hint from posterior mean / SD only; for inference use the credible intervals. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.254    0.088 #> cab    0.000    0.000 #> vb     0.303    0.128 #> rho    0.000    0.000 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) #>   Note: bipartite model (rho fixed to 0, cab fixed to 0)

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: suspect latent structure changes time (users’ tastes shift, items gain lose popularity), dynamic model lets latent positions additive effects drift via AR(1) processes. high autoregressive parameter (ρ\rho close 1) means slow, gradual change; low one means structure volatile period period. dynamic model reports estimated AR(1) persistence parameters latent positions (rho_uv) additive effects (rho_ab). Values close 1 indicate strong period--period persistence; values close 0 indicate near-independent latent positions across periods. posterior recovers true rho_uv = 0.9 within 95% credible interval, indicating lame() correctly identifies latent positions drift smoothly rather refreshing period.
code: # same reduced iterations as above for vignette speed. fit_dynamic <- lame(     Y = Y_list,     mode = "bipartite",     R_row = 2,     R_col = 2,     family = "binary",     dynamic_uv = TRUE,     dynamic_ab = TRUE,     burn = 100,     nscan = 500,     odens = 5,     verbose = FALSE,     plot = FALSE )  summary(fit_dynamic) #>  #> === Longitudinal AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> [1] "Y ~ a[i] + b[j] + U[i,1:2] %*% G %*% V[j,1:2]', family = 'binary'" #>  #> Time periods: 5  #> Family: binary  #> Mode: bipartite  #> Dynamic latent positions: enabled (rho_uv = 0.905 ) #> Dynamic additive effects: enabled (rho_ab = 0.402 ) #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>           Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper   #> intercept    0.005    0.036   0.139   0.889   -0.065    0.078   #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: stars are a visual hint from posterior mean / SD only; for inference use the credible intervals. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.289    0.089 #> cab    0.000    0.000 #> vb     0.324    0.158 #> rho    0.000    0.000 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) #>   Note: bipartite model (rho fixed to 0, cab fixed to 0) cat(sprintf("True rho_uv = %.2f | posterior mean = %.3f | 95%% CI = [%.3f, %.3f]\n",                         true_rho,                         mean(fit_dynamic$rho_uv),                         quantile(fit_dynamic$rho_uv, 0.025),                         quantile(fit_dynamic$rho_uv, 0.975))) #> True rho_uv = 0.90 | posterior mean = 0.905 | 95% CI = [0.865, 0.947]

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: suspect way row column latent spaces interact shifting time, just actor positions, set dynamic_G = TRUE. default, lame() runs Carter-Kohn FFBS vec(G_t) AR(1) state-space prior (persistence innovation variance sampled jointly inside loop) attaches three outputs: fit$G_cube – final draw’s per-period GtG_t (Rrow×Rcol×TR_\text{row} \times R_\text{col} \times T). fit$G_cube_post_mean – posterior-mean GtG_t averaged across stored draws, shape; recommended summary. fit$G_cube_post_sd – elementwise posterior SD across draws, shape. hyperparameter chains live fit$RHO_G fit$SIGMA_G2. rotation-drift diagnostic (fit$G_rotation_drift) compares variance raw GtG_t entries SVD-canonicalised counterparts; ratio large apparent time variation dominated latent-rotation drift rather real change interaction matrix. method = "als", dynamic_G = TRUE estimates per-period bilinear surface penalized point path normal, binary, Poisson panels. Named changing-composition panels aligned union row column actor sets fitting. object still stores G_cube, G_cube_post_mean, G_cube_post_sd compatibility MCMC code; G_cube_post_mean point path G_cube_post_sd zero ALS posterior draws. smoothing settings reported rho_G, RHO_G, SIGMA_G2, lambda_G_als. Frobenius norms across periods nearly identical (static-G fit GOF comparable), real evidence time-varying interaction structure stick dynamic_G = FALSE. drift substantially static-G fit shows worse GOF coverage, time-varying GtG_t meaningful work. rotation-drift diagnostic second sanity check: ratio ~5, apparent variation across GtG_t slices mostly U/V rotation rather real temporal change, canonical reporting G_cube_post_mean honest summary.
code:
fit_dynG <- lame(     Y = Y_list,     mode = "bipartite",     R_row = 2,     R_col = 2,     family = "binary",     dynamic_uv = TRUE,     dynamic_ab = TRUE,     dynamic_G = TRUE,     burn = 100,     nscan = 500,     odens = 5,     verbose = FALSE,     plot = FALSE )  # fit$G_cube_post_mean has dimensions R_row x R_col x n_periods if (!is.null(fit_dynG$G_cube_post_mean)) {     cat("G_cube_post_mean dimensions:", dim(fit_dynG$G_cube_post_mean), "
")     cat("Frobenius norm of posterior-mean G_t per period:
")     print(round(apply(fit_dynG$G_cube_post_mean, 3,                       function(g) sqrt(sum(g^2))), 3))     cat("Rotation-drift ratio (>= 5 flags latent-rotation domination):",         round(fit_dynG$G_rotation_drift$ratio, 2), "
") } else {     cat("G_cube was not attached (no bipartite + RA>0 + RB>0 case).
") } #> G_cube_post_mean dimensions: 2 2 5  #> Frobenius norm of posterior-mean G_t per period: #> [1] 8.645 8.696 8.498 8.185 8.057 #> Rotation-drift ratio (>= 5 flags latent-rotation domination): 1.08

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: dynamic effects, uv_plot can show trajectories, illustrating actor’s latent position moves space time. line traces one actor’s path first last period, revealing actors’ roles stable shifting.  Convergence diagnostics especially important dynamic models, since additional parameters (ρ\rho, innovation variances) need adequate samples well-estimated.
code: uv_plot(fit_dynamic, plot_type = "trajectory") +     ggtitle("Dynamic Latent Positions: Trajectories Over Time") trace_plot(fit_dynamic)

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: natural question whether dynamic model worth added complexity. goodness--fit plots provide one way compare: dynamic model’s posterior-predictive distributions better cover observed statistics, extra flexibility paying . panel observed bipartite statistic period appears solid Okabe-Ito orange (#D55E00) line filled points, posterior-predictive median dashed dark grey line, grey ribbon 95% posterior-predictive credible interval; three series dual-encoded colour linetype comparison stays legible greyscale colour-blind readers. bipartite fits three panels labelled “Sender Degree Heterogeneity” (row-mean SD), “Receiver Degree Heterogeneity” (column-mean SD), “Four Cycles”, bipartite analogue transitivity.   can also compare GOF statistics directly. bipartite networks, key statistics standard deviation row means (much users vary activity), standard deviation column means (much items vary popularity), four-cycle count (measure clustering bipartite networks, analogous transitivity unipartite networks).  simulated interaction matrix GG constant, actor positions drift via AR(1) ρ=0.9\rho = 0.9 – moderate persistence (lag-4 autocorrelation 0.94≈0.660.9^4 \approx 0.66, positions first last period correlate 0.6-0.8 move). genuinely fixed GG, positions; combined short panel short chains, static dynamic models fit similarly rather dynamic one clearly winning. boxplots overlap substantially cover dashed line, dynamic model’s extra flexibility paying . real applications user preferences item popularity genuinely shift time, dynamic model show tighter coverage observed statistics.
code: # gof plots for each model gof_plot(fit_static) gof_plot(fit_dynamic) gof_static <- fit_static$GOF gof_dynamic <- fit_dynamic$GOF  # extract posterior predictive samples (exclude column 1, which is observed) # each element is a matrix [n_time x n_mcmc], so colMeans averages across time extract_ppc <- function(gof, stat) {     mat <- gof[[stat]]     colMeans(mat[, -1, drop = FALSE]) }  # observed values (column 1, averaged across time periods) obs_vals <- c(     mean(gof_static$sd.rowmean[, 1]),     mean(gof_static$sd.colmean[, 1]),     mean(gof_static$four.cycles[, 1]) )  gof_df <- data.frame(     value = c(         extract_ppc(gof_static, "sd.rowmean"),         extract_ppc(gof_dynamic, "sd.rowmean"),         extract_ppc(gof_static, "sd.colmean"),         extract_ppc(gof_dynamic, "sd.colmean"),         extract_ppc(gof_static, "four.cycles"),         extract_ppc(gof_dynamic, "four.cycles")     ),     model = rep(rep(c("Static", "Dynamic"),                             each = ncol(gof_static$sd.rowmean) - 1), 3),     statistic = rep(c("Row Mean SD", "Column Mean SD", "Four-Cycles"),                                     each = 2 * (ncol(gof_static$sd.rowmean) - 1)) )  obs_df <- data.frame(     statistic = c("Row Mean SD", "Column Mean SD", "Four-Cycles"),     observed = obs_vals )  ggplot(gof_df, aes(x = model, y = value)) +     geom_boxplot() +     geom_hline(data = obs_df, aes(yintercept = observed),                         linetype = 2) +     facet_wrap(~statistic, scales = "free_y") +     labs(title = "GOF Comparison: Static vs Dynamic",             subtitle = "Dashed Line = Observed Value",             x = "Model", y = "Simulated Statistic") +     theme_bw() +     theme(         panel.border = element_blank(),         strip.background = element_rect(fill = "black", color = "black"),         strip.text = element_text(color = "white", hjust = 0)     )

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: number latent dimensions (R_row, R_col) controls rich multiplicative structure . dimensions model can’t capture latent clustering; many risk overfitting (slower computation). practical approach fit models different dimensions compare GOF statistics. one-sided posterior predictive tail probabilities, formal test p-values. Values near 0.5 indicate observed statistic lies bulk model’s posterior predictive distribution; values near 0 1 indicate model systematically - -predicts feature. (Note ame(), fit$GOF stores observed statistic row 1 alongside posterior-predictive draws, tail probability includes observed point — one-row bias negligible chain lengths use, worth knowing.) relationship R monotonic; adding latent dimensions can move tail probabilities away 0.5 (e.g. making predictive distribution dispersed), use together trajectory GOF plots rather single number optimise. sensible heuristic pick smallest R three tail probabilities sit comfortably away 0 1. particular simulated network three tail probabilities stay high (near 1) every R – fit -predicts row-degree heterogeneity four-cycles (simulated networks carry observed network) regardless rank – useful verdict: value R rescues statistics, limitation structural rather matter adding latent dimensions. exactly kind read diagnostic .
code: # include R=0 as a no-latent-space baseline dims_to_test <- list(     c(0, 0),     c(1, 1),     c(2, 2) )  # compute observed GOF statistics from the data obs_gof <- gof_stats(Y_bipartite, mode = "bipartite")  gof_results <- list() for(i in seq_along(dims_to_test)) {     fit_temp <- ame(         Y = Y_bipartite,         mode = "bipartite",         R_row = dims_to_test[[i]][1],         R_col = dims_to_test[[i]][2],         family = "binary",         burn = 200,         nscan = 2500,         odens = 5,         verbose = FALSE     )     # posterior predictive tail probability: proportion of simulated     # statistics at or above the observed value (one-sided)     gof_results[[i]] <- c(         R_row = dims_to_test[[i]][1],         R_col = dims_to_test[[i]][2],         pval_rowmean = mean(fit_temp$GOF[, "sd.rowmean"] >= obs_gof["sd.rowmean"]),         pval_colmean = mean(fit_temp$GOF[, "sd.colmean"] >= obs_gof["sd.colmean"]),         pval_fourcycles = mean(fit_temp$GOF[, "four.cycles"] >= obs_gof["four.cycles"])     ) }  do.call(rbind, gof_results) #>      R_row R_col pval_rowmean pval_colmean pval_fourcycles #> [1,]     0     0     1.000000    0.9900200       0.9920160 #> [2,]     1     1     0.998004    0.9221557       0.9820359 #> [3,]     2     2     0.988024    0.8742515       0.9700599

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: use bipartite models. Use bipartite mode whenever network two fundamentally different types nodes ties form types. adjacency matrix rectangular, almost certainly need . Even happens square (e.g., 20 students 20 courses), set mode = "bipartite" row column nodes represent different kinds entities. Interpreting output. additive effects (APM, BPM) direct interpretation: tell row nodes unusually active column nodes unusually popular, controlling covariates latent structure. latent positions (U, V) interaction matrix (G) together capture residual patterns association, answering question “beyond covariates overall activity levels, row nodes tend connect column nodes?”

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: Bayesian model, always check convergence interpreting results. trace_plot function shows MCMC traces posterior densities regression coefficients variance components.  Look traces mix well (long flat stretches slow drifts) densities smooth unimodal. short chains used vignette, convergence guaranteed. practice, run longer chains consider multiple starting values. numerical diagnostics use Stan-era stack cross-sectional vignette demonstrates. posterior::as_draws(fit_cross) returns draws_array keyed chain, posterior::summarise_draws() reports split-R̂\hat R, ess_bulk, ess_tail per coefficient variance component. honest -chain R̂\hat R run ame_parallel(..., n_chains = 4, fitter = "ame", combine_method = "pool") pooled fit carries chain_indicator column; summarise_draws() computes Rhat across chains. prior_summary(fit_cross) prints hyperparameters actually effect (g-prior β\beta, variance-component priors), loo::loo(fit_cross) works refitting save_log_lik = TRUE (Gaussian density normal, probit Bernoulli binary, log-Poisson poisson; pair loo::loo_compare() model selection). See cross-sectional vignette worked end--end stack.
code: trace_plot(fit_cross)

title: Bipartite Network Analysis
text: need latent positions tidy format (e.g., merging external data building custom plots), latent_positions() function returns data frame one row per actor-dimension-time combination: especially useful bipartite networks U (row nodes) V (column nodes) different numbers actors.
code: lp <- latent_positions(fit_cross) #> ℹ `posterior_sd` is "NA" because U/V samples were not saved. #> ℹ To get posterior SDs, refit with `posterior_opts = posterior_options(save_UV #>   = TRUE)`. #> This message is displayed once per session. head(lp) #>      actor dimension time       value posterior_sd type #> 1 Student1         1    1 -2.12110916           NA    U #> 2 Student2         1    1  0.49495598           NA    U #> 3 Student3         1    1  1.74106967           NA    U #> 4 Student4         1    1 -1.90187944           NA    U #> 5 Student5         1    1 -0.03972852           NA    U #> 6 Student6         1    1  4.32579340           NA    U  # filter to just the row nodes (students) lp_students <- lp[lp$type == "U", ] cat("Student positions:", nrow(lp_students), "rows\n") #> Student positions: 60 rows

title: Your First AME Model
text: Imagine ’re studying friendships high school. data nominated friend, plus information student (gender, race, grade). natural first instinct run logistic regression: sharing gender predict friendship? problem friendships aren’t independent observations. students just social others (nominate lots friends regardless ). students popular (receive lots nominations). friendships tend reciprocated: Alice names Bob, Bob likely name Alice. standard regression ignores , standard errors wrong. Additive Multiplicative Effects (AME) model handles dependencies directly. gives actor sender effect (aia_i, social ), receiver effect (bjb_j, popular ), position latent space (uiu_i, vjv_j) captures tends connect beyond covariates explain. name reflects two kinds actor-level terms: sender receiver effects enter model additively (ai+bja_i + b_j), latent positions enter multiplicatively (ui′vju_i'v_j, dot product). Think regression takes network structure seriously.

title: Your First AME Model
text: ’ll analyze friendship network Add Health study, longitudinal study adolescents United States. addhealthc3 dataset lame contains directed friendship nomination network along student characteristics. Notice na.rm = TRUE calls: network data often missing entries (diagonal NA self-ties undefined, dyads may unobserved). model handles missing values internally via data augmentation, NAs can stay matrix. applies missing covariates: NAs node attributes (race grade handful) propagate dyads built , ame() treats dyads unobserved reports many data-augmented. modeling, let’s look basic structure. much students vary number friends?  ’s real variation : students much social others, much popular. exactly sender receiver random effects capture.
code:
library(lame) library(ggplot2) set.seed(6886)  # load the Add Health friendship network data(addhealthc3)  # convert valued network to binary (any nomination = friendship) Y <- (addhealthc3$Y > 0) * 1 X_nodes <- addhealthc3$X  n <- nrow(Y) cat("Students:", n, "
") #> Students: 32 cat("Friendships:", sum(Y, na.rm = TRUE), "
") #> Friendships: 127 cat("Network density:", round(mean(Y, na.rm = TRUE), 3), "
") #> Network density: 0.128 out_degree <- rowSums(Y, na.rm = TRUE) in_degree <- colSums(Y, na.rm = TRUE)  degree_df <- data.frame(     Degree = c(out_degree, in_degree),     Type = rep(c("Nominations sent", "Nominations received"), each = n) )  ggplot(degree_df, aes(x = Degree)) +     geom_histogram(binwidth = 1) +     facet_wrap(~Type, ncol = 2) +     labs(x = "Number of Ties", y = "Count") +     theme_bw() +     theme(         panel.border = element_blank(),         strip.background = element_rect(fill = "black", color = "black"),         strip.text = element_text(color = "white", hjust = 0)     )

title: Your First AME Model
text: classic question friendship networks homophily: birds feather flock together? ’ll test whether students likely friends others gender, race, grade. deliberately include same_grade (binary: grade ) grade_diff (continuous: many grades apart) illustrate common modeling pitfall: collinearity. two covariates measure nearly thing, model cleanly separate effects. ’ll see consequences results .
code: # `outer(x, x, FUN)` builds an n x n matrix whose (i,j) entry is FUN(x[i], x[j]). # So `outer(female, female, "==")` returns TRUE where students i and j share the # same gender. Multiplying by 1 converts TRUE/FALSE to 1/0. # # homophily indicators: 1 if same, 0 if different same_female <- outer(X_nodes[,"female"], X_nodes[,"female"], "==") * 1 same_race <- outer(X_nodes[,"race"], X_nodes[,"race"], "==") * 1 same_grade <- outer(X_nodes[,"grade"], X_nodes[,"grade"], "==") * 1 # absolute grade difference; same shape, but a continuous covariate grade_diff <- abs(outer(X_nodes[,"grade"], X_nodes[,"grade"], "-"))  # pack into a 3D array (n x n x p) Xdyad <- array(NA, dim = c(n, n, 4)) Xdyad[,,1] <- same_female Xdyad[,,2] <- same_race Xdyad[,,3] <- same_grade Xdyad[,,4] <- grade_diff dimnames(Xdyad)[[3]] <- c('same_female', 'same_race', 'same_grade', 'grade_diff') for(k in 1:4) diag(Xdyad[,,k]) <- NA  # nodal covariates (sender and receiver characteristics) Xrow <- X_nodes[, c("female", "grade")] Xcol <- X_nodes[, c("female", "grade")]

title: Your First AME Model
text: Now fun part. fit binary probit AME model : Covariates homophily effects (similar students become friends?) Sender receiver random effects (students social/popular) Dyadic correlation (reciprocity: friendships tend mutual) 2-dimensional latent space (residual clustering beyond covariates explain)

what: A note for ERGM users: what AME assumes
title: Your First AME Model
text: ’re coming ergm / statnet, important conceptual difference dependence assumption. ERGM specifies joint distribution entire adjacency matrix uses change statistics (e.g. gwesp, triangle, kstar) encode unconditional higher-order dependence: changing one tie shifts probability ties terms directly. AME . AME assumes conditional dyadic independence: given additive effects (ai,bj)(a_i, b_j), latent positions (ui,vj)(u_i, v_j), dyadic correlation ρ\rho (yij,yji)(y_{ij}, y_{ji}), covariates, dyads independent. Higher-order structure (clustering, transitivity, degree heterogeneity) captured indirectly integrating latent random effects; actors similar uiu_i tend form ties overlapping neighborhoods, induces clustering partial transitivity marginal level. practical consequences ERGM user: degeneracy. likelihood factors dyads conditional random effects, AME exhibit model-degeneracy failure modes ERGMs triangle unconstrained kstar terms. change-statistic interpretation β\beta. coefficient same_grade partial association probit-latent scale, log-odds change conditional rest network. triangle structure missed. transitive closure pressure substantive target (gwesp leading example), AME typically -predicts triangles GOF ERGM right tool. target structural pattern + actor heterogeneity + covariate effects calibrated uncertainty, AME generally stable estimator.
code: fit <- ame(Y,                     Xdyad = Xdyad,                     Xrow = Xrow,                     Xcol = Xcol,                     R = 2,              # 2D latent space                     family = "binary",  # probit model for 0/1 data                     rvar = TRUE,        # sender random effects                     cvar = TRUE,        # receiver random effects                     dcor = TRUE,        # dyadic correlation (reciprocity)                     burn = 500,         # burn-in (lengthen for a final run)                     nscan = 2000,       # post-burn-in iterations                     odens = 25,         # thinning                     verbose = FALSE,                     gof = TRUE)

title: Your First AME Model
text: Let’s unpack key results: Homophily effects. Look regression coefficients credible intervals. grade_diff coefficient strongly negative credible interval excludes zero, telling us students apart grade much less likely friends. same_female coefficient positive (gender homophily) 95% credible interval includes zero, well-identified sample size – read sign suggestive, effect established. same_race coefficient captures race homophily controlling grade gender. collinearity pitfall. same_grade grade_diff measure nearly thing – grade difference 0 -grade event – model cleanly split grade signal . still identifies dominant effect: grade_diff strongly negative stable run run (-0.59, never near zero). suffers partition: same_grade left absorb small, noisy residual bump top linear grade-distance trend. lesson interpretation, estimate instability: include one , , grade effect lands single, clean coefficient. Variance components. sender variance (va) receiver variance (vb) quantify much students differ sociality popularity. Larger values mean heterogeneity. dyadic correlation (rho) captures reciprocity: values near 1 mean nominates B, B almost always nominates back. network, reciprocity strong. binary networks probit link, coefficients latent scale. marginal effect covariate response probability ϕ(x′β)⋅βk\phi(x'\beta) \cdot \beta_k, ϕ\phi standard normal density. density maximized ϕ(0)≈0.399\phi(0) \approx 0.399, source textbook “0.4×β0.4 \times \beta” rule thumb probit (see e.g. Wooldridge 2010, Econometric Analysis Cross Section Panel Data, ch. 15, Greene 2018, Econometric Analysis, ch. 17). approximation sharp baseline predicted probability near 0.5. Add Health friendship network density 0.128, well 0.5, ϕ(Φ−1(0.128))≈0.21\phi(\Phi^{-1}(0.128)) \approx 0.21 – marginal effect unit change covariate closer 0.21×β0.21 \times \beta 0.4×β0.4 \times \beta midpoint rule (fall , 0.18×β0.18 \times \beta, sparser p≈0.1p \approx 0.1 network). Two wrinkles specific AME: () random effects ai,bj,ui′vja_i, b_j, u_i'v_j contribute extra variance latent linear predictor, marginal effect integrating smaller factor 1/1+VRE1/\sqrt{1 + V_\text{RE}}; (ii) right number report depends whether want effect sample mean, average effect across observed dyads, effect specific covariate values. interpretation, compute quantity want predict(fit, type = "response") contrast predicted probabilities counterfactual covariate settings.
code: summary(fit) #>  #> === AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> [1] "Y ~ intercept + dyad(same_female, same_race, same_grade, grade_diff) + row(female, grade) + col(female, grade) + a[i] + b[j] + rho*e[ji] + U[i,1:2] %*% V[j,1:2], family = 'binary'" #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>                  Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper     #> intercept          -3.485    1.097  -3.177   0.001   -5.894   -1.611  ** #> female_row         -0.524    0.303  -1.727   0.084   -1.128   -0.002   . #> grade_row           0.065    0.085   0.763   0.445   -0.062    0.248     #> female_col         -0.386    0.277  -1.393   0.164   -0.898    0.081     #> grade_col           0.275    0.059   4.624       0    0.142    0.364 *** #> same_female_dyad    0.187    0.183    1.02   0.308   -0.163    0.519     #> same_race_dyad     -0.085    0.264  -0.321   0.748     -0.5    0.409     #> same_grade_dyad     0.169    0.261   0.645   0.519   -0.338     0.65     #> grade_diff_dyad    -0.602    0.101   -5.96       0   -0.783   -0.409 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: stars are a visual hint from posterior mean / SD only; for inference use the credible intervals. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.404    0.135 #> cab    0.032    0.091 #> vb     0.236    0.084 #> rho    0.829    0.085 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance)

title: Your First AME Model
text: Since model estimated via MCMC (Markov chain Monte Carlo), trace plots first check sampler explored posterior distribution thoroughly.  look : trace plots (top) fluctuate around stable mean. see long trends, chain converged. density plots (bottom) smooth unimodal. 80 post-burn-samples coefficients mix well; slow ones seed grade_diff, whose effective sample size drops single digits, – typically worse two across runs – rho, reciprocity parameter chronically slowest-mixing term fits. Read mixing problem, estimate problem: grade_diff’s point estimate stable run run even ESS low, near-twin same_grade mixes fine, poor mixing comes short run seed rather collinearity breakdown. grade_diff rho call longer multi-chain run quoting uncertainty.
code: trace_plot(fit, params = "beta", ncol = 3)

title: Your First AME Model
text: trace plot sanity check, diagnostic. Stan-era summary triple split-R̂\hat R, bulk ESS, tail ESS, posterior package computes three draws_array. lame registers as_draws() method reshapes BETA VC fit, workflow run stanfit works : conventional thresholds: split-R̂\hat R < 1.01 every monitored parameter; ess_bulk ess_tail ≥\ge 400 per chain (≥\ge 1600 four chains). demo stores 80 draws (one short chain), reach ESS target – needs longer, multi-chain run – R̂\hat R column still informative. coefficients clear R̂\hat R < 1.01 bar; two : grade_diff_dyad (R̂≈1.12\hat R \approx 1.12, ess_bulk single digits seed – point estimate stable regardless), rho (R̂≈1.08\hat R \approx 1.08), reciprocity parameter chronically slow-mixing term across seeds (typical run , grade_diff, worst-mixing parameter – fit’s seed simply pushes grade_diff bottom). single-chain fit like one reports within-chain split-R̂\hat R, informative within-chain non-stationarity -chain disagreement; four-chain fit gives cross-chain check.
code: library(posterior) #> This is posterior version 1.7.0 #>  #> Attaching package: 'posterior' #> The following object is masked from 'package:lame': #>  #>     as_draws #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     mad, sd, var #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     %in%, match draws <- posterior::as_draws(fit)              # draws_array [iter, chain, var] posterior::summarise_draws(draws)              # mean, sd, q5, q95, rhat, #> # A tibble: 14 × 10 #>    variable           mean  median     sd    mad      q5     q95   rhat ess_bulk #>    <chr>             <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl>    <dbl> #>  1 intercept       -3.49   -3.38   1.10   0.940  -5.30   -1.72    0.991    77.1  #>  2 female_row      -0.524  -0.527  0.303  0.261  -1.03   -0.0552  1.000    66.0  #>  3 grade_row        0.0645  0.0591 0.0846 0.0858 -0.0521  0.231   0.991    66.7  #>  4 female_col      -0.386  -0.367  0.277  0.266  -0.850   0.0281  0.989    73.8  #>  5 grade_col        0.275   0.278  0.0594 0.0541  0.186   0.359   1.00     57.5  #>  6 same_female_dy…  0.187   0.180  0.183  0.178  -0.143   0.502   1.01     67.4  #>  7 same_race_dyad  -0.0849 -0.0921 0.264  0.272  -0.466   0.334   1.03     80.2  #>  8 same_grade_dyad  0.169   0.195  0.261  0.276  -0.258   0.619   1.03     94.7  #>  9 grade_diff_dyad -0.602  -0.592  0.101  0.108  -0.769  -0.463   1.12      7.63 #> 10 va               0.404   0.380  0.135  0.136   0.223   0.654   0.994    81.3  #> 11 cab              0.0315  0.0215 0.0910 0.0867 -0.108   0.179   0.988    70.4  #> 12 vb               0.236   0.226  0.0838 0.0785  0.128   0.394   1.00     56.0  #> 13 rho              0.829   0.860  0.0849 0.0719  0.626   0.925   1.07      9.71 #> 14 ve               1       1      0      0       1       1      NA        NA    #> # ℹ 1 more variable: ess_tail <dbl>                                                # ess_bulk, ess_tail per param

title: Your First AME Model
text: real convergence assessment, run four chains different seeds let summarise_draws() compute Rhat across chains: pooled object carries chain_indicator as_draws() keeps chain identity separate, split-R̂\hat R needs. reshape feeds bayesplot::mcmc_trace() tidybayes::tidy_draws() directly.
code: fit_mc <- ame_parallel(     Y, Xdyad = Xdyad, Xrow = Xrow, Xcol = Xcol,     family = "binary", R = 2,     burn = 500, nscan = 2000, odens = 25,     n_chains = 4, cores = 1,            # cores = 1 keeps the chunk portable     combine_method = "pool",            # pooled fit; chain_indicator is preserved     verbose = FALSE ) posterior::summarise_draws(posterior::as_draws(fit_mc))

what: Sampler-failure diagnostics (the Gibbs analog of HMC divergences)
title: Your First AME Model
text: lame Gibbs / Metropolis-Hastings sampler, HMC, notion HMC divergence. closest analog per-block Metropolis acceptance / tryError count, exposed every fit fit$mh_counters (alias fit$tryErrorChecks). cross-sectional ame() fit counts inspect (zero); longitudinal lame() path additionally fires automatic warning block exceeds 5% failure rate. routine reporting: Treat block >5% failures suspicious; pair ess_bulk / ess_tail summarise_draws() mh_counters Gibbs-side equivalent Stan triple (R̂\hat R, ESS, divergences).
code: str(fit$mh_counters) #> List of 5 #>  $ beta: int 0 #>  $ Sab : int 0 #>  $ s2  : int 0 #>  $ rho : int 0 #>  $ UV  : int 0

what: Inspecting the priors actually in effect: prior_summary()
title: Your First AME Model
text: Modelled rstanarm::prior_summary(), prior_summary(fit) prints hyperparameters actually used (defaults filled ) g-prior β\beta variance components. Always run per fit; cheapest way catch typo prior = list(...) override.
code: prior_summary(fit) #>  #> ── Priors in effect (ame fit) ── #>  #> Regression coefficients: `beta ~ N(0, g * sigma^2 * (X'X)^-1)` (g-prior). #> `g` (top-level argument) = 992 #> Note: `g` is set at top level on `ame()` -- not inside `prior = list(...)`. #> `Sab0` = "matrix(0.2032, 0, 0, 0.2032)" #> `eta0` = "8" #> `Suv0` = "matrix(0.2032, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0.2032, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0.2032, 0, 0, 0, 0, #> 0.2032)" #> `kappa0` = "13"

title: Your First AME Model
text: refit save_log_lik = TRUE, fit carries [n_stored, n_obs] pointwise log-likelihood matrix loo::loo(fit) works directly via registered S3 method: six families normal, binary, cbin, tobit, poisson, ordinal stored log-likelihood exact family-specific Y density (Gaussian / probit Bernoulli / censored-Gaussian / log-Poisson / cumulative-probit), elpd_loo directly comparable loo() brms rstanarm fit family. two rank likelihoods frn rrl, exact marginal requires GHK Monte Carlo available longitudinal path via log_lik_method = "observed_ghk"; cross-sectional default falls back augmented-Z normal density emits one-time warning. Inspect fit$log_lik_method see branch used.
code: fit_ll <- ame(Y, Xdyad = Xdyad, Xrow = Xrow, Xcol = Xcol,               family = "binary", R = 2,               burn = 500, nscan = 2000, odens = 25,               save_log_lik = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)  # alternative model: drop the dyadic covariate fit_ll_null <- ame(Y, Xrow = Xrow, Xcol = Xcol,                    family = "binary", R = 2,                    burn = 500, nscan = 2000, odens = 25,                    save_log_lik = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)  loo::loo_compare(loo::loo(fit_ll), loo::loo(fit_ll_null))

what: Does the Model Fit the Data?
title: Your First AME Model
text: Goodness--fit (GOF) checks whether model can reproduce key structural features observed network. goes beyond dyad-level prediction: tests whether model captures emergent properties like degree heterogeneity, reciprocity patterns, clustering.  panel shows different network statistic. grey histogram posterior-predictive distribution networks simulated fitted model; dashed orange vertical line observed value scale. figure encodes observed-vs-predicted contrast two channels : colour (Okabe-Ito orange #D55E00 vs grey60) linetype (dashed vertical segment vs filled histogram bar), cue survives colour-blind viewers grayscale printing. legend strip top panel labels two glyphs explicitly “Observed” “Posterior predictive”. panel titles human-readable labels (“Sender Degree Heterogeneity”, “Receiver Degree Heterogeneity”, “Dyadic Dependence”, “Triadic Dependence”, “Transitivity”). user-facing aliases accepted gof_plot(..., statistics = ...) sd.row, sd.col, dyad.dep, triad.dep (cyclic-triad measure), trans.dep (transitive-triad analogue). actual column names stored fit$GOF differ slightly: row/column SDs sd.rowmean / sd.colmean cyclic-triad statistic cycle.dep, colnames(fit$GOF) returns sd.rowmean, sd.colmean, dyad.dep, cycle.dep, trans.dep. alias map applied inside gof_plot() rewrites sd.row → sd.rowmean, sd.col → sd.colmean, triad.dep → cycle.dep plotting. plot observed values Sender Receiver Degree Heterogeneity fall inside posterior-predictive histograms (although Receiver sits right mode), Dyadic Dependence (Y-vs-t(Y) correlation, essentially empirical reciprocity) also covered. three features explicitly modeled sender/receiver random effects, row/column variance components, dyadic correlation ρ\rho. two triad-level statistics tell different story: observed Triadic Dependence sits upper tail simulated distribution, observed Transitivity sits far right edge. AME -predicts triangle closure network, expected signature conditional dyadic independence. latent space soaks triangle structure clustering uiu_i vectors, absence explicit triangle-closure term means match observed transitivity. transitivity substantive target, fit ERGM; diagnostic concern , increasing R sometimes helps modestly. can also compute GOF fact using gof() function, useful want test custom statistics. built-trans.dep panel correlation-style clustering measure; ERGM users typically want raw counts match triangle / ttriple / gwesp family. one-liners custom stats: Read table way read ergm::gof(). statistic pp_p_right near 0 means simulated networks rarely hit observed value , .e. model -predicts quantity; value near 1 means -predicts. Add Health raw triangles count comes back reasonably well covered (pp_p_right around 0.25 run): AME fit absorbs much triangle pressure clustering uiu_i joint scale additive effects, raw count predictive distribution even though model gwesp-style change statistic likelihood. two_path driven marginal degree distribution additive effects, also usually well covered (pp_p_right around 0.4 ). structural gap shows ratio: trans_ratio (triangles / two-paths) unweighted clustering coefficient ERGM users recognise sna::gtrans, network comes back pp_p_right close zero (around 0.05 run). scalar summary targeted gwesp(decay = 0, fixed = TRUE): model gets absolute scale triangle counts roughly right concentrate triangles per two-path way data . deg_cor degree assortativity: AME captures sender-receiver covariance (cab), rest reflects higher-order structure outside dyadic likelihood.
code: gof_plot(fit) # ERGM-style custom statistics: #   triangles  : count of transitive triples i -> j, j -> k, i -> k, #                the directed analogue of ergm's `ttriple` change statistic #                (and what `gwesp(decay = 0, fixed = TRUE)` reduces to) #   two_path   : count of two-paths i -> j -> k (the "potential" triangles) #   trans_ratio: triangles / two_path -- the classic clustering coefficient #                (sna::gtrans), the right scalar summary of what gwesp targets #   deg_cor    : Pearson correlation of out-degree and in-degree custom_stats <- function(Y) {     Yb <- Y; Yb[is.na(Yb)] <- 0           # NA -> 0 for matrix multiply     YY <- Yb %*% Yb     triangles   <- sum(YY * Yb)            # transitive triples     two_path    <- sum(YY) - sum(diag(YY)) # two-paths excluding length-2 reciprocal loops     out_deg <- rowSums(Y, na.rm = TRUE)     in_deg  <- colSums(Y, na.rm = TRUE)     c(triangles   = triangles,       two_path    = two_path,       trans_ratio = triangles / max(two_path, 1),       deg_cor     = cor(out_deg, in_deg)) }  gof_custom <- gof(fit, custom_gof = custom_stats, nsim = 50, verbose = FALSE) obs <- gof_custom[1, ] sim <- gof_custom[-1, , drop = FALSE]  # posterior-predictive p-value: fraction of simulated networks at least as # extreme (right tail) as the observed; values near 0 mean the model # under-predicts the statistic, values near 1 mean it over-predicts pp_right <- function(o, s) mean(s >= o, na.rm = TRUE) data.frame(     stat       = colnames(gof_custom),     observed   = round(obs, 3),     sim_mean   = round(colMeans(sim, na.rm = TRUE), 3),     pp_p_right = round(mapply(pp_right, obs, as.data.frame(sim)), 3) )[c("triangles", "two_path", "trans_ratio", "deg_cor"), ] #>                    stat observed sim_mean pp_p_right #> triangles     triangles  239.000  183.060       0.24 #> two_path       two_path  493.000  472.940       0.42 #> trans_ratio trans_ratio    0.485    0.370       0.06 #> deg_cor         deg_cor    0.564    0.414       0.32

title: Your First AME Model
text: multiplicative effects (ui′vju_i'v_j) place student 2D latent space. Students near sender space (triangles) tend nominate similar friends; students near receiver space (circles) tend nominated similar students. captures clustering covariates alone can’t explain.  Students opposite sides plot dissimilar friendship patterns. Clusters students placed near likely share unobserved characteristic (social group, extracurricular activities) drives friendship choices beyond gender, race, grade.
code: uv_plot(fit, layout = "circle", show.edges = FALSE,                 label.nodes = TRUE, label.size = 2.5) +     theme_bw() +     theme(         panel.border = element_blank(),         axis.text = element_blank(),         axis.title = element_blank(),         panel.grid = element_blank()     )

what: Who Are the Most Social / Popular Students?
title: Your First AME Model
text: additive effects decompose individual heterogeneity sender effects (sociality) receiver effects (popularity):  Students large positive sender effects nominate friends expected given covariates; large positive receiver effects receive nominations.
code: p1 <- ab_plot(fit, effect = "sender", sorted = TRUE,                             title = "Sender Effects (Sociality)") p2 <- ab_plot(fit, effect = "receiver", sorted = TRUE,                             title = "Receiver Effects (Popularity)")  library(patchwork) p1 / p2

title: Your First AME Model
text: model gives predicted probabilities every possible tie, including unobserved ones. useful link prediction (likely become friends?) understanding model’s fit dyad level. caveat: sparse networks (density 0.1), raw accuracy can misleadingly high predicting “tie” every dyad already gets predictions right. Focus -diagonal cells confusion matrix see well model distinguishes true ties non-ties. Confusion Matrix (threshold = 0.5) accuracy flatters model: “predict modal class” baseline already gets printed baseline value, model’s lift baseline ’s meaningful. friendship network density ~13% baseline ~0.87, relevant question whether model’s accuracy materially 0.87, whether crosses 0.5. serious link prediction, area ROC curve (AUC) evaluates model’s ability rank true ties non-ties across thresholds rather relying single cutpoint. pROC precrec packages compute ROC PR-AUC pred_resp. sparse networks PR-AUC honest summary. useless model always predicts “tie” already AUROC near 0.5 PR-AUC near network density, gap PR-AUC density metric substantive interest.
code:
pred_resp <- predict(fit, type = "response")  # how well does the model classify? Y_vec <- as.vector(Y) pred_vec <- as.vector(pred_resp) keep <- !is.na(Y_vec)  # simple classification at threshold 0.5 pred_binary <- (pred_vec[keep] > 0.5) * 1 confusion <- table(Actual = Y_vec[keep], Predicted = pred_binary) knitr::kable(confusion, caption = "Confusion Matrix (threshold = 0.5)") accuracy <- sum(diag(confusion)) / sum(confusion) baseline <- max(mean(Y_vec[keep]), 1 - mean(Y_vec[keep]))  # always-predict-modal-class cat("
Accuracy:", round(accuracy, 3),     " | Baseline (predict modal class):", round(baseline, 3),     " | Lift:", round(accuracy - baseline, 3), "
") #>  #> Accuracy: 0.897  | Baseline (predict modal class): 0.872  | Lift: 0.025 # pROC: ROC curve and AUROC # install.packages("pROC") roc_obj <- pROC::roc(response = Y_vec[keep], predictor = pred_vec[keep],                      quiet = TRUE) auroc   <- as.numeric(pROC::auc(roc_obj))  # precrec: both ROC and Precision-Recall AUCs (the latter is more # informative when the positive class is rare, as in friendship networks) # install.packages("precrec") ev <- precrec::evalmod(scores = pred_vec[keep], labels = Y_vec[keep]) precrec::auc(ev)   # returns AUROC and PR-AUC side-by-side  cat("AUROC:", round(auroc, 3), "
",     "Baseline AUROC (random ranker):", 0.5, "
",     "Baseline PR-AUC (density of Y):", round(mean(Y_vec[keep]), 3), "
")

title: Your First AME Model
text: Reporting accuracy / AUC dyads model fit -sample fit, predictive performance. held-evaluation, mask random subset dyads NA fitting (AME sampler handles NA entries via data augmentation, masked dyads excluded likelihood), score predict(fit, type = "response") held-indices. Stratify split Y test set classes; density-0.1 network naive 10% mask leaves positive test dyads, report test-set positive count AUC. Two ML caveats. (1) actor identities train test (dyads masked); transductive link-prediction split, inductive one. latent positions ui,vju_i, v_j every actor still informed non-masked dyads. inductive evaluation holds entire actors, drop subset rows/columns YY fitting score model’s predictions actors using covariates (held-actors posterior ui,vju_i, v_j, predictions collapse Xβ+̂mean+b̂meanX\beta + \hat a_{\text{mean}} + \hat b_{\text{mean}}, unsurprisingly performs worse). (2) fit single chain modest length; stable held-AUC estimates run ame_parallel(..., n_chains = 4) pool. standard lame fit network n≲200n \lesssim 200 takes seconds minutes nscan = 2000; n≈500n \approx 500 expect tens minutes, beyond n≈1000n \approx 1000 per-iteration cost O(n2)O(n^2) dyadic operations starts dominate fast point estimator (ame_als(), see fast estimation vignette) recommended exploration tool. GPU backend current release.
code: # 80/20 stratified mask of off-diagonal dyads set.seed(6886) off_diag    <- which(row(Y) != col(Y) & !is.na(Y)) pos_idx     <- off_diag[Y[off_diag] == 1] neg_idx     <- off_diag[Y[off_diag] == 0] test_idx    <- c(sample(pos_idx, round(0.2 * length(pos_idx))),                  sample(neg_idx, round(0.2 * length(neg_idx))))  Y_train               <- Y Y_train[test_idx]     <- NA      # mask test dyads from the likelihood  fit_train <- ame(Y_train, Xdyad = Xdyad, Xrow = Xrow, Xcol = Xcol,                  R = 2, family = "binary",                  rvar = TRUE, cvar = TRUE, dcor = TRUE,                  burn = 500, nscan = 2000, odens = 25,                  verbose = FALSE, gof = FALSE)  pred_train <- predict(fit_train, type = "response")  # `evaluate_heldout()` is the shipped helper that vectorises the same scoring # call, gates AUROC / PR-AUC on whether {precrec} (preferred) or {pROC} is # installed, and falls back to Brier + log-loss otherwise. It accepts the # observed Y, predictions, and a logical TEST mask of the same shape. test_mask           <- matrix(FALSE, nrow(Y), ncol(Y),                               dimnames = dimnames(Y)) test_mask[test_idx] <- TRUE evaluate_heldout(y_obs = Y, y_pred = pred_train,                  mask = test_mask, family = "binary") # columns: n_eval, auroc, auprc, brier, logloss cat("Test dyads:", length(test_idx),     " | positives:", sum(Y[test_idx] == 1), "\n")

title: Your First AME Model
text: can generate new networks fitted posterior. posterior predictive check asks: draw parameter set posterior simulate network parameters, simulated network look like observed one whatever feature care (, overall density)? observed feature lies inside simulated distribution, model reproduces aspect data; lies tail, model missing something .
code: sims <- simulate(fit, nsim = 100)  # compare simulated vs observed density sim_densities <- sapply(sims$Y, function(y) mean(y, na.rm = TRUE)) obs_density <- mean(Y, na.rm = TRUE)  ggplot(data.frame(density = sim_densities), aes(x = density)) +     geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "grey60") +     # dual-encode the observed density as colour AND linetype so the cue     # survives greyscale printing and colour-blind viewing (Okabe-Ito orange).     geom_vline(xintercept = obs_density,                color = "#D55E00", linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +     labs(title = "Posterior Predictive Check: Network Density",             subtitle = "Dashed orange line = observed; grey histogram = simulated from model",             x = "Network Density (Mean Tie Probability)", y = "Replicate Count") +     theme_bw() +     theme(panel.border = element_blank())

title: Your First AME Model
text: Choosing R (latent dimensions). Start R = 0 (latent space), try R = 1 R = 2. Compare GOF statistics. adding dimensions doesn’t improve GOF, covariates additive effects sufficient. ame() / lame() warn R > floor(n/3) (min(nA, nB)/3 bipartite) rank multiplicative effects start absorbing structure belongs additive effects latent positions stop interpretable; practice R = 2 R = 3 right default unless specific reason push higher. MCMC settings. exploratory work, burn = 500, nscan = 2000, odens = 25 fine. final run, use least burn = 2000, nscan = 10000, odens = 25 check convergence trace_plot(). Variance components. Include rvar = TRUE cvar = TRUE unless specific reason . Include dcor = TRUE directed networks reciprocity plausible. Interpreting coefficients. binary networks, coefficients probit scale. Use predict(fit, type = "response") get predicted probabilities.

text: Multiple time periods? See lame overview longitudinal models Two types nodes? See bipartite vignette Evolving network structure? See dynamic effects vignette data types? family argument supports normal, binary, ordinal, poisson, tobit,

title: Your First AME Model
text: Hoff, PD (2021). Additive Multiplicative Effects Network Models. Statistical Science 36, 34–50. Minhas, S., Dorff, C., Gallop, M. B., Foster, M., Liu, H., Tellez, J., & Ward, M. D. (2022). Taking dyads seriously. Political Science Research Methods, 10(4), 703–721.

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: Networks change. Countries close allies decade ago may drifted apart. legislator’s co-sponsorship patterns shift gain seniority switch committee assignments. user’s purchasing habits evolve tastes change. standard AME model assumes actor fixed latent position fixed additive effects across time periods. reasonable starting point (pooling across time gives data precise estimates), can miss important dynamics. lame package provides two mechanisms letting model capture temporal change: dynamic_uv time-varying latent positions dynamic_ab time-varying additive effects. vignette explains options , use , interpret results.

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: standard AME model longitudinal data, tie actors ii jj time tt : yij,t=β′xij,t+ai+bj+ui′vj+ϵij,ty_{ij,t} = \beta'x_{ij,t} + a_i + b_j + u_i'v_j + \epsilon_{ij,t} covariates (xij,tx_{ij,t}) can vary time, everything else (sender effect aia_i, receiver effect bjb_j, latent positions uiu_i vjv_j) constant. means model assumes country’s tendency sanction others, position latent “sanctioning space,” 1993 2000.

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: set dynamic_uv = TRUE, actor’s latent position evolves time according AR(1) process: Ui,k,t=ρuvUi,k,t−1+ϵi,k,t,ϵi,k,t∼N(0,σuv2)U_{,k,t} = \rho_{uv} \, U_{,k,t-1} + \epsilon_{,k,t}, \quad \epsilon_{,k,t} \sim N(0, \sigma_{uv}^2)Vj,k,t=ρuvVj,k,t−1+ηj,k,t,ηj,k,t∼N(0,σuv2)V_{j,k,t} = \rho_{uv} \, V_{j,k,t-1} + \eta_{j,k,t}, \quad \eta_{j,k,t} \sim N(0, \sigma_{uv}^2) Note innovation parameterisation storage convention. symbol σuv2\sigma_{uv}^2 variance period--period innovation ϵi,k,t\epsilon_{,k,t}, marginal/stationary variance Ui,k,tU_{,k,t} — -fit field fit$sigma_uv holds per-iteration posterior draws innovation standard deviation σuv\sigma_{uv} (mean(fit$sigma_uv) posterior-mean SD mean(fit$sigma_uv)^2 recovers innovation variance). stationary AR(1), implied marginal variance σuv2/(1−ρuv2)\sigma_{uv}^2 / (1 - \rho_{uv}^2). matters simulation code supplies SD rnorm() recurrence: using marginal SD makes process variable factor 1/(1−ρuv2)1/(1 - \rho_{uv}^2). autoregressive parameter ρuv\rho_{uv} controls persistent positions . value close 1 means positions change slowly, last year’s position strong predictor year’s. value close 0 means positions essentially re-drawn period. practice, ρuv\rho_{uv} estimated data. useful believe underlying community structure evolving: alliances shift, social groups re-form, trading blocs realign.

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: set dynamic_ab = TRUE, sender receiver effects evolve time: ai,t=ρabai,t−1+ϵi,t,ϵi,t∼N(0,σab2)a_{,t} = \rho_{ab} \, a_{,t-1} + \epsilon_{,t}, \quad \epsilon_{,t} \sim N(0, \sigma_{ab}^2)bj,t=ρabbj,t−1+ηj,t,ηj,t∼N(0,σab2)b_{j,t} = \rho_{ab} \, b_{j,t-1} + \eta_{j,t}, \quad \eta_{j,t} \sim N(0, \sigma_{ab}^2) innovation parameterisation applies dynamic_uv: σab2\sigma_{ab}^2 innovation variance, fit$sigma_ab holds posterior draws innovation standard deviation σab\sigma_{ab} (square posterior mean get innovation variance); implied stationary marginal variance σab2/(1−ρab2)\sigma_{ab}^2 / (1 - \rho_{ab}^2). sampler sample_dynamic_ab_cpp uses stationary distribution N(0,σab2/(1−ρab2))N(0, \sigma_{ab}^2 / (1 - \rho_{ab}^2)) prior initial state ai,1a_{,1} — .e. t=0t = 0 prior precision 1/(σab2/(1−ρab2))1 / (\sigma_{ab}^2 / (1 - \rho_{ab}^2)) rather one-step innovation precision 1/σab21 / \sigma_{ab}^2 — runs single-pass Gibbs sweep across tt interior full conditional combines AR(1) look-back ai,t−1a_{,t-1} look-ahead ai,t+1a_{,t+1} (smoothing-style update), reducing look-back alone t=T−1t = T - 1. Substantively, dynamic_ab captures changes actors’ overall activity levels. country might become active sanctioner change government. student might become less socially active across semesters. changes much actor participates, connect (’s dynamic_uv captures). can use either option alone combine . practice, dynamic_ab often good place start, since changes overall activity common relatively easy estimate. dynamic_uv adds flexibility also parameters.

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: third dynamic option lets regression coefficients evolve time. set dynamic_beta = TRUE (pass subset like dynamic_beta = "dyad" make dyadic coefficients dynamic), selected coefficient follows independent AR(1): βk,t=ρβ,b(k)βk,t−1+ϵk,t,ϵk,t∼N(0,σβ,b(k)2Λkk)\beta_{k,t} = \rho_{\beta,b(k)} \, \beta_{k,t-1} + \epsilon_{k,t}, \quad \epsilon_{k,t} \sim N(0, \sigma_{\beta,b(k)}^2 \Lambda_{kk}) b(k)b(k) block coefficient kk (intercept / dyad / row / col), coefficients block share AR(1) persistence ρβ,b(k)\rho_{\beta,b(k)} innovation scale σβ,b(k)\sigma_{\beta,b(k)}. diagonal matrix Λ\Lambda per-coefficient scale built (period-averaged) design XtX innovation variance coefficient kk commensurate data-level information βk\beta_k (gg-prior analogue; see build_beta_state_scales() source); construction Λ\Lambda diagonal, innovations independent within block. package draws entire time path β\beta jointly via forward-filter / backward-sample (FFBS; Carter & Kohn 1994, Frühwirth-Schnatter 1994), efficient state-space sampler conditionally-Gaussian dynamic linear models. per-block quantity innovation SD σβ,b\sigma_{\beta,b} (variance); conditional variance innovation coefficient kk σβ,b(k)2Λkk\sigma_{\beta,b(k)}^2 \Lambda_{kk}. Note storage difference dynamic_uv/dynamic_ab: fit$sigma_beta fit$rho_beta hold last-iteration scalars, full posterior draws live fit$SIGMA_BETA fit$RHO_BETA – summarise (e.g. colMeans(fit$SIGMA_BETA)), square last-iteration fit$sigma_beta. useful believe covariate’s effect changes time: maybe trade increases probability cooperation strongly recession, peer’s behavior matters certain semesters. dynamic-coefficient path reported coef(fit) p x T matrix, per-period 95% CIs available via confint(fit). can pass dynamic_beta five forms: FALSE (default): every coefficient static. TRUE: every coefficient becomes dynamic. character vector block shortcuts: "intercept", "dyad", "row", "col", coefficient name colnames(fit$BETA). integer vector: column indices BETA layout. logical vector length p: per-coefficient mask. intercept (nodal coefficient) included dynamic_beta, additive-effects sampler imposes sum--zero constraint (b) intercept additive effects remain jointly identified. happens automatically. AR(1) hyperparameters ρβ\rho_\beta σβ2\sigma_\beta^2 priors: ρβ∼TruncNormal(0.8,0.15,0,0.999)\rho_\beta \sim \text{TruncNormal}(0.8, 0.15, 0, 0.999) σβ2∼InverseGamma(2,1)\sigma_\beta^2 \sim \text{InverseGamma}(2, 1) can override via prior = list(rho_beta_mean = ..., rho_beta_sd = ..., sigma_beta_shape = ..., sigma_beta_scale = ...).

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: Simulate small longitudinal network single dyadic covariate’s effect grows linearly time, fit dynamic_beta = "dyad". Migration warning amen / static-lame users. amen::ame() static lame fit, fit$BETA 2-D [n_stored, p] matrix apply(fit$BETA, 2, mean) gives posterior mean per coefficient. dynamic_beta active, fit$BETA becomes 3-D [n_stored, p, T] array. second margin still coefficient index shapes, apply(fit$BETA, 2, mean) still returns length-p vector looks superficially correct, now silently averages across iterations time periods. call error, output length matches old code expected, silent collapse can survive downstream plots single “average effect” hides time variation user trying recover. Two safe patterns: per-period posterior mean X1_dyad track linear ramp beta_t_true = c(-0.5, -0.125, 0.25, 0.625, 1.0). AR(1) prior provides mild shrinkage reduces per-period variance compared fitting separate ame() per period. summary(fit_db) prints Dynamic coefficients per period block per-coefficient Mean | Min | Max | Drift_pct | Dynamic columns; Drift_pct 100 * (Max - Min) / |Mean|, useful spotting coefficients move across periods.
code: library(lame) set.seed(2026)  n_db <- 15 T_db <- 5 beta_t_true <- seq(-0.5, 1.0, length.out = T_db)   # truth: rising effect  X_db <- replicate(T_db, matrix(rnorm(n_db * n_db), n_db, n_db), simplify = FALSE) Y_db <- vector("list", T_db) for (t in seq_len(T_db)) {     Yt <- beta_t_true[t] * X_db[[t]] + matrix(rnorm(n_db * n_db, 0, 0.4), n_db, n_db)     diag(Yt) <- NA     rownames(Yt) <- colnames(Yt) <- paste0("a", seq_len(n_db))     Y_db[[t]] <- Yt } names(Y_db) <- paste0("t", seq_len(T_db)) X_db_arr <- lapply(X_db, function(x) array(x, c(n_db, n_db, 1)))  fit_db <- lame(Y_db, Xdyad = X_db_arr,                family = "normal", R = 0,                nscan = 200, burn = 50, odens = 5,                dynamic_beta = "dyad", verbose = FALSE)  # fit$BETA is a 3-D array [n_stored x p x T] dim(fit_db$BETA) #> [1] 40  2  5  # coef() returns a p x T matrix of posterior means coef(fit_db) #>                     t1           t2           t3           t4           t5 #> intercept -0.008086411 -0.008086411 -0.008086411 -0.008086411 -0.008086411 #> X1_dyad   -0.475585013 -0.126231556  0.229613231  0.625957779  0.997500051  # confint() returns one row per coef[t] with 95% credible interval head(confint(fit_db), 8) #>                      2.5%        97.5% #> intercept[t1] -0.02780649  0.006971105 #> X1_dyad[t1]   -0.54245212 -0.431430480 #> intercept[t2] -0.02780649  0.006971105 #> X1_dyad[t2]   -0.17701452 -0.058029029 #> intercept[t3] -0.02780649  0.006971105 #> X1_dyad[t3]    0.17688772  0.287365069 #> intercept[t4] -0.02780649  0.006971105 #> X1_dyad[t4]    0.56717597  0.672599140 # check the shape before summarising coefficients if (length(dim(fit_db$BETA)) == 3L) {     # 3-D: take posterior mean over the iteration margin -> [p, T]     pm <- apply(fit_db$BETA, c(2, 3), mean) } else {     # 2-D amen-style fit     pm <- apply(fit_db$BETA, 2, mean) }  # or just use coef(), which returns a [p, T] matrix for dynamic fits # and a length-p vector for static fits -- no shape sniffing required coef(fit_db)

previous_headings: What Are Dynamic Effects? > Dynamic Regression Coefficients (dynamic_beta)
title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: lame() exposes stable argument dynamic_beta_kind selecting prior family time-varying coefficients. release full decision tree : Mean-reverting coefficient? Use default dynamic_beta_kind = "ar1". Permanent / unit-root drift (mean reversion)? Use dynamic_beta_kind = "rw1". Smooth curvature (acceleration matters; second-order smoothness)? Use dynamic_beta_kind = "rw2". Smooth known length-scale variation? Use dynamic_beta_kind = "matern32" (set length scale via prior = list(matern32_length_scale = ...); defaults max(2, T/4)). stationarity warning fires fitting AR(1), meaning posterior ρβ\rho_\beta concentrated 0.97, refit "rw1" ("rw2" acceleration matters). "random_walk" accepted alias "rw1". "rw2" "matern32" samplers use R-level joint Gaussian update (slower C++ FFBS used "ar1" / "rw1", produces full posterior draws works T). fit_db setup can refit kind. call swaps dynamic_beta_kind argument; coef() returns p x T matrix regardless. time_index supplied unequal gaps, AR(1) / RW1 conditional variance automatically scaled gap (per-coefficient diagonal entry, suppressing Λkk\Lambda_{kk} readability): qt=σβ2⋅1−ρβ2Δt1−ρβ2q_t = \sigma_\beta^2 \cdot \frac{1 - \rho_\beta^{2\Delta_t}}{1 - \rho_\beta^2} quarterly--annual data, example, gets correct prior. full diagonal entry σβ2Λkk(1−ρβ2Δt)/(1−ρβ2)\sigma_\beta^2 \Lambda_{kk} (1 - \rho_\beta^{2\Delta_t}) / (1 - \rho_\beta^2); Λ\Lambda factor period-invariant per-coefficient scale used equal-gap case. Equal-gap time_index byte-identical leaving unspecified. dynamic_beta_kind = "rw2" "matern32", time_index consumed joint precision-matrix construction (RW2 uses second-difference operator supplied positions; Matern 3/2 uses continuous-time kernel k(s,t)=(1+3|s−t|/ℓ)exp⁡(−3|s−t|/ℓ)k(s, t) = (1 + \sqrt{3}|s-t|/\ell)\exp(-\sqrt{3}|s-t|/\ell)), unequal-gap support uniform across kinds even though closed-form qtq_t AR(1)/RW1 case.
code: # rw1: random walk, rho pinned at 1 fit_rw1 <- lame(Y_db, Xdyad = X_db_arr, family = "normal", R = 0,                 nscan = 200, burn = 50, odens = 5,                 dynamic_beta = "dyad", dynamic_beta_kind = "rw1",                 verbose = FALSE) round(coef(fit_rw1), 3) #>               t1     t2     t3     t4     t5 #> intercept -0.006 -0.006 -0.006 -0.006 -0.006 #> X1_dyad   -0.478 -0.122  0.223  0.627  0.988 # rw2: smooth with curvature fit_rw2 <- lame(Y_db, Xdyad = X_db_arr, family = "normal", R = 0,                 nscan = 200, burn = 50, odens = 5,                 dynamic_beta = "dyad", dynamic_beta_kind = "rw2",                 verbose = FALSE) round(coef(fit_rw2), 3) #>               t1     t2     t3     t4     t5 #> intercept -0.007 -0.007 -0.007 -0.007 -0.007 #> X1_dyad   -0.480 -0.130  0.232  0.624  1.001 # matern 3/2: smooth gp with a length-scale knob fit_m32 <- lame(Y_db, Xdyad = X_db_arr, family = "normal", R = 0,                 nscan = 200, burn = 50, odens = 5,                 dynamic_beta = "dyad", dynamic_beta_kind = "matern32",                 prior = list(matern32_length_scale = 2),                 verbose = FALSE) round(coef(fit_m32), 3) #>               t1     t2     t3     t4     t5 #> intercept -0.007 -0.007 -0.007 -0.007 -0.007 #> X1_dyad   -0.476 -0.132  0.232  0.627  0.993 # observations at t = 1, 2, 4, 8 (a doubling gap structure) fit_gaps <- lame(Y_db, Xdyad = X_db_arr, family = "normal", R = 0,                  nscan = 200, burn = 50, odens = 5,                  dynamic_beta = "dyad",                  time_index = c(1, 2, 4, 8, 16)[seq_len(T_db)],                  verbose = FALSE) fit_gaps$time_index #> [1]  1  2  4  8 16 round(coef(fit_gaps), 3) #>               t1     t2     t3     t4     t5 #> intercept -0.009 -0.009 -0.009 -0.009 -0.009 #> X1_dyad   -0.477 -0.126  0.230  0.627  0.996

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: multiple coefficient blocks dynamic (intercept / dyad / row / col), block AR(1) hyperparameters (ρb,σb)(\rho_b, \sigma_b) default, short panels (small TT) can leave one block’s hyperparameters badly -identified. dynamic_beta_pool adds hierarchical layer shrinks block-level hyperparameters toward shared mean: use "rho" want blocks share common persistence (believe e.g. dyadic nodal coefficients drift similar speed data alone can’t pin ρb\rho_b ), "sigma" blocks share innovation scale (common case blocks standardised consistently), "" want full hierarchical pooling. default "none" leaves block independent. Pooling implemented hierarchical MH step block-level hyperpriors valuable T≤5T \le 5 panels per-block posteriors otherwise collapse priors. Diagnostic check. pooling , compare per-block posterior dispersion (rho_beta, sigma_beta) identical fit dynamic_beta_pool = "none". pooling layer useful work -block sd--means shrink "" relative "none". short panels single dynamic block (dynamic_beta = "dyad" alone) hierarchical layer blocks borrow strength , pooled fit can actually look noisier RHO_BETA hyperprior near-flat mixing distribution single posterior draw; pooling earns keep two blocks (e.g. dynamic_beta = c("intercept", "dyad", "row", "col")) dynamic .
code: fit_pool <- lame(Y_db, Xdyad = X_db_arr, family = "normal", R = 0,                  nscan = 200, burn = 50, odens = 5,                  dynamic_beta = "dyad", dynamic_beta_pool = "both",                  verbose = FALSE) fit_pool$dynamic_beta_pool #> [1] "both"

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: research questions ask whether one actor responds covariate differently another, whether gap evolves, set dynamic_beta_per_actor = "row" ("col") choose relevant covariate via per_actor_covariate_idx. sampler imposes per-period sum--zero constraint per-actor deviations, population coefficient remains coef(fit) deviations stored fit$THETA_ACTOR (n_iter x n_actors x T array default keep_per_actor = "auto"). Known dimnames gap. theta_actor_mean currently returned without rownames()/colnames() even input Y actor names named periods. need labelled output, attach manually fit using whatever object fit still carries labels, e.g. dimnames(fit_pa$theta_actor_mean) <- list(rownames(fit_pa$YPM[[1]]), names(fit_pa$YPM)). sum--zero identifiability constraint (per_actor_identifiability = "center", default) enforced exactly: colSums(fit_pa$theta_actor_mean) numerically zero every period.
code: fit_pa <- lame(Y_db, Xdyad = X_db_arr, family = "normal", R = 0,                dynamic_beta_per_actor = "row",                per_actor_covariate_idx = 1L,                keep_per_actor = "summary",                nscan = 200, burn = 50, odens = 5, verbose = FALSE) str(fit_pa$theta_actor_mean)   # n_actors x T posterior-mean deviations  # check the per-period sum-to-zero constraint colSums(fit_pa$theta_actor_mean)   # ~ 0 0 0 0 0
