title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: fitting, two helpers summarize coefficient path: summary(fit): prints Dynamic coefficients per period block (mentioned ) per-block posterior-mean rho_beta. stationarity warning fires , block, 5th percentile posterior ρβ\rho_\beta ≥0.97\ge 0.97and IQR 0.1 — , least 95% posterior mass sits 0.97 little spread. print method recommends switching dynamic_beta_kind = "rw1". companion forecast warning (next section) uses complementary trigger: upper bound central 95% credible interval reaching 0.99. detect_change_point(fit, threshold_bf = 5): heuristic regime-switch diagnostic. Computes maximum first-difference |Δβt||\Delta \beta_t| scaled σβ\sigma_\beta, compares prior-null reference built resampling (ρ,σ)(\rho, \sigma) posterior, reports tail-ratio score historical bf column. Large values (≥5) flag coefficients abrupt breaks. heuristic diagnostic, marginal-likelihood Bayes factor. Worked example simulated linear-ramp data (real break present, expect modest scores): row reports largest one-period change one coefficient, tail-ratio score, flag. diagnostic deliberately conservative: fires jump large relative AR(1) prior can absorb. modest planted break beta_t_true = c(rep(-0.5, 3), rep(1.0, 2)) reliably trip : T=5T = 5 score small noisy run run (often 0-2, occasionally clearing threshold 5), sampler can widen σβ\sigma_\beta absorb single step. stark break (e.g. c(rep(-2, 3), rep(2, 2))) pushes scaled first-difference reliably past prior reference (scores roughly 6-16 across runs, always clearing threshold 5). smooth linear ramp score 0, expected process abrupt jump. Interpret bf column bounded tail-ratio score: values 3 weak, values 3 10 suggest jump worth inspecting, values 10 indicate jump AR(1) prior difficulty generating. construction bf = Pr(M_post > m_star) / 0.05, m_star 95% quantile prior null, score bounded 20. value near 20 means posterior path almost never looks smooth prior reference, separate change-point model formal Bayes factor 20. t_hat column points largest scaled jump sits posterior; treat flag visual inspection coefficient trajectory, formal break-time estimate. short panels, score can unstable run run. prior elicitation fitting, see dynamic_beta_prior_summary(T = 10, kind = "ar1", rho_mean = 0.8, rho_sd = 0.15). simulates paths prior can check implied max first-difference, roughness, range, trend correlation distributions chosen T.
code: detect_change_point(fit_db, threshold_bf = 5) #>      coef bf m_post_mean m_prior_q95 t_hat  warn #> 1 X1_dyad  0   0.7085288    2.814472     4 FALSE

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: change-point diagnostic targets coefficients; temporal-trend posterior-predictive check targets network statistic . gof_temporal() fits linear time trend chosen network statistic (density / mean / reciprocity / transitivity), computes slope every simulate(fit) replicate, returns two-sided Gelman-style posterior-predictive p-value, p_pp = 2 * min(p_up, 1 - p_up). runs 0 (observed slope extreme tail) 1 (observed slope dead-centre). print method’s reading: small p_pp (0.05) means observed temporal trend incompatible fitted model; large p_pp (toward 1) means observed trend well covered predictive distribution. telling us? stat = "mean" tracks average edge weight across network period. simulation dyadic covariate zero-mean, βtXt\beta_t X_t averages roughly zero regardless βt\beta_t moves: network mean essentially trend (observed per-period slope near 0). high p_pp therefore confirms fit reproduces flat statistic; verdict (real, rising) trend coefficient. key distinction change-point diagnostic : gof_temporal() tests trends network statistic, detect_change_point() per-period coef(fit) test trends coefficient. static-beta fit also returns high (well-covered) p_pp , reason – trend network mean either model miss. gof_temporal() earns keep substantive statistic drifts – network whose density reciprocity climbs time. static fit track drift lands p_pp near 0, printed “incompatible” verdict points toward dynamic_beta (dynamic_ab / dynamic_uv) interpreting time-averaged summaries. probe whether coefficient trends, use per-period coef(fit), change-point diagnostic, static-vs-dynamic LOO comparison.
code: gof_temporal(fit_db, stat = "mean", n_rep = 200, seed = 1) #>  #> ── Temporal-trend posterior-predictive check ── #>  #> • Statistic: "mean" #> • Observed slope (per-period): 0.00534 #> • Replicates: 200 #> • Posterior-predictive p-value (two-sided): 0.83 #> Observed temporal trend is well covered by the fitted model.

what: Inspecting the priors actually in effect: prior_summary()
title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: Modelled rstanarm::prior_summary(), prior_summary(fit) prints hyperparameters actually used (defaults filled ) AR(1) coefficient block, variance components, g-prior beta. Always run per fit; cheapest way catch typo prior = list(...) override.
code: prior_summary(fit_db) #>  #> ── Priors in effect (lame fit) ── #>  #> Regression coefficients: `beta ~ N(0, g * sigma^2 * (X'X)^-1)` (g-prior). #> `g` (vector, top-level argument) = "225, 225" #> Note: `g` is set at top level on `lame()` -- not inside `prior = list(...)`. #> `Sab0` = "matrix(1, 0, 0, 1)" #> `eta0` = "4" #> `etaab` = "4" #>  #> ── Dynamic AR(1) priors #> `rho_beta_mean` = 0.8 #> `rho_beta_sd` = 0.15 #> `sigma_beta_shape` = 2 #> `sigma_beta_scale` = 1 #> `sigma_beta_init` = 0.25 #> `rho_beta_lower` = 0 #> `rho_beta_upper` = 0.999

text: single chain ever buys within-chain split-R̂\hat R. honest -chain diagnostic per-period coefficient path, run four parallel chains different seeds call rhat_dynamic_beta() per-chain fit list: Use rhat_mvt < 1.01 rhat_max_univariate < 1.01 joint pass condition path. univariate column compare Stan-era 1.01 threshold per scalar; multivariate column catches case individual periods look fine trajectory disagrees across chains.
code: fit_list_db <- lame_parallel(     Y_db, Xdyad = X_db_arr,     family = "normal", R = 0,     nscan = 200, burn = 50, odens = 5,     dynamic_beta = "dyad",     n_chains = 4, cores = 1,           # cores = 1 keeps the vignette portable     combine_method = "list",            # one fit per chain     verbose = FALSE ) #>  #> ── Running 4 chains sequentially ── #>  #> Starting chain 1 (`lame()`) #> Completed chain 1 #> Starting chain 2 (`lame()`) #> Completed chain 2 #> Starting chain 3 (`lame()`) #> Completed chain 3 #> Starting chain 4 (`lame()`) #> Completed chain 4  # multivariate (Brooks-Gelman) Rhat per coefficient on the length-T path, # alongside the max univariate split-Rhat over the T per-period scalars. rhat_dynamic_beta(fit_list_db) #>        coef  rhat_mvt rhat_max_univariate n_chains n_iter_per_chain T #> 1 intercept 0.9989955           0.9913762        4               40 5 #> 2   X1_dyad 1.0475509           1.0206500        4               40 5  # the pooled fit also routes through posterior::as_draws() with per-period # coefficients flattened to "coef[t]" variable names, so summarise_draws() # gives you ess_bulk / ess_tail per (coef, period). fit_pool_db <- combine_ame_chains(fit_list_db) #>  #> ── MCMC Convergence Diagnostics #> Number of chains: 4 #> Samples per chain: "400, 400, 400, 400" #> Diagnostics cover regression coefficients and variance components. #> ✔ All parameters converged (R-hat < 1.1) posterior::summarise_draws(posterior::as_draws(fit_pool_db)) #> # what: Sampler-failure diagnostics (the Gibbs analog of HMC divergences)
title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: lame sampler Gibbs / Metropolis-Hastings, HMC, produce HMC-style divergences. closest analog per-block Metropolis acceptance / tryError count, exposed every fit fit$mh_counters (alias fit$tryErrorChecks). post-MCMC warning fires automatically block exceeds 10% failure rate; routine reporting: Treat block >5% failures suspicious; combine rhat_dynamic_beta() summarise_draws() output full diagnostic pass. Pairing ess_bulk / ess_tail summarise_draws() mh_counters totals gives Gibbs-side equivalent Stan diagnostic triple (R-hat, ESS, divergences).
code: # per-block failure counts and acceptance proxies for the just-fit chain str(fit_db$mh_counters)

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: least one dynamic component active, predict(fit, h = K) propagates AR(1) / RW1 state-space model forward KK periods returns list KK matrices (one per future period). propagation samples (ρβ,σβ)(\rho_\beta, \sigma_\beta) posterior draw, resulting forecast accounts hyperparameter uncertainty. Forecast variance behavior depends dynamic_beta_kind. AR(1) |ρβ|<1|\rho_\beta| < 1 hh-step conditional variance σβ2(1−ρβ2h)/(1−ρβ2)\sigma_\beta^2 (1 - \rho_\beta^{2h}) / (1 - \rho_\beta^2) saturates stationary variance σβ2/(1−ρβ2)\sigma_\beta^2 / (1 - \rho_\beta^2) h→∞h \\infty. saturation level explodes ρβ→1\rho_\beta \1, AR(1) forecasts beyond periods become uninformative regime even though variance technically converges. RW1 (unit-root case) variance grows exactly linearly hh, contributing σβ2⋅h\sigma_\beta^2 \cdot h per step (hh-step conditional variance RW1 piece alone h⋅σβ2h \cdot \sigma_\beta^2, saturation). predict() emits one-per-fit warning upper bound central 95% posterior credible interval ρβ\rho_\beta reaches 0.99 (.e. quantile(RHO_BETA, 0.975) >= 0.99); note 0.99 forecast trigger intentionally higher 0.97 stationarity flag used summary(fit). regime, prefer dynamic_beta_kind = "rw1" underlying process genuinely non-stationary, treat horizons beyond h≈3h \approx 3 cautiously either case. Forecasting "rw2" / "matern32" supported currently uses AR(1) forward recursion ρβ\rho_\beta pinned 1 (since neither prior identifies AR(1) persistence), forecast variance grows linearly hh just like RW1; smoothing length-scale structure priors informs posterior path β\beta -sample shape --sample propagation. predict() warning copy kind-aware: AR(1) reports stationary-variance saturation explosion ρβ→1\rho_\beta \1, RW1 / RW2 / Matern 3/2 report linear variance growth, “refit dynamic_beta_kind = \"rw1\"” suggestion printed AR(1) (nag user already unit-root branch). can pass newdata = list_of_future_X_arrays combine counterfactual future covariates forecast (one n×n×pn \times n \times p array per future period). family = "poisson" fits supplied period_exposure fit time, future-period exposure passed predict(..., newexposure = ...). forecast multiplies exp⁡(η)\exp(\eta) newexposure[k] horizon kk, response-scale matrix reported units original Y. newexposure omitted, forecast reuses last observed period_exposure value (falls back 1 fits use exposure offsets).
code: # h = 3-step forecast on the link scale fc_link <- predict(fit_db, h = 3, type = "link") #> Warning: ! Posterior 95% interval for `rho_beta` reaches 0.998. #> ℹ Under AR(1) the `h`-step forecast variance saturates at the stationary level #>   `sigma_beta^2 / (1 - rho_beta^2)`, which itself explodes as `rho_beta -> 1`; #>   horizons beyond "3" become uninformative in this regime. #> ℹ Consider refitting with `dynamic_beta_kind = "rw1"` if the underlying process #>   is genuinely unit-root. length(fc_link)              # 3 (one matrix per future period) #> [1] 3 dim(fc_link[[1]])            # n x n #> [1] 15 15  # response scale applies the family inverse link per draw fc_resp <- predict(fit_db, h = 3, type = "response")  # by_draw = TRUE returns an [n, n, h, n_draws] array of per-draw forecasts fc_full <- predict(fit_db, h = 3, type = "response", by_draw = TRUE) dim(fc_full)                 # n x n x 3 x n_draws #> [1] 15 15  3 40  # interval = "credible" returns lower / median / upper per horizon fc_ci <- predict(fit_db, h = 3, type = "response", interval = "credible") str(fc_ci[[1]])              # list($lower, $median, $upper) #> List of 3 #>  $ lower : num [1:15, 1:15] -0.1042 -2.3041 -0.3186 -0.7478 -0.0547 ... #>  $ median: num [1:15, 1:15] 0.178 -1.089 0.991 -0.355 0.277 ... #>  $ upper : num [1:15, 1:15] 0.396 0.403 2.048 0.129 0.536 ... # poisson fit with period_exposure = c(1, 5, 25, 100) # pass the future exposure explicitly when h > 0: fc_pois <- predict(fit_pois, h = 2, type = "response",                    newexposure = c(200, 400))

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: Pass save_log_lik = TRUE lame attaches [n_stored, n_obs] pointwise log-likelihood matrix fit$log_lik. , loo::loo(fit) loo::waic(fit) work directly package registers S3 methods hand stored matrix loo::loo.matrix(): One important caveat cross-software comparisons. six families – normal, binary, cbin, tobit, poisson, ordinal – stored pointwise log-likelihood exact family-specific Y density response scale (Gaussian density normal, probit Bernoulli log-pmf binary/cbin, censored-Gaussian tobit, log-Poisson poisson, cumulative-probit log-pmf ordinal). six, elpd_loo directly comparable loo() result Stan / brms fit family data, modulo lame Gibbs sampler’s R̂\hat R ESS satisfactory. two rank likelihoods frn rrl, exact marginal needs GHK Monte Carlo currently available longitudinal path via log_lik_method = "observed_ghk"; default observed_exact two families falls back augmented-Z normal density emits one-time warning. Inspect fit$log_lik_method fit see branch used.
code: fit_ll <- lame(Y_db, Xdyad = X_db_arr, family = "normal", R = 0,                nscan = 200, burn = 50, odens = 5,                dynamic_beta = "dyad",                save_log_lik = TRUE,                verbose = FALSE)  dim(fit_ll$log_lik)        # [n_stored, n_observations] #> [1]   40 1050 loo_db <- loo::loo(fit_ll) #> Warning: Some Pareto k diagnostic values are too high. See help('pareto-k-diagnostic') for details. print(loo_db) #>  #> Computed from 40 by 1050 log-likelihood matrix. #>  #>          Estimate   SE #> elpd_loo   -511.8 22.6 #> p_loo        33.6  1.5 #> looic      1023.5 45.1 #> ------ #> MCSE of elpd_loo is NA. #> MCSE and ESS estimates assume independent draws (r_eff=1). #>  #> Pareto k diagnostic values: #>                           Count Pct.    Min. ESS #> (-Inf, 0.38]   (good)     671   63.9%   27       #>    (0.38, 1]   (bad)      370   35.2%   <NA>     #>     (1, Inf)   (very bad)   9    0.9%   <NA>     #> See help('pareto-k-diagnostic') for details.

text: per-iteration log-likelihood matrix exceed RAM (large n_stored x n_obs), pass save_log_lik = "chunked" instead stream per-column-chunk binary files. chunks recovered transparently call loo(fit) (directly via read_log_lik(fit)). -disk layout row-major within chunk one readBin per chunk suffices.

text: AR(1) coefficients innovation variances sensible default priors: ρuv∼TruncNormal(0.9,0.1,0,1)\rho_{uv} \sim \text{TruncNormal}(0.9, 0.1, 0, 1), centered high persistence ρab∼TruncNormal(0.8,0.15,0,1)\rho_{ab} \sim \text{TruncNormal}(0.8, 0.15, 0, 1), slightly less persistent σuv2,σab2∼InverseGamma(2,1)\sigma_{uv}^2, \sigma_{ab}^2 \sim \text{InverseGamma}(2, 1) can customized via prior argument strong beliefs rate change.

what: Genuine Dynamics: What They Look Like
title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: start example dynamics truly present, can see model recovers real signal. simulate 25 actors 5 periods actor’s latent position evolves via AR(1) process ρ=0.85\rho = 0.85 innovation SD σuv=0.3\sigma_{uv} = 0.3 (matching innovation parameterisation C++ sampler uses). Initial positions drawn SD 1.5, larger implied stationary SD 0.3/1−0.852≈0.570.3/\sqrt{1 - 0.85^2} \approx 0.57. seeds simulation strong latent structure AR(1) drifts toward stationarity, producing network realistic density (roughly 30%) negative intercept. trajectory plot shows actors move latent space time. genuine temporal structure, paths show coherent movement rather random jumping:  eight actors trajectory legend becomes rainbow loses meaning. Pass highlight = small character vector actor names grey rest colour highlighted set colour-blind-safe Okabe–Ito palette:  Always check convergence. Dynamic models parameters static models, adequate mixing harder achieve requires longer chains:
code: library(lame) set.seed(6886)  n_dyn <- 25 n_per <- 5 R_dyn <- 2  # evolving latent positions with AR(1) dynamics U <- matrix(rnorm(n_dyn * R_dyn, 0, 1.5), n_dyn, R_dyn) V <- matrix(rnorm(n_dyn * R_dyn, 0, 1.5), n_dyn, R_dyn)  Y_dyn <- list() for(t in 1:n_per) {     if(t > 1) {         U <- 0.85 * U + matrix(rnorm(n_dyn * R_dyn, 0, 0.3), n_dyn, R_dyn)         V <- 0.85 * V + matrix(rnorm(n_dyn * R_dyn, 0, 0.3), n_dyn, R_dyn)     }     eta <- -1 + U %*% t(V)     Y_t <- matrix(rbinom(n_dyn * n_dyn, 1, pnorm(eta)), n_dyn, n_dyn)     diag(Y_t) <- NA     rownames(Y_t) <- colnames(Y_t) <- paste0("A", 1:n_dyn)     Y_dyn[[t]] <- Y_t }  # iterations are small so the vignette builds quickly # use burn >= 1000 and nscan >= 5000 for real analyses. fit_dyn_real <- lame(Y_dyn, R = 2,     dynamic_uv = TRUE, dynamic_ab = TRUE,     family = "binary",     burn = 100, nscan = 1000, odens = 10,     verbose = FALSE, plot = FALSE)  summary(fit_dyn_real) #>  #> === Longitudinal AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> [1] "Y ~ a[i] + b[j] + rho*e[ji] + U[i,1:2] %*% V[j,1:2], family = 'binary'" #>  #> Time periods: 5  #> Family: binary  #> Mode: unipartite  #> Dynamic latent positions: enabled (rho_uv = 0.943 ) #> Dynamic additive effects: enabled (rho_ab = 0.542 ) #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>           Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper     #> intercept   -0.746    0.148  -5.031       0   -1.027   -0.452 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: stars are a visual hint from posterior mean / SD only; for inference use the credible intervals. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.120    0.097 #> cab    0.058    0.087 #> vb     0.134    0.081 #> rho    0.201    0.116 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) uv_plot(fit_dyn_real, plot_type = "trajectory") uv_plot(fit_dyn_real, plot_type = "trajectory",         highlight = c("A1", "A5", "A10", "A15")) trace_plot(fit_dyn_real)

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: understand dynamic model behaves signal, fit model independent networks (temporal correlation). useful null baseline: estimated rho values tell prior alone produces data uninformative. can compare estimated persistence parameters across two settings: 5 time periods might expect prior dominate, yet posterior means can substantially different prior centre 0.95. correlated fit lands upper-AR(1) range (posterior mean around 0.94 ; true 0.85 actually falls 95% credible interval, short panel length well-known small-sample upward bias AR(1) estimators large enough even interval misses truth), null fit collapses well prior centre data carry period--period correlation prior anchor . words: even deliberately tight prior centred near 1 always pull clearly nonsensical posterior back toward 1, strength pull depends data. even modest panel AR(1) parameter identifiable enough real work, posterior mean substitute inspecting trajectory plots, remain honest diagnostic point estimate rho_uv alone (compare coherent paths erratic paths ).
code: set.seed(6886) n <- 30 n_periods <- 5  # independent binary networks with no temporal structure Y_list <- list() for(t in 1:n_periods) {     Y_t <- matrix(rbinom(n*n, 1, 0.2), n, n)     diag(Y_t) <- NA     rownames(Y_t) <- colnames(Y_t) <- paste0("Actor", 1:n)     Y_list[[t]] <- Y_t }  # fit with a custom prior on rho_uv to illustrate prior sensitivity prior_custom <- list(     rho_uv_mean = 0.95,    # expect very slow change in latent positions     rho_uv_sd = 0.05       # tight prior )  fit_null <- lame(     Y = Y_list,     R = 2,     dynamic_uv = TRUE,     dynamic_ab = TRUE,     family = "binary",     prior = prior_custom,     burn = 100,     nscan = 1000,     odens = 10,     verbose = FALSE,     plot = FALSE )  summary(fit_null) #>  #> === Longitudinal AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> [1] "Y ~ a[i] + b[j] + rho*e[ji] + U[i,1:2] %*% V[j,1:2], family = 'binary'" #>  #> Time periods: 5  #> Family: binary  #> Mode: unipartite  #> Dynamic latent positions: enabled (rho_uv = 0.741 ) #> Dynamic additive effects: enabled (rho_ab = 0.536 ) #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>           Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper     #> intercept   -0.879    0.158  -5.556       0   -1.176   -0.651 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: stars are a visual hint from posterior mean / SD only; for inference use the credible intervals. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.078    0.042 #> cab    0.028    0.040 #> vb     0.080    0.042 #> rho    0.211    0.080 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) cat("Independent data rho_uv:", round(mean(fit_null$rho_uv), 3), "\n") #> Independent data rho_uv: 0.741 cat("Correlated data rho_uv:", round(mean(fit_dyn_real$rho_uv), 3), "\n") #> Correlated data rho_uv: 0.943

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: trajectory plot independent data looks different: paths erratic, without smooth coherence genuinely correlated positions.  additive effects show similar behavior. Without genuine temporal structure, trajectories reflect noise rather real shifts actor activity.
code: uv_plot(fit_null, plot_type = "trajectory") ab_plot(fit_null, effect = "sender", plot_type = "trajectory") #> ℹ Showing top 5 and bottom 5 actors by average effect #> → Use `show_actors` to specify actors to display

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: natural workflow fit several specifications compare goodness--fit. use independent data show dynamic effects add nothing signal. Compare GOF statistics across models. specification, compute posterior predictive p-values: often simulated network statistics exceed observed ones? Values near 0.5 indicate good fit. Since data generated without temporal structure, dynamic specifications systematically -fit static model. short chains small panel posterior-predictive p-values noisy line exactly across specifications – read comparison “specification clearly better ”, rather expecting identical numbers values pinned near 0.5.
code: # four small fits to compare specifications. nscan is intentionally # small here so the vignette stays under CRAN's vignette compute # budget; for a real comparison use nscan >= 2000 per fit and run them # under `ame_parallel()` with 4 chains. fit_static <- lame(Y_list, R = 2, family = "binary",                                     burn = 50, nscan = 300, odens = 5,                                     verbose = FALSE, plot = FALSE)  fit_uv <- lame(Y_list, R = 2, dynamic_uv = TRUE, family = "binary",                             burn = 50, nscan = 300, odens = 5,                             verbose = FALSE, plot = FALSE)  fit_ab <- lame(Y_list, R = 2, dynamic_ab = TRUE, family = "binary",                             burn = 50, nscan = 300, odens = 5,                             verbose = FALSE, plot = FALSE)  fit_full <- lame(Y_list, R = 2, dynamic_uv = TRUE, dynamic_ab = TRUE,                                 family = "binary",                                 burn = 50, nscan = 300, odens = 5,                                 verbose = FALSE, plot = FALSE) # compute p-values for each model and each GOF statistic compute_pvals <- function(fit) {     gof <- fit$GOF     sapply(names(gof), function(stat) {         mat <- gof[[stat]]         obs <- mat[, 1]           # first column = observed         sims <- mat[, -1]         # remaining = posterior predictive         mean(colMeans(sims) >= mean(obs))     }) }  gof_comparison <- rbind(     Static     = compute_pvals(fit_static),     Dynamic_UV = compute_pvals(fit_uv),     Dynamic_AB = compute_pvals(fit_ab),     Full       = compute_pvals(fit_full) ) round(gof_comparison, 3) #>            sd.rowmean sd.colmean dyad.dep cycle.dep trans.dep #> Static          0.883      0.983    0.550     0.583     0.433 #> Dynamic_UV      0.950      0.983    0.417     0.633     0.383 #> Dynamic_AB      1.000      0.967    0.950     0.650     0.367 #> Full            0.867      0.917    0.950     0.467     0.267

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: Use dynamic_ab suspect actors’ overall activity levels change time. common many settings: countries go isolationist vs. interventionist periods, users churn platforms, students become less engaged across semesters. Use dynamic_uv suspect underlying community structure shifting. stronger claim, just actors less active, pattern connects changing. Examples include political realignment, market disruption, generational turnover social network. Use dynamic_beta suspect covariate’s effect tie formation changes time, actors community structure stable. Example: trade’s effect alliance formation may strengthen economic recessions weaken otherwise; peer influence may matter semesters others. dynamic_beta right tool question strongly X translates Y, connecting (dynamic_uv’s territory) generally active (dynamic_ab’s). Use multiple together believe several types change happening simultaneously. flexible specification also data-hungry. short panels (time periods) sparse networks, dynamic parameters may well-identified, might better simpler specification. Stick static time periods, network structure genuinely stable, primarily care average covariate effects rather evolve.

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: One subtlety dynamic latent space models: latent space identified orthogonal rotation time point posterior draw. Even actor positions evolving smoothly truth, raw estimated UtU_t matrices can appear “jump” periods due arbitrary rotations, posterior means across draws can shrink toward zero different draws sit different rotations latent space. procrustes_align() function applies standard orthogonal-Procrustes rotation (Gower 1975) align time period’s positions previous one: Methodological caveat. default, procrustes_align() aligns posterior-mean trajectory fit$U (n x R x T array posterior means) across time . Hoff (2005) / Sewell–Chen (2015) standard Procrustes-align posterior draw reference (first draw, posterior mean) computing per-period summaries, rotation indeterminacy per-draw property, per-mean one. per_draw = TRUE argument hook workflow – looks 4-D per-draw trajectory cube fit$U_full / fit$V_full ([n, R, T, S]). cube currently persisted either ame() lame(), per_draw = TRUE emits informational note falls back mean-trajectory alignment every fit today; full per-draw alignment planned extension. meantime, dynamic latent positions trajectory view (uv_plot(plot_type = "trajectory")) honest visualisation per-period credible intervals raw U_t entries. can also extract aligned positions tidy data frame using latent_positions() align = TRUE (default lame objects): data frame ready ggplot2 want build custom trajectory plots (e.g., highlighting specific actors overlaying external events).
code: # align latent positions across time aligned <- procrustes_align(fit_null) str(aligned$U)  # 3D array: actors x dimensions x time #>  num [1:30, 1:2, 1:5] -0.2481 -0.0187 0.0272 0.0907 0.0224 ... #>  - attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 3 #>   ..$ : chr [1:30] "Actor1" "Actor10" "Actor11" "Actor12" ... #>   ..$ : NULL #>   ..$ : NULL lp <- latent_positions(fit_null, align = TRUE) #> ℹ `posterior_sd` is "NA" because U/V samples were not saved. #> ℹ To get posterior SDs, refit with `posterior_opts = posterior_options(save_UV #>   = TRUE)`. #> This message is displayed once per session. head(lp) #>     actor dimension time       value posterior_sd type #> 1  Actor1         1    1 -0.24807855           NA    U #> 2 Actor10         1    1 -0.01872939           NA    U #> 3 Actor11         1    1  0.02719917           NA    U #> 4 Actor12         1    1  0.09072103           NA    U #> 5 Actor13         1    1  0.02235868           NA    U #> 6 Actor14         1    1 -0.05799678           NA    U

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: Outside dynamic-β\beta Rhat workflow , parallel wrapper recommended way () get honest -chain Rhat parameter (b) cut wall-clock time roughly linearly n_chains idle cores. ame_parallel() auto-dispatches ame() cross-sectional data lame() list / 3-D array; lame_parallel() thin wrapper forces longitudinal fitter. accept full ame() / lame() argument list via .... practical note scale. chain runs R worker, memory cost roughly n_chains x single-chain footprint; network n≈200n \approx 200 nscan = 4000 hundred MB per chain, fine laptop; n≈1000n \approx 1000 plan dozens GB run n_chains = 8 parallel prefer fewer, longer chains. GPU backend. numerical work CPU via RcppArmadillo, right scaling lever cores plus fast point estimator (ame_als() / lame_als(), see fast estimation vignette) n≳500n \gtrsim 500 exploratory passes; reserve full MCMC fit final calibrated run.
code: # 4 chains, one per core, pooled into a single fit. # `cores` defaults to `n_chains`; the wrapper clamps `cores` to # `parallel::detectCores(logical = TRUE)` and emits a `cli_warn` when you # over-request, so a stray `cores = 16` on a 4-core laptop will be capped # (not silently honoured). For a *physical*-core cap (avoiding hyperthread # oversubscription on BLAS-heavy workloads), pass it yourself: n_phys <- max(1L, parallel::detectCores(logical = FALSE) - 1L) data(YX_bin_list)  fit_pool <- ame_parallel(   Y               = YX_bin_list$Y,   Xdyad           = YX_bin_list$X,   family          = "binary",   R               = 2,   rvar = TRUE, cvar = TRUE, dcor = TRUE,   burn = 1000, nscan = 4000, odens = 25,   n_chains        = 4,   cores           = min(4, n_phys),   # set to 1 for sequential   combine_method  = "pool",            # one merged fit; use "list" for per-chain   verbose         = FALSE )  summary(fit_pool)            # pooled posterior, 4x the draws of a single chain  # `compute_mcmc_diagnostics()` reports per-parameter between-chain Rhat and # bulk/tail ESS off the pooled fit (when chains are preserved internally): compute_mcmc_diagnostics(fit_pool)

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: dynamic effects implemented C++ via Rcpp RcppArmadillo. AR(1) updates use Gibbs sampling block updates across actors time point, efficient large networks.

title: Dynamic Effects in Longitudinal AME Models
text: Hoff, PD (2021). Additive Multiplicative Effects Network Models. Statistical Science 36, 34–50. Hoff, PD (2005). Bilinear mixed-effects models dyadic data. Journal American Statistical Association 100(469), 286–295. original AME formulation underlying amen lame. Sewell, D. K., & Chen, Y. (2015). Latent space models dynamic networks. Journal American Statistical Association, 110(512), 1646-1657. Durante, D., & Dunson, D. B. (2014). Nonparametric Bayes dynamic modeling relational data. Biometrika, 101(4), 883-898. Carter, C. K., & Kohn, R. (1994). Gibbs sampling state space models. Biometrika 81(3), 541–553. original forward-filter / backward-sample algorithm used dynamic_beta_kind = "ar1" / "rw1". Frühwirth-Schnatter, S. (1994). Data augmentation dynamic linear models. Journal Time Series Analysis 15(2), 183–202. Independent derivation FFBS dynamic linear models. Gower, J. C. (1975). Generalized Procrustes analysis. Psychometrika 40(1), 33–51. orthogonal-Procrustes rotation underlying procrustes_align().

what: When to use the fast estimator
title: Fast (MCMC-free) AME Estimation
text: ame() lame() fit AME models Bayesian MCMC, gives calibrated posterior inference can take minutes. rapid model exploration, trying several ranks, screening covariates, getting starting values, package also ships fast, MCMC-free point estimator: ame_als(): cross-sectional networks lame_als(): longitudinal (replicated) networks ame_als_bootstrap(): bootstrap standard errors intervals fits model z_ij = mu + beta’x_ij + a_i + b_j + u_i’v_j + e_ij iterative block coordinate descent rather Gibbs sampling. typically tens hundreds times faster ame(), point estimates usually close. Use point estimate enough; use ame() / lame() target posterior summary.

previous_headings: When to use the fast estimator
title: Fast (MCMC-free) AME Estimation
text: numbers indicative normal-family AME R = 2; absolute timings depend heavily CPU, BLAS, whether high-clock workstation virtualised / WSL host. dev box (WSL2, single-threaded BLAS) benchmark ran roughly 2-4× slower workstation reference column, treat order-- magnitude. point estimator scales O(n² · iter) per BCD pass dominated linear system block; MCMC scales similarly per iteration pays constant running thousands iterations. “ratio” column actually feel: ALS fit completes MCMC finishes burn-reasonable n. ranges wide purpose: single-threaded BLAS virtualised host, small-n speed ratios measured several times larger “≈ 30×” figure older docs, MCMC’s per-iteration overhead fixed ALS solve nearly free n ≤ 100. Treat ratio “ALS finishes MCMC’s burn-” rather number plan . binary/poisson point-estimate speedup ratio much smaller (often 3-10× small networks) IRLS path runs 3-5 reweighting iterations per fit, MCMC binary sampler cheaper per iteration normal sampler. Bipartite unipartite ALS run broadly similar cost comparable dimensions; faster depends exact shape hardware, read much small differences. bootstrap can dominate. bootstrap = 100 parametric bootstrap runs full ALS fit 101 times. binary ame_als(R = 1, bootstrap = 100) n = 50, measured ≈ 135 s — slower equivalent MCMC fit. need point estimate, skip bootstrap (bootstrap = 0, default) use sandwich vcov()/confint() fit. need interval estimates scale, compare bootstrap cost regular ame() MCMC run; binary networks MCMC fit cheaper. benchmark hardware:
code:
set.seed(1) n <- 100 Y <- matrix(rnorm(n*n), n, n); diag(Y) <- NA rownames(Y) <- colnames(Y) <- paste0("a", sprintf("%03d", 1:n))  t_als <- system.time(ame_als(Y, R = 2, family = "normal", verbose = FALSE)) t_mcmc <- system.time(ame(Y, R = 2, family = "normal", burn = 500,                           nscan = 4000, odens = 25, verbose = FALSE, plot = FALSE)) cat("ALS:  ", round(t_als["elapsed"], 2), "s
",     "MCMC: ", round(t_mcmc["elapsed"], 2), "s
",     "ratio:", round(t_mcmc["elapsed"] / t_als["elapsed"], 1), "x
")

what: What ALS can do vs. what only MCMC does
title: Fast (MCMC-free) AME Estimation
text: ALS fast point estimator. covers static AME workflows several dynamic workflows, posterior-specific features still require Bayesian MCMC fits. current coverage : call unified front door MCMC-arguments, ALS dispatcher warns lists exactly ones ignored – arguments entry point actually accepts. Two caveats edges: nscan / burn / odens / g accepted--ignored warning ame(method = "als") lame(method = "als"). static ALS dispatcher warns ignores MCMC tuning arguments nscan, burn, odens, prior settings used fast path, g. Dynamic ALS posterior priors, use selected entries prior deterministic smoothing controls (prior$rho_ab_mean, prior$rho_beta_mean, prior$rho_uv_mean, prior$rho_G_mean, prior$lambda_*_als). Dynamic requests stricter: supported dynamic_ab, selected dynamic_beta, AR(1) t dynamic_uv, bipartite dynamic_G requests route dynamic ALS normal, binary, Poisson panels. Named changing-composition panels aligned union actor set actor-entry gaps break smoothing penalties. Student-t dynamic UV fits attach final local transition-weight matrices fit$lambda_u fit$lambda_v. lame(method = "als", dynamic_uv = TRUE, dynamic_uv_kind = "snap") routes lame_snap_als() supported normal unipartite bipartite panels; unsupported dynamic stacks raise specific error rather silently ignored. Use als_max_iter, als_tol, als_stability longer dynamic ALS runs start-sensitivity checks. Passing longitudinal-arguments dynamic_uv, dynamic_ab, dynamic_beta ame() (cross-sectional) raises usual “longitudinal-argument” error ALS runs, valid ame() arguments . n_chains ame() / ame_parallel() argument; lame() formal, lame(method = "als", n_chains = ...) raises “unused argument” error rather warn--ignore. Use bootstrap = N ALS uncertainty regardless entry point.

title: Fast (MCMC-free) AME Estimation
text: coef(), fitted(), residuals(), predict() plot() work model object. printed variance components (va, vb, cab, rho, ve) descriptive summaries fitted effects, random-effect variance components.
code: set.seed(1) n  <- 40 a  <- rnorm(n, 0, 0.5); b <- rnorm(n, 0, 0.5) Xd <- matrix(rnorm(n * n), n, n) Y  <- 0.5 + 0.8 * Xd + outer(a, b, "+") + matrix(rnorm(n * n), n, n) diag(Y) <- NA  fit <- ame_als(Y, Xdyad = Xd, R = 1, family = "normal", verbose = FALSE) fit #> AME fit via iterative block coordinate descent (fast, MCMC-free) #> Family: normal | Mode: unipartite | R = 1 #> Network: 40 x 40, 1 time slice #> Converged: TRUE in 5 iterations #>  #> Coefficients: #>  intercept dyad1_dyad  #>     0.6315     0.7944 #>  #> Variance components: #>      va     cab      vb     rho      ve  #>  0.1929  0.0461  0.2217 -0.0198  0.9395 #>  #> va/vb: variances of the sender/receiver effects | cab: their covariance | rho: #> dyadic residual reciprocity | ve: residual variance. #> (Descriptive summaries, not random-effect variance components.) #> Point estimate only; no uncertainty. Re-fit with `bootstrap = N` (e.g. #> `ame_als(Y, ..., bootstrap = 200)`) to attach bootstrap intervals in one call.

title: Fast (MCMC-free) AME Estimation
text: information criterion R. Fit values look elbow deviance / SSE: curve declines smoothly clear elbow, keep R small confirm chosen rank ame() fit.
code: devs <- sapply(0:3, function(r)     ame_als(Y, Xdyad = Xd, R = r, family = "normal",                 verbose = FALSE)$deviance) data.frame(R = 0:3, deviance = round(devs, 1)) #>   R deviance #> 1 0   1617.5 #> 2 1   1465.6 #> 3 2   1324.9 #> 4 3   1193.4

title: Fast (MCMC-free) AME Estimation
text: binary poisson data default non_normal_method = "irls" runs iteratively reweighted least squares loop, returning coefficients calibrated link scale (probit binary, log poisson). alternative non_normal_method = "transform" faster coefficients uncalibrated rank scale, good sign ranking effects magnitude.
code: set.seed(2) Yb <- 1 * (Y > median(Y, na.rm = TRUE)); diag(Yb) <- NA fb <- ame_als(Yb, Xdyad = Xd, R = 0, family = "binary", verbose = FALSE) coef(fb) #>  intercept dyad1_dyad  #> 0.01191482 0.80192554

title: Fast (MCMC-free) AME Estimation
text: point estimator carries uncertainty. Use bootstrap: type = "parametric" (default) simulates fresh outcomes fitted model refits; type = "block" resamples time slices available longitudinal fits several time points. confint() vcov() work bootstrap object. fast analytic alternative regression coefficients conditional sandwich covariance, vcov() / confint() applied directly fit. anti-conservative (holds additive multiplicative effects fixed); bootstrap recommended tool full inference.
code: bt <- ame_als_bootstrap(fb, R = 100, type = "parametric",                             seed = 1, verbose = FALSE) summary(bt) #>  #> ── Bootstrap results: fast AME estimator ─────────────────────────────────────── #> Type: parametric | Family: binary | Mode: unipartite #> Replicates: 100 valid of 100 (100%) #> Network: 40 x 40, 1 time slice, R = 0 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Regression coefficients #>            Estimate Boot SE 2.5%   97.5%  sig #> intercept  0.0119   0.139   -0.271 0.23       #> dyad1_dyad 0.8019   0.5385  0.746  1.0016 * #> * = 95% percentile CI excludes zero #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Variance components #>     Estimate Boot SE    2.5%  97.5% #> va    0.2443  0.2128  0.2276 0.5657 #> cab   0.0278  0.1098 -0.0662 0.1277 #> vb    0.2490  0.2289  0.2067 0.4989 #> rho  -0.0075  0.0402 -0.1028 0.0606 #> ve    1.9504 15.9786  2.0901 3.8576 #> ! point estimate outside its bootstrap CI for: ve -- a residual #> parametric-bootstrap bias; treat that interval as approximate. #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Additive effects (rotation-invariant, aggregated directly) #> Sender a: mean |SE| = 0.5983 #> Receiver b: mean |SE| = 0.596

what: ALS bootstrap vs MCMC posterior on the same data
title: Fast (MCMC-free) AME Estimation
text: useful sanity check fast estimator fit data engines overlay coefficient intervals. calibrated link (non_normal_method = "irls" binary, default) ALS bootstrap give coefficient story qualitatively similar MCMC posterior cases model fits well; large differences mean posterior fit better reference dataset.  Two intervals side zero substantial overlap means ALS fine point estimate coefficient; intervals disagree sign location point MCMC fit dataset.
code: # wrap Xd into a 3-D array with an explicit slice name so the ALS and MCMC # paths produce identical coefficient names (the auto-naming default differs # between the two engines) Xd_arr <- array(Xd, dim = c(nrow(Xd), ncol(Xd), 1),                 dimnames = list(NULL, NULL, "Xd"))  # refit the binary ALS fit with the bootstrap attached so tidy() returns # bootstrap-based intervals fb_b <- ame_als(Yb, Xdyad = Xd_arr, R = 0, family = "binary",                 verbose = FALSE, bootstrap = 100, bootstrap_seed = 1)  # fit the same data with MCMC using a short chain for a quick comparison fit_mcmc <- ame(Yb, Xdyad = Xd_arr, family = "binary", R = 0,                 burn = 200, nscan = 1000, odens = 5,                 verbose = FALSE, plot = FALSE, gof = FALSE,                 seed = 1)  # tidy both; the term names match, and the four columns we select # (term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high) are common to both engines # (ALS tidy adds an extra se_source column, which we drop here) als_tdy  <- broom::tidy(fb_b) mcmc_tdy <- broom::tidy(fit_mcmc) df <- rbind(     cbind(estimator = "ALS bootstrap",           als_tdy[, c("term", "estimate", "conf.low", "conf.high")]),     cbind(estimator = "MCMC posterior",           mcmc_tdy[, c("term", "estimate", "conf.low", "conf.high")]) )  library(ggplot2) ggplot(df, aes(x = estimate, y = term,                 colour = estimator, shape = estimator)) +     geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +     geom_pointrange(aes(xmin = conf.low, xmax = conf.high),                     position = position_dodge(width = 0.45),                     size = 0.5) +     scale_colour_manual(values = c("ALS bootstrap"  = "#0072B2",                                     "MCMC posterior" = "#D55E00")) +     scale_shape_manual(values  = c("ALS bootstrap"  = 16,                                     "MCMC posterior" = 17)) +     labs(x = "coefficient (probit scale)", y = NULL,          colour = NULL, shape = NULL) +     theme_bw() +     theme(panel.border    = element_blank(),           axis.ticks      = element_blank(),           legend.position = "bottom")

title: Fast (MCMC-free) AME Estimation
text: lame_als() longitudinal counterpart. effects static (pooled across time), non-dynamic lame() fit.
code: set.seed(3) Yl <- replicate(5, {     # intercept 0.4 + sender/receiver structure + noise (no dyadic     # covariate is passed below, so only the intercept is recoverable)     m <- 0.4 + outer(a, b, "+") + matrix(rnorm(n * n), n, n)     diag(m) <- NA     m }, simplify = FALSE) lf <- lame_als(Yl, R = 1, family = "normal", verbose = FALSE) coef(lf) #> intercept  #> 0.4978209

title: Fast (MCMC-free) AME Estimation
text: fast estimator returns point estimates bootstrap sandwich uncertainty. MCMC estimator returns posterior summaries.

title: Forecasting Longitudinal Networks with lame
text: lame() least one dynamic component (dynamic_beta, dynamic_ab, dynamic_uv) gives state-space model exactly right object forecasting future periods. vignette walks : Fit model dynamic coefficients. Forecast h periods ahead link scale response scale. Compute counterfactual forecasts feeding alternative future covariates. Visualise forecast per-coefficient ribbon plot. Diagnose whether forecast variance exploded (near- unit-root coefficients).

title: Forecasting Longitudinal Networks with lame
text: Forecasting needs fit dynamic coefficient actually identified period, simulate panel delivers : 30 actors 6 periods, single dyadic covariate trade, directed binary outcome cooperation. effect trade cooperation genuinely time-varying mean-reverting – follows AR(1) around 0.5 persistence 0.7, starting high (0.9) settling toward long-run level. network moderately dense (~37%), makes per-period coefficients estimable; regime forecasting well-posed. note data choice: dynamic_beta needs reasonable number periods enough ties per period pin coefficient period. sparse panels (say rare-event sanctions network ~2% density 3-4 years) carry per-period information, AR(1) persistence runs unit root, forecast variance explodes (Step 5 shows exactly failure mode). simulated panel lets us show forecasting working show failing. recovered trade_dyad path tracks simulated truth, (confirm Step 5) posterior ρβ\rho_\beta sits comfortably 1, forecast well-posed. quick diagnostic: summary(fit) prints per-block posterior-mean ρ_β fires stationarity warning , block, 5th percentile posterior ρ_β ≥ 0.97 IQR < 0.1 (.e. least 95% mass sits near unit root little spread). forecast-time warning fires complementary trigger: upper 97.5% credible bound ρ_β reaches 0.99. either warning fires, refit dynamic_beta_kind = "rw1". RW1 unit-root construction right prior permanent-drift coefficients.
code: library(lame) set.seed(2026)  n_fc <- 30 T_fc <- 6 rho_true   <- 0.7 beta_bar   <- 0.5 beta_t_true <- numeric(T_fc) beta_t_true[1] <- 0.9 for (t in 2:T_fc) {     beta_t_true[t] <- beta_bar + rho_true * (beta_t_true[t - 1] - beta_bar) +         rnorm(1, 0, 0.15) }  Xdyad <- lapply(seq_len(T_fc), function(t) {     x <- matrix(rnorm(n_fc * n_fc), n_fc, n_fc)     array(x, dim = c(n_fc, n_fc, 1),           dimnames = list(NULL, NULL, "trade")) }) a_fc <- rnorm(n_fc, 0, 0.4) b_fc <- rnorm(n_fc, 0, 0.4) Y <- lapply(seq_len(T_fc), function(t) {     eta <- -0.5 + beta_t_true[t] * Xdyad[[t]][, , 1] + outer(a_fc, b_fc, "+")     Yt  <- matrix(rbinom(n_fc * n_fc, 1, pnorm(eta)), n_fc, n_fc)     diag(Yt) <- NA     rownames(Yt) <- colnames(Yt) <- sprintf("a%02d", seq_len(n_fc))     Yt }) names(Y) <- paste0("t", seq_len(T_fc))  round(beta_t_true, 3)                                   # the truth we recover #> [1] 0.900 0.858 0.589 0.583 0.545 0.432 sapply(Y, function(y) round(mean(y, na.rm = TRUE), 3))  # per-period density #>    t1    t2    t3    t4    t5    t6  #> 0.400 0.374 0.378 0.367 0.352 0.357  fit <- lame(     Y, Xdyad = Xdyad,     family = "binary", R = 0,     dynamic_beta = "dyad",      # the trade coefficient is AR(1)     dynamic_beta_kind = "ar1",  # mean-reverting; switch to "rw1" for drift     nscan = 800, burn = 200, odens = 5,     verbose = FALSE ) #> Note: Square matrix assumed to be unipartite. Use mode='bipartite' for square bipartite networks.  # the recovered per-period coefficient tracks the simulated path round(coef(fit)["trade_dyad", ], 3) #>    t1    t2    t3    t4    t5    t6  #> 0.913 0.867 0.587 0.532 0.532 0.519

title: Forecasting Longitudinal Networks with lame
text: typical cell fc_ci[[1]] carries informative lower / median / upper triple – genuine sub-interval [0, 1] rather whole range, coefficient path mean-reverting (rho_beta stays comfortably 1). small fraction cells reach boundary, vast majority stay well inside . interval widens horizon h forecast uncertainty accumulates; stationary fit rarely collapses toward degenerate [0, 1] see pervasively near unit root (Step 5 shows failure mode warning catches ). forecast propagates (β_t, a_t, b_t, U_t, V_t) forward drawing joint posterior dynamic state-space hyperparameters (ρ_β, σ_β, ρ_ab, σ_ab, ρ_uv, σ_uv) posterior draw. forecasting machinery works family; binary example just illustration. family = "normal", type = "response" returns forecasted continuous outcome; family = "poisson", response-scale forecast multiplies exp(η) future-period exposure (pass via newexposure = ...; see also period_exposure note vignettes/dynamic_effects.Rmd).
code: set.seed(1)   # predict(h=) propagates the AR(1) state stochastically; seed for reproducibility # 3-step-ahead forecast on the link scale (linear predictor) fc_link <- predict(fit, h = 3, type = "link") length(fc_link)         # 3 (one matrix per future period) #> [1] 3 dim(fc_link[[1]])       # n x n  (30 x 30 here; unipartite) #> [1] 30 30  # response-scale forecast applies the family inverse link per draw fc_resp <- predict(fit, h = 3, type = "response") # binary: each entry is a posterior-mean predicted probability range(fc_resp[[1]], na.rm = TRUE) #> [1] 0.02417271 0.89431247  # by_draw = TRUE returns the full [n, n, h, n_draws] array fc_full <- predict(fit, h = 3, type = "response", by_draw = TRUE) dim(fc_full)            # n x n x 3 x n_draws #> [1]  30  30   3 160  # interval = "credible" returns a list of length-3 (lower / median / upper) # matrices per period at the requested quantiles (default 95%) fc_ci <- predict(fit, h = 3, type = "response", interval = "credible") str(fc_ci[[1]])         # list($lower, $median, $upper), each n x n #> List of 3 #>  $ lower : num [1:30, 1:30] 0.00243 0.03421 0.00524 0.05889 0.10498 ... #>  $ median: num [1:30, 1:30] 0.0964 0.0475 0.1608 0.1226 0.154 ... #>  $ upper : num [1:30, 1:30] 0.6033 0.0637 0.736 0.2268 0.2097 ...

title: Forecasting Longitudinal Networks with lame
text: family = "poisson", pass period_exposure = e lame() (non-negative numeric vector length T) per-period rate scaled known exposure (e.g. weeks observed, population size, number edge-formation opportunities). sampler treats log(period_exposure[t]) fixed offset linear predictor: Poisson mean period t becomes period_exposure[t] * exp(η_t) instead exp(η_t). Setting non-trivial (value equal 1) period_exposure non-Poisson family rejected error. call predict(fit, h = K) later without newexposure, response-scale forecast reuses last observed exposure every future period; pass newexposure = rep(e_new, K) forecast different exposure path.

title: Forecasting Longitudinal Networks with lame
text: Pass newdata = list_of_h_future_X_arrays combine alternative future covariates forecast. array n x n x p slice order along 3rd dimension must match original dimnames(Xdyad[[1]])[[3]] fit (single slice, trade). trade coefficient positive mean-reverting, one-unit increase trade raises posterior-mean cooperation probability h = 3 average (mean median delta_h3 positive). per-dyad shift varies size, minority dyads even show small negative shift: three-step-ahead forecast coefficient uncertain (posterior h = 3 centred well zero nonzero mass ), marginal probit effect ϕ(η)β\phi(\eta)\,\beta smallest dyads whose baseline linear predictor η=ai+bj+βxij\eta = a_i + b_j + \beta x_{ij} already sits far 0.5 probability boundary. substantive interpretation, summarise delta_h3 dyads matter rather reporting aggregate summary(). Two cautions counterfactual covariates. (1) dynamic_beta draws coefficient path forward posterior, covariate change can produce different magnitudes different horizons, multiplier trade uncertain drifting. (2) Multiplicative counterfactuals covariates take negative zero values (centred trade covariate) make substantive sense; prefer additive shifts , used + 1 rather * 2 .
code: set.seed(1)   # forecast draws are stochastic; seed so the printed delta summary reproduces # baseline scenario: freeze the trade covariate at the last observed period last_X <- Xdyad[[length(Xdyad)]] X_future <- list(last_X, last_X, last_X) fc_cf <- predict(fit, h = 3, type = "response", newdata = X_future)  # counterfactual: shift every dyad's trade covariate up by one unit # going forward. Because `trade` is a standardised (mean-zero) covariate, # an additive +1 shift -- "one standard deviation more trade for every # pair" -- is the interpretable counterfactual; a multiplicative scaling # would be meaningless on a centred covariate (see caution 2 below). X_future_up <- lapply(X_future, function(x) {     x[, , 1] <- x[, , 1] + 1     x }) fc_up <- predict(fit, h = 3, type = "response", newdata = X_future_up)  # delta is the per-dyad change in posterior-mean cooperation probability # at h = 3, attributable solely to the +1 trade shift delta_h3 <- fc_up[[3]] - fc_cf[[3]] summary(as.vector(delta_h3)) #>     Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max.  #> -0.04954  0.04330  0.08606  0.07467  0.10922  0.13965

title: Forecasting Longitudinal Networks with lame
text: predict(fit, h = ...) fires one-per-fit warning upper 97.5% credible bound ρ_β reaches 0.99 (.e. posterior puts non-trivial mass near unit root). mean-reverting fit bound sits around 0.98, warning fire forecasts well-posed. two regimes still qualitatively different forecast-variance behavior, getting distinction right matters picking horizon: AR(1), dynamic_beta_kind = "ar1" |ρ|<1|\rho| < 1: conditional hh-step variance βT+h\beta_{T+h} given training window σβ2⋅(1−ρ2h)/(1−ρ2)\sigma_\beta^2 \cdot (1 - \rho^{2h}) / (1 - \rho^2). saturates (increment hh h+1h+1 shrinks geometrically zero) limit stationary variance σβ2/(1−ρ2)\sigma_\beta^2 / (1 - \rho^2). stationary level blows ρ→1\rho \1, AR(1) still informative long horizons ρ\rho comfortably bounded away 1. RW1, dynamic_beta_kind = "rw1": ρ=1\rho = 1 construction hh-step variance exactly σβ2⋅h\sigma_\beta^2 \cdot h, linear growth hh, never saturating. words: AR(1) eventually forgets training window falls back stationary distribution; RW1 keeps adding innovation variance every period. AR(1) forecasts can stay tight moderate hh ρ\rho small; RW1 forecasts get linearly wider every step regardless. AR(1) stationarity warning fires underlying process genuinely unit-root, refit dynamic_beta_kind = "rw1"; AR(1) posterior ρ\rho near---1 science says “mean-revert eventually,” stick AR(1) cap reported horizon h≈3h \approx 3. note printed warning text. post-v4 fix, cli message branches dynamic_beta_kind: AR(1) reports hh-step forecast variance saturates stationary level σβ2/(1−ρβ2)\sigma_\beta^2 / (1 - \rho_\beta^2) (explodes ρβ→1\rho_\beta \1), RW1 reports hh-step variance grows linearly hh. strictly-correct distinction — AR(1) saturates geometrically, RW1 grows exactly linearly — version cite methods write-. longer-horizon diagnostic, use exact rolling-origin leave-future-CV: nscan = 200, burn = 50, odens = 5 settings inside lfo() chosen vignette builds well minute laptop. give 200 / 5 = 40 stored draws per leave-refit (burn-run discarded, subtracted nscan), enough return sensible point estimate per-period elpd demonstration, tighter use LFO output drives inference. real analyses, pass nscan = 5000, burn = 1000, odens = 25 (whatever matches lame() baseline) verify per-period elpd stable across two independent runs relying . mean-reverting fit two leave-folds return per-period elpd roughly -560 -620 870 scored dyads (-0.65 -0.7 nats/dyad), two folds close – model forecasts held-period well origin 5 origin 6, want see late regime change. elpd instead dropped sharply last fold, empirical signal regime change near end training window; reach detect_change_point(fit) (heuristic Bayes-factor diagnostic) localise .
code: # refit on Y[1:(t-1)] for each t in periods and score Y[[t]]. # Skip the very first leave-out (would leave a 1-period training window # which is too short for dynamic_beta); use the last 2 origins. set.seed(1)  # the internal h=1 forecast draws inside lfo() are stochastic; seed for reproducibility T_fit <- length(fit$YPM) lfo_periods <- tail(seq_len(T_fit), max(1L, min(2L, T_fit - 2L))) lfo_res <- lfo(fit, periods = lfo_periods, refit = TRUE,                nscan = 200, burn = 50, odens = 5, verbose = FALSE) print(lfo_res) #>  #> ── Exact rolling-origin LFO ── #>  #> Periods evaluated: 5 and 6 #> Refit per leave-out: TRUE #> Total elpd_lfo: -1173.8 #>  period      elpd n_obs #>       5 -580.6145   870 #>       6 -593.1495   870

title: Forecasting Longitudinal Networks with lame
text: forecast_pit() complements lfo() asking sharper question: given held-outcomes, h-step posterior-predictive distribution well calibrated? continuous families PIT Gaussian CDF evaluated observation; binary / Poisson / ordinal Czado-Gneiting-Held randomised PIT (Czado, Gneiting & Held 2009). well-calibrated forecast produces PIT values Uniform(0, 1). (fit_train fit five periods, ρβ\rho_\beta posterior sits closer unit root six-period fit internal forecast prints Step-5 unit-root note. note shorter training fit, main model; calibration result still valid.) Read two summaries together. pit$cover_95 – fraction held-cells whose observed outcome falls central 95% posterior-predictive interval, target 0.95 – stable summary; lands 0.93, close nominal, forecast intervals roughly right width. pit$ks_p tests whether full PIT distribution Uniform(0, 1); value 0.05 flags mis-calibration. binary outcome PIT randomised Czado-Gneiting-Held version, single held-period KS statistic noisy – hovers near 0.05 threshold run run (seed chunk). Treat cover_95 headline ks_p corroborating evidence; -read single borderline KS p-value one held-period. sharper test, hold two periods pair plot(pit) pit$pit see whether mis-calibration tails (- vs -dispersion) centre (location bias). sparse, short, near-unit-root panel summaries degrade together – cover_95 drops well 0.95 ks_p collapses toward 0 – calibration-side signature forecast-variance explosion Step 5 warning flags.
code: # fit on the first five periods, hold out the sixth fit_train <- lame(     Y[1:5], Xdyad = Xdyad[1:5],     family = "binary", R = 0,     dynamic_beta = "dyad",     nscan = 400, burn = 100, odens = 5,     verbose = FALSE ) set.seed(1)   # the randomised PIT draws are stochastic; seed for reproducibility pit <- forecast_pit(fit_train, y_future = list(Y[[6]])) #> Warning: ! Posterior 95% interval for `rho_beta` reaches 0.999. #> ℹ Under AR(1) the `h`-step forecast variance saturates at the stationary level #>   `sigma_beta^2 / (1 - rho_beta^2)`, which itself explodes as `rho_beta -> 1`; #>   horizons beyond "3" become uninformative in this regime. #> ℹ Consider refitting with `dynamic_beta_kind = "rw1"` if the underlying process #>   is genuinely unit-root. print(pit) #>  #> ── Forecast PIT calibration check ── #>  #> • Family: "binary" #> • Forecast horizon: 1 #> • Held-out dyads: 870 #> • KS stat vs Uniform(0,1): 0.0393 #> • KS p-value: 0.136 #> • Fraction in [0.025, 0.975]: 0.932 (expect 0.95) #> PIT values are consistent with Uniform(0, 1) at the 5% level.

title: Forecasting Longitudinal Networks with lame
text: two candidate specifications, say static-beta baseline dynamic_beta = "dyad" variant, loo::loo_compare() one-liner ranks --sample predictive performance. fits need save_log_lik = TRUE loo() can read stored pointwise log-likelihood matrix fit$log_lik: eval = FALSE block keeps vignette build fast; printed elpd_diff -8.4 se_diff 3.1 illustrative values expect see scale panel — literally running chunk reproduces. (Passing bare loo() objects rather named list still works labels rows model1 / model2.) read output. top row preferred model (fit_dyn ); elpd_diff always zero . subsequent row reports difference expected log pointwise predictive density relative top model, together standard error difference. example , fit_dyn beats fit_static 8.4 elpd units SE 3.1, |elpd_diff| / se_diff ~ 2.7: dynamic-beta specification preferred roughly “2-sigma” threshold. sociologist writing say “time-varying coefficient model preferred static specification 8 elpd units (SE 3.1), consistent nontrivial drift effect panel window.” |elpd_diff| closer SE — say -2.0 (SE 3.0) — two specifications predictively indistinguishable simpler static model defensible choice. common rule thumb: |elpd_diff| exceeds roughly 2 * se_diff, better-ranked model preferred reasonable confidence; smaller gaps within noise two specifications predictively indistinguishable. family = "binary" one six families exact closed-form log-likelihood (normal, binary, cbin, tobit, poisson, ordinal; see log-lik scale discussion Dynamic Effects vignette), two elpd_loo values response scale absolute numbers directly comparable loo() result probit-link brms fit data, modulo lame Gibbs sampler’s R-hat / ESS satisfactory. rank likelihoods frn rrl, default observed_exact falls back augmented-Z normal approximation valid relative comparison within lame; opt log_lik_method = "observed_ghk" exact marginal two families.
code: fit_static <- lame(     Y, Xdyad = Xdyad, family = "binary", R = 0,     nscan = 2000, burn = 500, odens = 5,     save_log_lik = TRUE,     verbose = FALSE ) fit_dyn <- lame(     Y, Xdyad = Xdyad, family = "binary", R = 0,     dynamic_beta = "dyad",     nscan = 2000, burn = 500, odens = 5,     save_log_lik = TRUE,     verbose = FALSE )  # rank the two fits: the row with elpd_diff = 0 is the best, # subsequent rows show elpd_diff and its SE relative to it. Pass a NAMED # list so the rows are labelled by model name rather than "model1"/"model2". cmp <- loo::loo_compare(list(fit_static = loo::loo(fit_static),                              fit_dyn    = loo::loo(fit_dyn))) cmp #>            elpd_diff se_diff #> fit_dyn     0.0       0.0 #> fit_static -8.4       3.1

title: lame Overview
text: lame package provides tools fitting Longitudinal Additive Multiplicative Effects models network data observed time. study relationships actors (countries trading , legislators co-sponsoring bills, students forming friendships across semesters) repeated observations relationships, lame designed . modeling approach builds Additive Multiplicative Effects (AME) framework developed Peter Hoff, whose amen package (GitHub) provides foundational implementation cross-sectional network analysis. lame package extends framework several directions: Longitudinal replicated data: amen focuses single networks (ame()) basic replicated designs (ame_rep()), lame built ground panel data, networks observed across multiple time periods, potentially actors entering leaving sample. Dynamic effects: Actors’ positions latent space baseline activity levels can evolve time via AR(1) processes (dynamic_uv dynamic_ab), letting model network structure changes rather assuming fixed. C++ acceleration: Core sampling routines implemented Rcpp/RcppArmadillo faster computation, matters many time periods actors. Bipartite networks: Explicit support two-mode networks (e.g., countries treaties, users products) separate latent spaces row column nodes. ggplot2-based diagnostics: Built-visualization functions (trace_plot, gof_plot, ab_plot, uv_plot) return standard ggplot2 figures can theme extend. Standard S3 methods: coef(), confint(), fitted(), residuals(), predict(), simulate() work expect R model object. package part netify-verse, works netify package data preparation.

title: lame Overview
text: amen lame export ame() function. load packages session, lame prints .onAttach startup message know unqualified ame(...) calls dispatch whichever package attached last; use lame::ame(...) amen::ame(...) explicit. table maps breaking differences old amen scripts: common porting fix family = "nrm" → family = "normal" family = "bin" → family = "binary". Everything else either still works unchanged surfaces deprecation warning rather error.

title: lame Overview
text: see lame works practice, analyze Dutch college friendship network (van de Bunt, van Duijn, Snijders 1999), panel 32 students arrived strangers rated ’s friendship seven time points. (drop cold-start first wave, students barely knew almost ties exist; six waves remain.) substantive question whether students sort gender, smoking status, academic program, whether residual clustering remains covariates absorbed. dataset includes: Y: directed friendship ratings -1 4 scale (-1 negative/troubled relationship, 0 uncertain tie, 1-4 increasing friendship). binarize positive rating tie ((Y > 0) * 1), rare -1 ratings fold 0 “tie”. X: three node-level attributes: male, smoker, program. construct three dyadic homophily indicators node attributes: same_male, same_smoker, same_program. friendship network’s average density rises across panel students settle , though trajectory monotone (density bounces roughly 0.40 0.63 across six waves):
code: library(lame) library(ggplot2) set.seed(6886)  data("dutchcollege")  n <- nrow(dutchcollege$Y) T_all <- dim(dutchcollege$Y)[3] actor_names <- sprintf("S%02d", seq_len(n))  # binarize and drop the cold-start wave (t = 1 has almost no ties) Y <- lapply(2:T_all, function(t) {     Yt <- (dutchcollege$Y[, , t] > 0) * 1     diag(Yt) <- NA     rownames(Yt) <- colnames(Yt) <- actor_names     Yt }) names(Y) <- paste0("t", 2:T_all)  # nodal covariates passed identically as sender and receiver X_node <- dutchcollege$X rownames(X_node) <- actor_names Xrow <- lapply(seq_along(Y), function(t) X_node) Xcol <- Xrow  # dyadic homophily indicators same_male    <- outer(X_node[, "male"],    X_node[, "male"],    "==") * 1 same_smoker  <- outer(X_node[, "smoker"],  X_node[, "smoker"],  "==") * 1 same_program <- outer(X_node[, "program"], X_node[, "program"], "==") * 1 Xdyad_one <- array(0, dim = c(n, n, 3),                    dimnames = list(actor_names, actor_names,                                    c("same_male", "same_smoker", "same_program"))) Xdyad_one[, , 1] <- same_male Xdyad_one[, , 2] <- same_smoker Xdyad_one[, , 3] <- same_program Xdyad <- lapply(seq_along(Y), function(t) Xdyad_one) sapply(Y, function(y) round(mean(y, na.rm = TRUE), 2)) #>   t2   t3   t4   t5   t6   t7  #> 0.40 0.52 0.47 0.49 0.63 0.51

title: lame Overview
text: fit binary probit AME model sender random effects (rvar), receiver random effects (cvar), dyadic correlation (dcor), one-dimensional latent space (R = 1). random effects capture sender heterogeneity (students simply nominate friends) receiver heterogeneity (students nominated often), latent space picks residual clustering: students befriend similar people reasons observed covariates capture. use R = 1 32 students two-dimensional latent space competes three dyadic homophily indicators signal chain mixes poorly dyadic coefficients; R = 1 resolves cleanly substantive results nearly identical.
code: fit <- lame(     Y = Y,     Xdyad = Xdyad,           # dyadic homophily indicators     Xrow = Xrow,             # sender covariates     Xcol = Xcol,             # receiver covariates     family = "binary",       # binary probit model     rvar = TRUE,             # sender random effects     cvar = TRUE,             # receiver random effects     dcor = TRUE,             # dyadic correlation (reciprocity)     R = 1,                   # 1-D multiplicative latent space     symmetric = FALSE,       # friendships are directed     burn = 500,              # burn-in     nscan = 4000,            # post-burn-in iterations     odens = 10,              # thinning -> 400 stored draws     verbose = FALSE,     plot = FALSE )

title: lame Overview
text: lot lands , let’s walk . Intercept. probit intercept latent linear predictor every covariate zero every random effect sits mean. average student: covariates uncentered, program particular coded 2-4 never zero, “covariates zero” extrapolates well outside observed data. familiar qnorm(density) rule thumb describes predictor average covariate profile, -zero corner. two reconcile exactly add back mean covariate contribution: intercept strongly negative, predictor average covariate profile (centroid_lp) sits close qnorm(density) – uncentered covariates account nearly entire gap, leaving small shift random-effect variance. intercept absorbs mean covariate contribution; baseline probability. want intercept read directly baseline, center covariates fitting. Either way, read predicted probabilities response scale predict(fit, type = "response"). Homophily. three dyadic coefficients (same_male, same_smoker, same_program) measure whether students type likely friends, holding sender, receiver, latent positions fixed. Positive values mean homophily; credible intervals exclude zero indicate direction association well-identified. Program homophily tends strongest three network, consistent substantive expectation students bond around shared coursework. Nodal covariates. male_row / male_col measure whether male students unusually active senders popular receivers, similarly smoker program. 32 students, credible intervals individual nodal effects wide; treat sign suggestive, magnitude caution. Variance components. va (sender variance) vb (receiver variance) quantify much students differ sociability popularity beyond covariates explain. dyadic correlation rho captures reciprocity: values away zero indicate ties tend mutual. friendship networks rho > 0 rule rather exception.
code: summary(fit) #>  #> === Longitudinal AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> [1] "Y ~ dyad(same_male, same_smoker, same_program) + row(male, smoker, program) + col(male, smoker, program) + a[i] + b[j] + rho*e[ji] + U[i,1:1] %*% V[j,1:1], family = 'binary'" #>  #> Time periods: 6  #> Family: binary  #> Mode: unipartite  #>  #> Note: STATIC fit pooled across 6 time periods -- #>   U, V, a, b are time-invariant; per-period predictions vary #>   only through per-period covariates. For time-varying effects, #>   refit with dynamic_uv = TRUE and/or dynamic_ab = TRUE. #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>                   Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper     #> intercept           -2.869    1.208  -2.376   0.018   -5.241   -0.572   * #> male_row             0.939    0.417   2.251   0.024    0.212    1.761   * #> smoker_row          -0.951     0.34  -2.799   0.005   -1.583   -0.311  ** #> program_row          0.497    0.231   2.153   0.031    0.059    0.947   * #> male_col             0.991    0.267   3.708       0    0.446    1.516 *** #> smoker_col          -0.062    0.217  -0.286   0.775   -0.525    0.341     #> program_col          0.149    0.145   1.028   0.304   -0.111    0.437     #> same_male_dyad       0.446    0.058   7.654       0    0.333    0.566 *** #> same_smoker_dyad     0.143    0.056   2.551   0.011    0.037    0.244   * #> same_program_dyad    0.577    0.053  10.948       0    0.472    0.669 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: stars are a visual hint from posterior mean / SD only; for inference use the credible intervals. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.797    0.217 #> cab    0.291    0.107 #> vb     0.268    0.078 #> rho    0.321    0.042 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) bhat        <- colMeans(fit$BETA) slice_means <- apply(fit$X[[1]], 3, mean, na.rm = TRUE)   # mean of each design column centroid_lp <- sum(bhat * slice_means[names(bhat)])       # predictor at the average profile c(intercept     = unname(bhat["intercept"]),   centroid_lp   = centroid_lp,   qnorm_density = qnorm(mean(unlist(Y), na.rm = TRUE))) #>     intercept   centroid_lp qnorm_density  #>   -2.86919980   -0.08283855    0.00842291

title: lame Overview
text: model estimated via MCMC, trace plots posterior-density plots useful checks. trace_plot function shows sampled values iterations (top panels) corresponding posterior densities (bottom panels) parameter.  Trace plots bounce around stable mean without long-term trends sticky regions, density plots smooth unimodal. Numerically, common MCMC checks split-R̂\hat R < 1.01 monitored parameters bulk / tail ESS ≥\ge 400 per chain. 400 stored samples, regression coefficients mix well seed. variance components mix slowly: summarise_draws table , va carries highest R̂\hat R lowest ESS. parameter mixes worst seed-dependent short chain, treat fit compact worked example rather final diagnostic run.
code: trace_plot(fit, params = "beta")

title: lame Overview
text: lame registers as_draws() methods fit drops straight Stan-era diagnostic ecosystem (posterior, bayesplot, tidybayes). reshape lifts BETA VC blocks draws_array indexed iteration / chain / variable, means posterior::summarise_draws() gives per-parameter posterior mean, median, sd, MAD, 5%/95% quantiles, split-R̂\hat R, bulk / tail ESS one call: single-chain fit like one chain = 1, R̂\hat R reduces within-chain split-R̂\hat R, informative within-chain non-stationarity -chain disagreement. honest R̂\hat R across independently initialised chains, see multi-chain section dynamic-effects vignette.

title: lame Overview
text: gof_plot function compares observed network statistics posterior predictive distributions. tells us whether model can reproduce key structural features data, just dyad-level relationships, emergent properties like much actors vary degree (heterogeneity), whether friends--friends tend friends (transitivity), whether ties tend reciprocated (dyadic dependence).  longitudinal model, gof_plot shows statistics across time. figure encodes three series panel using colour linetype redundantly: observed series solid orange line filled orange points, posterior-predictive median dashed dark line, 95% credible interval grey ribbon (width controlled credible.level argument). observed series falls inside ribbon, model reproducing aspect network; falls outside, model missing something. Falling outside substantive finding data-generating process, failure report. panel-strip headers human-readable labels (Sender Degree Heterogeneity, etc.). fit$GOF named list whose elements sd.rowmean, sd.colmean, dyad.dep, cycle.dep (cyclic triads), trans.dep; user-facing aliases gof_plot(..., statistics = ...) sd.row, sd.col, dyad.dep, triad.dep, trans.dep. Reading figure honestly: Dutch college fit reproduces average level reciprocity cyclic-triad structure -predicts sender degree heterogeneity, transitivity, (panel ends) receiver heterogeneity. observed Sender Degree Heterogeneity series sits upper edge 95% band five six periods (inside band t=1t = 1); students nominate many friends additive sender effect aia_i plus latent uiu_i can absorb. Transitivity -predicted every period – observed value sits upper edge band t=1t = 1 t=6t = 6 – triangle closure systematically low latent space. Receiver Degree Heterogeneity band t=1,2t = 1, 2, inside t=3t = 3 t=5t = 5, band t=4t = 4 t=6t = 6. Triadic Dependence panel (cyclic triads, cycle.dep) sits inside band five six periods (outside t=1t = 1). Dyadic Dependence (reciprocity) variable: band t=1t = 1 55 (marginally t=2t = 2), t=3t = 3 t=6t = 6, inside t=4t = 4. standard signature latent-space model friendship network. AME assumes conditional dyadic independence: given additive multiplicative effects, dyads independent, triangle closure soaked clustering latent positions uiu_i. ERGMs handle triangle closure directly via terms like gwesp triangle. two model classes complementary. ERGM right answer triangle-closure questions; AME right answer analyst wants stable, non-degenerate estimates explicit actor heterogeneity coherent posterior forecast simulate .
code: gof_plot(fit)
