title: lame Overview
text: figure read qualitative. scalar posterior-predictive p-value temporal trend chosen network statistic, gof_temporal() complements gof_plot(). computes chosen statistic observed period, fits OLS slope period index, draws posterior-predictive replicate panels via simulate(fit), recomputes slope replicate, returns two-sided Gelman-style posterior-predictive p-value defined 2 * min(p_up, 1 - p_up), runs 0 (observed slope extreme tail) 1 (observed slope dead-centre). static fit truly trending data yields p_pp near 0 (observed slope unreachable); fit captures trend yields p_pp well away 0 (observed slope central predictive distribution). p_pp 0 density says static fit reproduce empirical net densification slope (+0.025 per period run); expected diagnostic fit without dynamic_beta time-varying covariates consistent per-period mass shift gof_plot(). reciprocity check tells story opposite direction: static fit also rejects observed reciprocity slope (p_pp near 0), reciprocity actually drifts downward across panel (slope −0.03-0.03 per period) static ρ\rho track drift. checks pointing structural mismatch: model parameters constant across periods reproduce period--period drift either marginal quantity. companion diagnostic detect_change_point() reports heuristic tail-ratio score comparing posterior path dynamic_beta coefficient AR(1) prior null. requires fit dynamic_beta active therefore applicable static fit shown ; worked example lives Dynamic Effects vignette.
code: # density rises across the panel on net (the network gets denser as # students settle in, though the trajectory is not monotone), so it is the # natural temporal-trend target. gt_density <- gof_temporal(fit, stat = "density", n_rep = 100, seed = 6886) gt_density   # prints stat, observed slope, n_rep, and the p_pp #>  #> ── Temporal-trend posterior-predictive check ── #>  #> • Statistic: "density" #> • Observed slope (per-period): 0.025 #> • Replicates: 100 #> • Posterior-predictive p-value (two-sided): 0 #> Observed temporal trend is incompatible with the fitted model.  # reciprocity is the natural second target for a directed network. gt_recip <- gof_temporal(fit, stat = "reciprocity", n_rep = 100, seed = 6886) gt_recip #>  #> ── Temporal-trend posterior-predictive check ── #>  #> • Statistic: "reciprocity" #> • Observed slope (per-period): -0.0306 #> • Replicates: 100 #> • Posterior-predictive p-value (two-sided): 0 #> Observed temporal trend is incompatible with the fitted model.

title: lame Overview
text: multiplicative effects capture patterns association go beyond covariates additive effects explain. uv_plot function visualizes latent positions. fit R = 1, latent space one-dimensional: student’s sender position (triangle) receiver position (circle) single number, uv_plot lays along one axis (two reference circles drawn scale). Students land near share friendship-formation patterns covariates additive effects miss; sign separates two latent groups. R = 2 call spread positions across genuine two-dimensional plane.  R = 1 latent space one-dimensional, positions fall along single axis two circles serve reference scale; marker size encodes magnitude. Students extremes – whose sender (triangle) receiver (circle) positions diverge – distinctive friendship-formation patterns covariates additive effects explain . R = 2 call spreads positions across genuine plane.
code: uv_plot(fit) +     ggtitle("Dutch college friendship: multiplicative effects") +     theme_bw() +     theme(         panel.border = element_blank(),         legend.position = "none",         axis.text  = element_blank(),         axis.title = element_blank(),         panel.grid = element_blank()     )

title: lame Overview
text: need estimated latent positions tidy format (custom plots, merging external data, exporting tools), latent_positions() function returns data frame: row gives one actor’s position one latent dimension one time point; type column distinguishes sender position ("U") receiver position ("V"), directed fit returns two rows per actor per dimension. static fit like one positions change time, latent_positions() returns single slice (labelled time = 1) applies every period rather repeating . dynamic models (dynamic_uv = TRUE), positions evolve time latent_positions() returns time point separately (see dynamic effects vignette). using dynamic latent positions, latent space identified rotation time point. procrustes_align() function aligns time period’s positions previous one, removing arbitrary rotations trajectories across time interpretable:
code: lp <- latent_positions(fit) #> ℹ `posterior_sd` is "NA" because U/V samples were not saved. #> ℹ To get posterior SDs, refit with `posterior_opts = posterior_options(save_UV #>   = TRUE)`. #> This message is displayed once per session. head(lp) #>   actor dimension time      value posterior_sd type #> 1   S01         1    1 -1.3214083           NA    U #> 2   S02         1    1 -0.1260811           NA    U #> 3   S03         1    1  0.2875313           NA    U #> 4   S04         1    1  0.3985233           NA    U #> 5   S05         1    1  0.5024891           NA    U #> 6   S06         1    1  0.8535405           NA    U # procrustes alignment is useful for dynamic models. # for a static model, positions are already aligned. aligned <- procrustes_align(fit) #> ℹ Latent positions are static (single time point). No alignment needed. str(aligned$U) #>  num [1:32, 1] -1.321 -0.126 0.288 0.399 0.502 ... #>  - attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2 #>   ..$ : chr [1:32] "S01" "S02" "S03" "S04" ... #>   ..$ : NULL

title: lame Overview
text: lame integrates netify package via netify::to_amen(netlet, lame = TRUE) bridge: feed long-format edgelist (tidy network representation) netify::netify() pass result to_amen() straight lame() ame(). lame = TRUE flag preserves per-period dyadic-covariate axis (required longitudinal models). fit built, tidy(), autoplot(), prediction_draws_long() give broom-/ggplot-/marginaleffects-ready output composes naturally rest tidyverse.

title: lame Overview
code: library(netify)  # 1. tidy edgelist -> netify netlet <- netify::netify(     edges_long,     actor1 = "from", actor2 = "to", time = "year",     weight = "tie", dyad_vars = "distance",     symmetric = FALSE, mode = "unipartite",     output_format = "longit_array" )  # 2. netify -> lame-ready list (lame = TRUE for longitudinal) amen_in <- netify::to_amen(netlet, lame = TRUE)  # 3. fit (same Dutch-college style fit we built above) fit <- lame(     Y      = amen_in$Y,     Xdyad  = amen_in$Xdyad,     Xrow   = amen_in$Xrow,     Xcol   = amen_in$Xcol,     family = "binary", R = 1,     burn = 500, nscan = 4000, odens = 10,     verbose = FALSE )

title: lame Overview
text: rest round-trip runs fit object built Dutch-college data (netify install needed):
code: # tidy(fit) -> term / estimate / std.error / statistic / p.value / # conf.low / conf.high; one row per coefficient (or per # coefficient x period for dynamic_beta fits). The generic ships with # lame so this runs without broom installed; when broom is on the path # `broom::tidy(fit)` dispatches to the same method via # generics::tidy registration. tidy(fit) #> # A tibble: 10 × 7 #>    term              estimate std.error statistic  p.value conf.low conf.high #>    <chr>                <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #>  1 intercept          -2.87      1.21      -2.38  1.75e- 2  -5.24      -0.572 #>  2 male_row            0.939     0.417      2.25  2.44e- 2   0.212      1.76  #>  3 smoker_row         -0.951     0.340     -2.80  5.12e- 3  -1.58      -0.311 #>  4 program_row         0.497     0.231      2.15  3.13e- 2   0.0592     0.947 #>  5 male_col            0.991     0.267      3.71  2.09e- 4   0.446      1.52  #>  6 smoker_col         -0.0620    0.217     -0.286 7.75e- 1  -0.525      0.341 #>  7 program_col         0.149     0.145      1.03  3.04e- 1  -0.111      0.437 #>  8 same_male_dyad      0.446     0.0583     7.65  1.95e-14   0.333      0.566 #>  9 same_smoker_dyad    0.143     0.0561     2.55  1.07e- 2   0.0369     0.244 #> 10 same_program_dyad   0.577     0.0527    10.9   0          0.472      0.669

what: Coefficient tables: glance() + tidy() -> modelsummary / gt / kableExtra
title: lame Overview
text: lame ships tidy.lame() glance.lame(), registered generics::tidy / generics::glance load time, broom-aware table package consumes lame fit directly. compact route modelsummary::modelsummary(fit), uses tidy() coefficient panel glance() goodness--fit footer: Multiple fits compose side--side table way lm() / glm() – pass named list, one column per fit. compare full specification -latent-space variant (R = 0) see much coefficient story soaked multiplicative effects: Reading R = 1 column versus R = 0 column shows latent space can redistribute signal: residual clustering real, homophily coefficients move toward zero AME column structure loaded onto same_program (etc.) instead absorbed latent positions. small panel (32 students, short chains) movement modest uniform across three homophily terms, widely overlapping credible intervals, treat comparison illustrating mechanism rather precise decomposition; direction look , exact shift. Side--side MCMC ALS one table. tidy.ame_als() glance.ame_als() registered alongside MCMC methods, modelsummary(list("MCMC" = fit, "ALS" = fit_als)) works directly. ALS row reports sandwich-based SEs bootstrap attached bootstrap-based SEs ame_als(..., bootstrap = K) used; se_source column tidy(fit_als) records one supplied interval. prefer gt directly, tidy() frame composes formatter; every column follows broom contract kableExtra::kbl(), flextable::flextable(), gt::gt() work without custom shim:  generic dispatches = "uv" (latent positions) = "ab" (sender / receiver random effects), entire random-effects diagnostic suite reachable single broom-style verb:
code: # glance() returns the one-row model summary modelsummary uses for its # lower panel: nobs, n_actors, n_periods, n_stored, family, mode, R, # dynamic_uv / dynamic_ab / dynamic_beta, elpd_loo (NA unless save_log_lik=TRUE). broom::glance(fit) #> # A tibble: 1 × 13 #>    nobs n_actors n_row_actors n_col_actors n_periods n_stored family mode      R #>   <int>    <int>        <int>        <int>     <int>    <int> <chr>  <chr> <int> #> 1  5952       32           NA           NA         6      400 binary unip…     1 #> # ℹ 4 more variables: dynamic_uv <lgl>, dynamic_ab <lgl>, dynamic_beta <lgl>, #> #   elpd_loo <dbl>  # pass the fit straight to modelsummary -- tidy() drives the upper # (coefficients) panel, glance() drives the lower (GOF) panel. modelsummary::modelsummary(     list("Dutch college" = fit),     statistic = "conf.int",     gof_map = c("nobs", "n_actors", "n_periods", "n_stored",                 "family", "R", "dynamic_uv", "dynamic_ab") ) fit_noUV <- lame(     Y = Y, Xdyad = Xdyad, Xrow = Xrow, Xcol = Xcol,     family = "binary", rvar = TRUE, cvar = TRUE, dcor = TRUE,     R = 0,                          # no latent space     symmetric = FALSE,     burn = 500, nscan = 4000, odens = 10,     verbose = FALSE, plot = FALSE )  modelsummary::modelsummary(     list("AME (R = 1)" = fit, "Additive only (R = 0)" = fit_noUV),     statistic = "conf.int",     gof_map = c("nobs", "n_actors", "n_periods", "n_stored",                 "family", "R", "dynamic_uv", "dynamic_ab") ) library(dplyr) library(gt)  broom::tidy(fit) |>     mutate(`Estimate (95% CI)` = sprintf("%.3f (%.3f, %.3f)",                                          estimate, conf.low, conf.high)) |>     select(term, `Estimate (95% CI)`, p.value) |>     gt::gt() |>     gt::fmt_number(columns = p.value, decimals = 3) |>     gt::tab_header(title = "Posterior coefficient summary",                    subtitle = "lame::lame() fit, Dutch college panel") # autoplot.lame returns a horizontal coefplot for static fits and a # ribbon-per-period for dynamic_beta fits, so the same call works for # both. ggplot layers compose on top. autoplot(fit) +     ggtitle("Dutch college friendship: posterior coefficients") # which = "uv": delegates to uv_plot(); trajectory for dynamic_uv, # snapshot for static -- returns a ggplot in both cases. autoplot(fit, which = "uv") # which = "ab": delegates to ab_plot() (sender side by default); # call ab_plot(fit, effect = "receiver") for the column-side plot. autoplot(fit, which = "ab") # prediction_draws_long() returns a long-format data frame with # .chain / .iteration / .draw / period / period_label / i / j / # actor_i / actor_j / .value -- ready for tidybayes / marginaleffects. pdl <- prediction_draws_long(fit, type = "response", n_draws = 50) # the diagonal (i == j) is a self-tie, undefined in a friendship network, but # the linear predictor is still numerically defined there -- drop it so the # per-dyad summaries below cover only real (off-diagonal) pairs. pdl <- pdl[pdl$i != pdl$j, ] head(pdl, 3) #> # A tibble: 3 × 10 #>   .chain .iteration .draw period period_label     i     j actor_i actor_j #>    <int>      <int> <int>  <int> <chr>        <int> <int> <chr>   <chr>   #> 1      1          1     1      1 t2               2     1 S02     S01     #> 2      1          1     1      1 t2               3     1 S03     S01     #> 3      1          1     1      1 t2               4     1 S04     S01     #> # ℹ 1 more variable: .value <dbl> dim(pdl) #> [1] 297600     10

title: lame Overview
text: prediction_draws_long() already uses .draw / .value column convention, can pipe straight tidybayes::spread_draws()- style workflow, , since columns already right shape, go directly dplyr per-dyad posterior summaries: .chain / .iteration / .draw / .value columns already follow tidybayes / marginaleffects convention, data frame composes directly ggdist::stat_pointinterval(), tidybayes::point_interval(), marginaleffects::posterior_draws() without intermediate spread_draws() call (generic dispatches draws / brms / Stan model objects, long data frame – point returning long shape directly skip step).
code: library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union pdl |>     group_by(actor_i, actor_j, period_label) |>     summarise(p_hat = mean(.value),               lo    = quantile(.value, 0.025),               hi    = quantile(.value, 0.975),               .groups = "drop") |>     head(3) #> # A tibble: 3 × 6 #>   actor_i actor_j period_label  p_hat            lo    hi #>   <chr>   <chr>   <chr>         <dbl>         <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 S01     S02     t2           0.0199 0.00000000237 0.138 #> 2 S01     S02     t3           0.0199 0.00000000237 0.138 #> 3 S01     S02     t4           0.0199 0.00000000237 0.138

what: Model comparison: loo::loo_compare() as a one-liner
title: lame Overview
text: two specifications table – say static-coefficient baseline dynamic_beta = "dyad" variant – standard --sample yardstick expected log pointwise predictive density (elpd_loo) returned loo::loo(). fits need save_log_lik = TRUE loo() can read pointwise log-likelihood matrix fit$log_lik; loo::loo_compare() ranks single call: read output. top row preferred model always carries elpd_diff = 0. subsequent row reports difference expected log pointwise predictive density relative top model, together standard error difference. printout , fit_dyn beats fit_static 8.4 elpd units SE 3.1, |elpd_diff|/se_diff≈2.7|\text{elpd\_diff}| / \text{se\_diff} \approx 2.7 – dynamic-beta specification preferred roughly “2-sigma” threshold. natural way phrase comparison: “time-varying-coefficient model preferred static specification 8.4 elpd units (SE 3.1) leave-one-cross-validation, |elpd_diff|/SE ratio 2.7. consistent non-trivial drift dyadic coefficient panel window; static specification rejected conventional thresholds.” |elpd_diff| closer SE (say -2.0 (SE 3.0)), two specifications predictively indistinguishable simpler static model defensible choice. numbers printout illustrative; preferred model gap size depend data, chain length, well AME captures structural features network. loo() run short Gibbs chain (kind replicator typically uses iterating specification) also commonly flag percent dyads Pareto k̂>0.7\hat k > 0.7; treat warning sign importance-sampling approximation straining either lengthen chain apply loo::loo_moment_match() relying elpd_diff inference. family = "binary" one six families exact closed-form pointwise log-likelihood (normal, binary, cbin, tobit, poisson, ordinal), absolute elpd_loo values directly comparable loo() result probit-link brms fit data, modulo R̂\hat R ESS satisfactory lame Gibbs sampler. frn rrl, see log_lik_method = "observed_ghk" caveat Dynamic Effects.
code: fit_static <- lame(Y, Xdyad = Xdyad, family = "binary", R = 2,                    burn = 500, nscan = 4000, odens = 10,                    save_log_lik = TRUE, verbose = FALSE) fit_dyn    <- lame(Y, Xdyad = Xdyad, family = "binary", R = 2,                    dynamic_beta = "dyad",                    burn = 500, nscan = 4000, odens = 10,                    save_log_lik = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)  cmp <- loo::loo_compare(loo::loo(fit_static), loo::loo(fit_dyn)) cmp #>            elpd_diff se_diff #> fit_dyn     0.0       0.0 #> fit_static -8.4       3.1

title: lame Overview
text: vignette covered core workflow: fitting, convergence, GOF, visualization. specialized topics: Single networks (time series)? See First AME Model detailed cross-sectional walkthrough Two types nodes? See Bipartite Networks Evolving network structure? See Dynamic Effects Just want quick version? title: Getting Started with lame
text: Networks trade, conflict, alliance, friendship, sanction share common inferential difficulty: tie two actors rarely independent ties around . central actor sends , attracts , shifts incentives every actor sharing neighbour. lame package fits Longitudinal Additive Multiplicative Effects models kind data. estimates covariate effects directed tie scale accounting sender heterogeneity, receiver heterogeneity, reciprocity, residual relational structure covariates alone capture. model decomposes tie three pieces. Every actor tendency send ties (aia_i) receive ties (bjb_j), reflecting active popular . actor also occupies latent position (uiu_i sender, vjv_j receiver) actors similar positions tend form similar tie patterns. Covariates shift baseline probability ties top actor-level pieces. observations span multiple periods, components can evolve time via AR(1) process – short “autoregressive order 1”, meaning period’s value slightly noisy copy previous period’s value (positions drift smoothly rather jumping around).

what: Coming from a tidy edgelist? Use netify::to_amen(lame = TRUE)
title: Getting Started with lame
text: data already lives long-format edgelist (one row per sender / receiver / period), cleanest way lame via netify package, lame authors maintain part netify-verse. netify::to_amen() bridge produces exact list shape (Y, Xdyad, Xrow, Xcol) lame() ame() consume, lame = TRUE preserving per-period dyadic-covariate axis required longitudinal models:
code: library(netify) library(lame)  # 1. tidy edgelist -> netify object (one row per sender x receiver x year) netlet <- netify::netify(     edges_long,     actor1 = "from", actor2 = "to", time = "year",     weight = "tie", dyad_vars = "distance",     symmetric = FALSE, mode = "unipartite",     output_format = "longit_array" )  # 2. netify -> lame-ready list (lame = TRUE keeps the per-period Xdyad axis) amen_in <- netify::to_amen(netlet, lame = TRUE)  # 3. drop straight into lame() fit <- lame(     Y     = amen_in$Y,     Xdyad = amen_in$Xdyad,     Xrow  = amen_in$Xrow,     Xcol  = amen_in$Xcol,     family = "binary", R = 2,     burn = 500, nscan = 4000, odens = 10,     verbose = FALSE )

previous_headings: Coming from a tidy edgelist? Use netify::to_amen(lame = TRUE)
what: Pure base-R long-format CSV -> 3-D array (no netify dependency)
title: Getting Started with lame
text: rather pull netify, long-format edgelist can reshaped Y / Xdyad lists lame() expects base R . pattern copy-pasteable: write tiny CSV tempfile, read back, pivot per-period arrays. reshape block fully self-contained evaluates can inspect resulting list shapes; lame() call bottom held vignette builds quickly. Using netify required. Anyone can build list square matrices, optional 3-D covariate arrays, fine. netify::to_amen() bridge path least resistance tidy data frame, base-R recipe shows underlying shape contract users prefer assemble inputs . want worked end--end example without writing CSV, data("YX_bin_long") ships 50-actor, 4-period synthetic panel [50, 50, 4] YY array plus [50, 50, 3, 4] XX array. Despite dataset name, shipped YY holds latent probit-scale predictor rather 0/1 ties (range(YX_bin_long$Y, na.rm = TRUE) spans roughly −24-24 2020; bare range() returns NA diagonal NA); threshold zero recover binary tie indicator slice list--matrices shape lame() consumes: broom / ggplot2 / tidybayes round-trip resulting fit demonstrated overview vignette.
code: set.seed(6886) # write a tiny long-format CSV: one row per (sender, receiver, year) edges_long <- data.frame(     from     = rep(c("A", "B", "C", "D"), each = 8),     to       = rep(rep(c("A", "B", "C", "D"), each = 2), 4),     year     = rep(2020:2021, 16),     tie      = sample(0:1, 32, replace = TRUE),     distance = round(runif(32), 2) ) csv_path <- tempfile(fileext = ".csv") write.csv(edges_long, csv_path, row.names = FALSE)  # read back and reshape to a list-of-matrices keyed by year dat       <- read.csv(csv_path, stringsAsFactors = FALSE) actors    <- sort(unique(c(dat$from, dat$to))) periods   <- sort(unique(dat$year)) n         <- length(actors)  empty_mat <- function() {     matrix(NA_real_, n, n, dimnames = list(actors, actors)) }  Y_list <- lapply(periods, function(yr) {     m  <- empty_mat()     d  <- dat[dat$year == yr, , drop = FALSE]     # vectorised fill: pair (from, to) -> tie     idx <- cbind(match(d$from, actors), match(d$to, actors))     m[idx] <- d$tie     diag(m) <- NA  # self-ties undefined in a unipartite network     m }) names(Y_list) <- as.character(periods)  # xdyad: one [n, n, 1] array per period, third-dim name = covariate name Xdyad_list <- lapply(periods, function(yr) {     m  <- matrix(0, n, n, dimnames = list(actors, actors))     d  <- dat[dat$year == yr, , drop = FALSE]     idx <- cbind(match(d$from, actors), match(d$to, actors))     m[idx] <- d$distance     array(m, dim = c(n, n, 1),           dimnames = list(actors, actors, "distance")) }) names(Xdyad_list) <- as.character(periods)  # inspect the shape contract lame() consumes str(Y_list, max.level = 1) #> List of 2 #>  $ 2020: num [1:4, 1:4] NA 1 1 1 1 NA 1 1 1 1 ... #>   ..- attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2 #>  $ 2021: num [1:4, 1:4] NA 1 1 0 1 NA 0 0 0 1 ... #>   ..- attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2 str(Xdyad_list[[1]]) #>  num [1:4, 1:4, 1] 0.14 0.54 0.05 0.64 0.6 0.62 0.21 0.37 0.96 0.37 ... #>  - attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 3 #>   ..$ : chr [1:4] "A" "B" "C" "D" #>   ..$ : chr [1:4] "A" "B" "C" "D" #>   ..$ : chr "distance" # the fit itself is identical to any other lame() call; this chunk is # held out only so the vignette builds quickly. with the toy 4-actor # data above, R = 0 and a small chain just confirms the pipe runs # end-to-end; for substantive inference use your own larger panel. fit <- lame(     Y      = Y_list,     Xdyad  = Xdyad_list,     family = "binary", R = 0,     burn = 200, nscan = 2000, odens = 10,     verbose = FALSE ) data("YX_bin_long") Y_bin <- lapply(seq_len(dim(YX_bin_long$Y)[3]), function(t) {     Yt <- (YX_bin_long$Y[, , t] > 0) * 1   # threshold latent z to 0/1     diag(Yt) <- NA     Yt })

title: Getting Started with lame
text: Let’s fit model small longitudinal binary network one dyadic covariate (bilateral similarity score drives tie formation). truth intercept = -1.0 covariate effect beta = 0.7. intercept lands near true -1.0 similarity_dyad lands near true 0.7, basic sanity check sampler recovering known parameters. _dyad suffix coefficient name flags covariate pair-level quantity; sender-receiver-covariates carry _row _col suffixes respectively. remaining variance components describe residual actor-level dyad-level structure covariate alone explain: va: variance sender random effects, capturing much actors differ overall tendency send ties. vb: variance receiver random effects, capturing much differ tendency receive. cab: covariance sender receiver effects within actor, indicating whether prolific senders also frequent receivers. rho: within-dyad residual correlation, capturing reciprocity beyond additive effects covariates explain. note MCMC settings. three key parameters burn (iterations discarded burn-), nscan (post-burn-iterations kept inference), odens (thinning: keep every odens-th sample reduce autocorrelation). settings , store nscan / odens = 1000 / 10 = 100 posterior samples (kept small vignette build time). final run, aim least 1000 stored samples adequate effective sample sizes (e.g. burn = 1000, nscan = 25000, odens = 25). note actor naming. lame() sorts actors alphabetically name align across time periods covariate arrays. names sort different order appear positionally (e.g. "N1", "N10", "N11", ..., "N2"), pass Xdyad / Xrow / Xcol matching dimnames package can align , use names sort correctly (zero-padded like "N01" ... "N25", non-numeric like "Alice", "Bob"). package inherit Y’s names onto unnamed covariate arrays can, explicit dimnames safer.
code: library(lame) set.seed(6886)  # simulate 3 time periods of a 25-node directed network with a # dyadic similarity covariate driving the ties. n <- 25; T_periods <- 3 true_intercept <- -1.0 true_beta      <- 0.7  # zero-padded names sort the same way alphabetically as positionally, # so `lame()`'s internal alphabetic actor sort leaves the row order # actor order can change with names like "N1","N10","N2",... # actors (N1, N10, N2, ...); the fit is still correct as long as Y and # the X arrays share the same names, but the stored output comes back in # the sorted order, not your input order. actor_names <- sprintf("N%02d", seq_len(n))  X_list <- lapply(seq_len(T_periods), function(t) {     # 3-D array [actor x actor x covariate]; the third-dim name becomes     # the coefficient label downstream     x <- matrix(rnorm(n * n), n, n)     array(x, dim = c(n, n, 1),           dimnames = list(actor_names, actor_names, "similarity")) }) Y_list <- lapply(seq_len(T_periods), function(t) {     # build the linear predictor eta = intercept + beta * X (the same     # arithmetic a logistic / probit regression would do). then push it     # through pnorm() -- the standard-normal CDF -- to turn it into a     # tie probability in [0, 1]. that CDF link is what "probit" means,     # and it is the link `family = "binary"` uses inside `lame()`. finally,     # draw a 0/1 tie from a Bernoulli with that probability.     #     # the `[, , 1]` peels the first (and only) covariate slice off the     # 3-D array, leaving an n x n matrix. third-dim index = covariate.     eta <- true_intercept + true_beta * X_list[[t]][, , 1]     Y   <- matrix(rbinom(n * n, 1, pnorm(eta)), n, n)     diag(Y) <- NA   # self-ties are undefined in a unipartite network     rownames(Y) <- colnames(Y) <- actor_names     Y })  # fit a longitudinal AME model with the dyadic similarity covariate. fit <- lame(     Y = Y_list,     Xdyad = X_list,         # one similarity matrix per period     R = 2,                  # 2D latent space     family = "binary",      # probit for 0/1 networks     burn = 100,             # burn-in (use 1000+ for real work)     nscan = 1000,           # post-burn-in samples (use 5000+ for real work)     odens = 10,             # thinning     verbose = FALSE,        # suppress the progress bar / iteration log     plot = FALSE            # don't pop up live MCMC diagnostic plots during sampling                             # `lame()` draws live diagnostics when plot = TRUE;                             #  `ame()` accepts plot = for signature parity but ignores it                             #  -- the single-period sampler has no live plotting) )  summary(fit) #>  #> === Longitudinal AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> [1] "Y ~ dyad(similarity) + a[i] + b[j] + rho*e[ji] + U[i,1:2] %*% V[j,1:2], family = 'binary'" #>  #> Time periods: 3  #> Family: binary  #> Mode: unipartite  #>  #> Note: STATIC fit pooled across 3 time periods -- #>   U, V, a, b are time-invariant; per-period predictions vary #>   only through per-period covariates. For time-varying effects, #>   refit with dynamic_uv = TRUE and/or dynamic_ab = TRUE. #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>                 Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper     #> intercept         -1.107    0.086   -12.8       0   -1.245   -0.915 *** #> similarity_dyad    0.755     0.05  15.071       0    0.654    0.841 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: stars are a visual hint from posterior mean / SD only; for inference use the credible intervals. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.080    0.028 #> cab   -0.004    0.019 #> vb     0.074    0.022 #> rho   -0.135    0.093 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance)

what: What Can You Do with a Fitted Model?
title: Getting Started with lame
text: lame objects work standard R methods ’d expect: two regression coefficients (intercept similarity_dyad) land close simulation truth. Predicted probabilities span wide range dyads high similarity intercept-plus-effect linear predictor much larger dyads low similarity. Use confint(fit) 95% credible intervals coefficients. type = "response" gives predictions back natural scale outcome (probabilities family = "binary", expected counts "poisson", expected values "normal"). type = "link" gives underlying linear predictor (probit-scale value Φ(⋅)\Phi(\cdot) transform binary), want plan compute marginal effects hand. h-step-ahead forecasts longitudinal fit, use predict(fit, h = K) (see forecasting vignette).
code: # regression coefficients (posterior means) coef(fit) #>       intercept similarity_dyad  #>      -1.1065454       0.7550219  # 95% credible intervals confint(fit) #>                       2.5%      97.5% #> intercept       -1.2452112 -0.9150417 #> similarity_dyad  0.6544237  0.8412236  # broom-style one-row-per-coefficient frame; ships with lame so it works # without broom installed and dispatches through broom::tidy(fit) when # broom is loaded. glance(fit) gives the one-row model summary that # modelsummary uses for its lower panel. See the overview vignette for # the full modelsummary / tidybayes / autoplot round-trip. tidy(fit) #> # A tibble: 2 × 7 #>   term            estimate std.error statistic p.value conf.low conf.high #>   <chr>              <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 intercept         -1.11     0.0865     -12.8       0   -1.25     -0.915 #> 2 similarity_dyad    0.755    0.0501      15.1       0    0.654     0.841 glance(fit) #> # A tibble: 1 × 13 #>    nobs n_actors n_row_actors n_col_actors n_periods n_stored family mode      R #>   <int>    <int>        <int>        <int>     <int>    <int> <chr>  <chr> <int> #> 1  1800       25           NA           NA         3      100 binary unip…     2 #> # ℹ 4 more variables: dynamic_uv <lgl>, dynamic_ab <lgl>, dynamic_beta <lgl>, #> #   elpd_loo <dbl>  # predicted probabilities for every dyad at every time point. # for a `lame()` fit (panel data), `predict()` returns a *list of length T*, # where T is the number of time periods. Each element is an n x n matrix of # posterior-mean predicted tie probabilities (between 0 and 1, since # family = "binary"). For a single-period `ame()` fit, predict() returns # the n x n matrix directly, not wrapped in a list. Y_hat <- predict(fit, type = "response") length(Y_hat)                      # 3, one matrix per period #> [1] 3 dim(Y_hat[[1]])                    # 25 x 25 #> [1] 25 25 cat("Predicted probability range:",         round(range(unlist(Y_hat), na.rm = TRUE), 3), "\n") #> Predicted probability range: 0 0.97  # residuals: same list-of-matrices shape, observed minus predicted resid_list <- residuals(fit) cat("Residual SD:", round(sd(unlist(resid_list), na.rm = TRUE), 3), "\n") #> Residual SD: 0.345

title: Getting Started with lame
text: Two diagnostics matter AME fit. first whether MCMC sampler explored posterior adequately. trace plots mix freely around stable mean, without trends stuck regions, marginal densities unimodal.  fit two regression parameters, intercept similarity_dyad, mix around simulation truths −1.0-1.0 0.70.7. second diagnostic whether model reproduces structural features observed network. Posterior-predictive goodness--fit plots simulate networks fitted model compare structural statistics observed data. observed series dual-encoded Okabe-Ito orange (#D55E00) solid line points; posterior-predictive median grey-dashed 95% credible interval grey ribbon. colour-plus-linetype encoding stays legible greyscale colour-blind readers.  Panel titles use human-readable names (Sender Degree Heterogeneity, Transitivity, ) rather underlying column codes sd.rowmean trans.dep; internal codes fit$GOF returns directly. generated data purely eta = -1 + 0.7 * X (planted degree heterogeneity, reciprocity, clustering), model reproduces almost every structural statistic: across five statistics three periods, fourteen fifteen observed values sit inside 95% posterior-predictive bands. lone marginal excursion (transitivity one period) sits just past band edge statistic whose values near zero, .e. Monte-Carlo noise rather structural misfit. real friendship trade data picture different: least one panel, typically Transitivity Sender Degree Heterogeneity, drifts clearly outside band. misfit informative: tells analyst higher-order features AME absorbs actor latent structure remain unexplained. substantive finding data, grounds discard fit.
code: trace_plot(fit, params = "beta") gof_plot(fit)

title: Getting Started with lame
text: single network (time series), use ame() instead lame(): output methods . difference lame() pools information across time periods, giving precise estimates structure stable.
code: fit_cs <- ame(     Y = Y_list[[1]],         # just one time period     Xdyad = X_list[[1]],     # 3-D array [n, n, 1] with "similarity" slice name     R = 2,     family = "binary",     burn = 100,     nscan = 1000,     odens = 10,     verbose = FALSE )  coef(fit_cs) #>       intercept similarity_dyad  #>      -1.1772019       0.7421418

title: Getting Started with lame
text: believe network structure changing time (alliances shifting, friendships evolving), can let latent positions additive effects drift time. drift modelled AR(1) process: period’s value noisy copy previous period’s value, persistence parameter ρ∈(−1,1)\rho \(-1, 1) controlling strongly past predicts present (ρ\rho near 1 = slow drift, ρ\rho near 0 = period almost independent). model estimates persistent latent positions (ρuv\rho_{uv}) persistent additive effects (ρab\rho_{ab}). Values near 1 indicate slow evolution; values near 0 indicate near-independent positions across periods. simulated data use single latent structure across three periods, varying dyadic similarity covariate, persistence estimates reflect mix prior whatever stable-position signal chain extracts three time points. dynamic effects vignette provides longer-panel example temporal evolution genuinely present simulation persistence parameters informative.
code: fit_dyn <- lame(     Y = Y_list,     Xdyad = X_list,     R = 2,     dynamic_ab = TRUE,    # time-varying sociality/popularity     dynamic_uv = TRUE,    # time-varying latent positions     family = "binary",     burn = 100,     nscan = 1000,     odens = 10,     verbose = FALSE,     plot = FALSE )  summary(fit_dyn) #>  #> === Longitudinal AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> [1] "Y ~ dyad(similarity) + a[i] + b[j] + rho*e[ji] + U[i,1:2] %*% V[j,1:2], family = 'binary'" #>  #> Time periods: 3  #> Family: binary  #> Mode: unipartite  #> Dynamic latent positions: enabled (rho_uv = 0.685 ) #> Dynamic additive effects: enabled (rho_ab = 0.48 ) #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>                 Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper     #> intercept         -1.096    0.196  -5.591       0   -1.489   -0.711 *** #> similarity_dyad    0.745    0.048  15.563       0    0.656    0.826 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: stars are a visual hint from posterior mean / SD only; for inference use the credible intervals. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.104    0.063 #> cab    0.041    0.061 #> vb     0.108    0.061 #> rho    0.204    0.157 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance)

title: Getting Started with lame
text: two-mode networks (students courses, countries treaties), pass rectangular matrix set mode = "bipartite". simulate 15 row × 10 column network dyadic similarity score drives ties. Notice bipartite summary reports cab = 0.000 rho = 0.000. two parameters describe relationships sender receiver tie, makes sense set actors can play roles. bipartite network rows columns different kinds entities (rows = students, columns = courses; rows = donors, columns = candidates), row actor never also column actor. within-actor sender / receiver covariance reciprocity →ji \j j→ij \estimate, package fixes zero. similarity_dyad coefficient land near simulation truth 0.6 (seed point estimate 0.66, little truth, 95% interval comfortably covers 0.6). bipartite vignette walks full workflow.
code: set.seed(42)          # seed the data simulation so the recovery is reproducible nA <- 15; nB <- 10 row_names <- sprintf("R%02d", seq_len(nA)) col_names <- sprintf("C%02d", seq_len(nB)) X_bip <- lapply(1:3, function(t) {     x <- matrix(rnorm(nA * nB), nA, nB)     array(x, dim = c(nA, nB, 1),           dimnames = list(row_names, col_names, "similarity")) }) Y_bip <- lapply(1:3, function(t) {     eta <- -0.8 + 0.6 * X_bip[[t]][, , 1]     Y   <- matrix(rbinom(nA * nB, 1, pnorm(eta)), nA, nB)     rownames(Y) <- row_names; colnames(Y) <- col_names     Y })  fit_bip <- lame(     Y = Y_bip,     Xdyad = X_bip,     mode = "bipartite",     R = 2,     family = "binary",     burn = 100, nscan = 1000, odens = 10,     verbose = FALSE, plot = FALSE )  summary(fit_bip) #>  #> === Longitudinal AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> [1] "Y ~ dyad(similarity) + a[i] + b[j] + U[i,1:2] %*% G %*% V[j,1:2]', family = 'binary'" #>  #> Time periods: 3  #> Family: binary  #> Mode: bipartite  #>  #> Note: STATIC fit pooled across 3 time periods -- #>   U, V, a, b are time-invariant; per-period predictions vary #>   only through per-period covariates. For time-varying effects, #>   refit with dynamic_uv = TRUE and/or dynamic_ab = TRUE. #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>                 Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper     #> intercept         -0.896    0.382  -2.346   0.019   -1.587   -0.246   * #> similarity_dyad    0.655    0.092   7.099       0    0.485    0.834 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: stars are a visual hint from posterior mean / SD only; for inference use the credible intervals. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.438    0.136 #> cab    0.000    0.000 #> vb     0.563    0.210 #> rho    0.000    0.000 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) #>   Note: bipartite model (rho fixed to 0, cab fixed to 0)

title: Getting Started with lame
text: Five features show advanced workflows rarely first model. 1. R > n/3 warning. ame() lame() warn latent-space rank R exceeds floor(n/3) (floor(min(nA, nB)/3) bipartite mode). Past cap, multiplicative effects start absorbing structure belongs additive effects, latent positions stop interpretable. warning advisory rather error, users substantive reason push higher can override . practice R = 2 R = 3 right default. 2. Long runs may finish: max_seconds + checkpoint_path + resume_from. fit shared machine job queue wall-clock limit, pass max_seconds = S checkpoint_path = "X.rds" lame(). sampler writes checkpoint every checkpoint_every = 100L iterations (override per fit) terminates cleanly wall clock hits S, leaving fit$terminated_early = TRUE. Continue saved call passing resume_from = "X.rds" lame(): continuation reseeds RNG checkpoint re-invokes lame() original call arguments (burn = 0 continuation re-pay burn-). chain restarts default prior-driven start values rather checkpoint’s posterior means, give short re-equilibration period using continuation samples part chain. (Inspect fit2$BETA[1:50, ] see warm-tail.) Two caveats iterative resume cycles. First, saved checkpoint_path max_seconds values stripped re-evaluating saved call; pass new values resume call continuation also checkpoint stop wall-clock budget. Second, continuation runs burn = 0; verbose = TRUE works resume call (shows sampling progress bar without burn-period). 3. K-panel joint posterior: lame_multi(). K parallel networks (K country panels, K experimental conditions, K schools) want one shared coefficient posterior, fit panel independently lame() pool per-panel beta posteriors precision-weighted shared posterior: exact conditional independence panels given beta, , panels share covariate effects panel-specific actor effects, latent positions, variance components. single joint MCMC across panels (full hierarchical shrinkage) requires sampler refactor; lame_multi() practical wrapper standard assumption. 4. Memory-conscious loo(): save_log_lik = "chunked". LOO WAIC long runs, save_log_lik = TRUE stores [n_stored, n_obs] log-likelihood matrix RAM. matrix exceed memory, save_log_lik = "chunked" streams per-column-chunk binary files (named loglik_chunk_001.bin, loglik_chunk_002.bin, …) directory log_lik_path (default: fresh subdirectory tempdir()), loo(fit) reads back transparently. -disk layout row-major within chunk readback one readBin per chunk. Override per-file column width log_lik_chunk_size (default 10000L). See vignettes/dynamic_effects.Rmd worked example. 5. Multi-chain diagnostics: lame_parallel() rhat_dynamic_beta(). Single-chain R̂\hat R within-chain split statistic . honest -chain R̂\hat R, run K chains different seeds via lame_parallel(..., n_chains = 4, combine_method = "list"), call rhat_dynamic_beta() returned list get multivariate (Brooks-Gelman) R̂\hat R length-T coefficient path max univariate split-R̂\hat R T per-period scalars. lame_parallel() (thin wrapper around ame_parallel() fitter = "lame") warn clamp cores ask machine’s logical-core count, common silent foot-gun shared workers. pooled-chain fit (combine_method = "pool") also routes posterior::as_draws() posterior::summarise_draws() gives per-coef[t] ess_bulk / ess_tail. full demo lives vignettes/dynamic_effects.Rmd. 6. Held-predictive scoring: evaluate_heldout(). honest predictive validation, mask random subset dyads NA Y, refit (sampler imputes NA cells via data augmentation), score masked cells evaluate_heldout(y_obs, y_pred, mask, family = "binary"). helper picks right rule family: AUROC / PR-AUC / Brier / log-loss binary / cbin, RMSE / MAE / mean log-density normal / tobit, RMSE + Poisson mean log-density poisson. AUROC / PR-AUC require (AUROC alone via ); optional helper degrades gracefully neither installed. See cross-sectional vignette worked end--end example.
code: ck <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds") fit1 <- lame(     Y = Y_list, Xdyad = X_list, R = 2, family = "binary",     nscan = 5000, burn = 200, odens = 25,     max_seconds = 30,          # stop after 30 s wall clock     checkpoint_path = ck,      # write state here     checkpoint_every = 100L,     verbose = FALSE ) if (isTRUE(fit1$terminated_early)) {     # consolidated entry point; `nscan` on the resume call is     # treated as `nscan_more` (additional stored draws).     fit2 <- lame(resume_from = ck, nscan = 2000)         # same helper form         # fit2 <- lame_resume(ck, nscan_more = 2000)     } # each y_panel* is itself a list or 3-D array of per-panel networks # -- i.e. the input to lame() for that panel on its own. Likewise each # x_panel* is a list of dyadic covariate arrays. passing a bare matrix # per panel will error from lame(). fit_multi <- lame_multi(     Y_list     = list(Y_panel1, Y_panel2, Y_panel3),     Xdyad_list = list(X_panel1, X_panel2, X_panel3),     family = "binary", R = 2,     nscan = 2000, burn = 200, odens = 20,     verbose = FALSE ) str(fit_multi$beta_shared)        # pooled beta mean (per-period if dynamic) fit_multi$beta_deviations         # per-panel deviations from shared mean

what: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: Longitudinal Additive Multiplicative Effects Models Networks Trade flows, friendship nominations, alliance ties, sanctions examples network data: observation tie two actors (countries, people, organisations), independent unit. non-independence breaks assumptions standard regression makes. actors send lot ties, receive lot, ties tend reciprocated, actors similar attributes tend behave similarly. ignore patterns just run logistic regression every (sender, receiver) pair, coefficients standard errors . lame package fits Longitudinal Additive Multiplicative Effects models account structure. Concretely, model gives every actor two random effects (sender effect aia_i active receiver effect bjb_j popular ), plus position (ui,vj)(u_i, v_j) low-dimensional latent space captures patterns covariates , homophily (“similar actors tie ”) transitivity (“friends friends friends”). Covariates enter additively, just like regression. “longitudinal” piece lets pieces drift across time periods. package builds directly Peter Hoff’s amen extends four directions: (1) lame() fits panel networks (actors observed multiple time points) time-varying effects; (2) supports bipartite (rectangular) networks — countries--international-organisations (states belong IGOs) — alongside familiar unipartite (square) case; (3) MCMC sampler written C++ (via Rcpp / RcppArmadillo) scales network sizes typical political-science applications; (4) fitted object exposes usual coef(), vcov(), confint(), predict(), fitted(), residuals(), simulate() methods, plugs rest R modelling ecosystem way lm() glm() . Two ways estimate model. Alongside Bayesian MCMC sampler, lame ships fast, MCMC-free point estimator, ame_als() / lame_als(), adapted Social Influence Regression (SIR) estimator Minhas & Hoff (2025). fits additive--multiplicative structure iterative block coordinate descent (alternating least squares / IRLS) normal, binary, Poisson families, parametric-bootstrap sandwich standard errors usual S3 methods. Use data exploration, model screening, starting values, large networks; use MCMC path (ame() / lame()) need full posterior rank/censored families. longitudinal normal, binary, Poisson panels, including named panels actors enter exit, lame(..., method = "als") dynamic point-estimation path time-varying additive effects, selected regression coefficients, AR(1) latent factors directed, symmetric, bipartite panels, Student-t latent-factor drift directed, symmetric, bipartite panels, bipartite dynamic G. normal unipartite bipartite snap-shift panels, lame_snap_als() gives fast ALS snap score using sequential alignment lower-tail drift updates default. terms like probit, latent space, AR(1) new , vignette("lame") walk-introduces one context first fit. Five estimators form core public API. ame() lame() fit cross-sectional longitudinal networks via Bayesian MCMC; lame() additionally adds dynamic additive, multiplicative, (optionally) regression-coefficient effects autoregressive processes. ame_als() lame_als() fast, MCMC-free static counterparts (see Fast (MCMC-free) estimation), lame(..., method = "als") routes supported dynamic requests dynamic ALS point-estimation path. lame_snap_als() separate fast approximate estimator dynamic snap-shift latent positions normal unipartite bipartite panels.

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: Install GitHub (requires C++ build tools: Xcode CLI macOS, Rtools Windows, build-essential Linux): build_vignettes = TRUE recommended: package ships long-form vignettes (vignette(package = "lame") install), built locally install. first install may take minutes Rcpp / RcppArmadillo C++ code compiles.
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github(     "netify-dev/lame",     dependencies    = TRUE,     build_vignettes = TRUE )

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: R? R dimension multiplicative-effects latent space, small space actor gets coordinates say “tend tie ” beyond covariates sender / receiver effects explain. R = 0 fits additive part (sender + receiver + dyadic correlation); R = 2 adds 2-D latent space captures higher-order structure like homophily (similar actors tying ), clustering, transitivity (friends friends friends). package warns R > floor(n/3) high-rank latent factors can absorb structure belongs additive effects. Start R = 2 first fit; try R = 0, 1, 2, 3 compare gof_plot() panels pick value. See ?ame math guidance. naming gotcha (one beginners hit). ame() / lame() align actors across Y, Xdyad, Xrow, Xcol sorted dimnames. natural-looking idiom silently breaks , alphabetic sort gives "N1","N10","N11",...,"N19","N2","N20",..., "N1","N2",...,"N10",.... positionally-aligned (unnamed) covariate array gets matched re-ordered Y X rows longer correspond Y rows. Two safe patterns: package inherit Y’s dimnames onto unnamed covariate arrays safely can, explicitly matching dimnames Y, Xdyad, Xrow, Xcol always safest option.
code: library(lame)  # load example data # `YX_bin` is a synthetic dataset bundled with the package containing a # 100-actor binary network and 8 dyadic covariates. `data("YX_bin")` # puts the list `YX_bin` into your environment; run `?YX_bin` for the # data dictionary, and inspect it before fitting: data("YX_bin") str(YX_bin, max.level = 1)        # list of 2: Y (100x100), X (100x100x8) Y      <- YX_bin$Y                # 100 x 100 binary "sociomatrix":                                   #   rows = senders, cols = receivers,                                   #   entries 0/1, diagonal NA (self-ties undefined) # the bundled YX_bin$X array carries a literal "intercept" slice # as its first slice, because the package's worked-example tradition is # to include it explicitly. `ame()` adds its own intercept by default # (the `intercept = TRUE` argument), so we drop the redundant slice here # to avoid a rank-deficient design matrix: Xdyad  <- YX_bin$X[, , -1]        # 100 x 100 x 7 array of dyadic covariates # the third dim of a 3-D Xdyad array names the covariates (one slice each): dimnames(Xdyad)[[3]]              # prints: "rgpa" "rsmoke" "cgpa" "csmoke" ...  # fit a cross-sectional ame model on one snapshot # defaults below are sized for a real fit: nscan = 4000 # with odens = 10 gives ~400 stored draws, which is enough to read trace # plots and credible intervals. Drop to nscan = 500 only if you just want # to see the function run. fit <- ame(   Y       = Y,            # n × n network matrix   Xdyad   = Xdyad,        # Dyadic covariates (n × n × p array)   family  = "binary",     # Outcome family   R       = 2,            # Latent dimensions (multiplicative effects, see ?ame)   burn    = 500,          # Burn-in iterations (discarded)   nscan   = 4000,         # Post-burn iterations   odens   = 10,           # Thinning: keep every odens-th iter -> ~400 stored draws   verbose = FALSE         # Silent (drop for a live progress bar) )  summary(fit)              # Coefficients, variance components, GOF # Check convergence on a fresh fit: trace_plot(fit)           # one panel per parameter # gof_plot(fit)           # posterior-predictive GOF rownames(Y) <- colnames(Y) <- paste0("N", 1:25)  # "N1","N2",...,"N10",...,"N25" rownames(Y) <- colnames(Y) <- sprintf("N%02d", 1:25)  # zero-padded: sort matches position # or just give actors real names: rownames(Y) <- colnames(Y) <- c("Alice", "Bob", ...)

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: panel (sequence network snapshots), use lame() pass list Y matrices (lists covariates):
code: # `data("vignette_data")` brings four objects into your environment: #   Y, Xdyad, Xrow, Xcol  -- each a length-4 list (one entry per year), # covering 35 countries observed over 4 years of sanctions data. # See `?vignette_data`. data("vignette_data")     # Y is now a list of 4 binary 35x35 matrices  fit_long <- lame(   Y      = Y,             # List of T matrices   Xdyad  = Xdyad,         # List of T dyadic-covariate arrays   Xrow   = Xrow,          # List of T sender-covariate matrices   Xcol   = Xcol,          # List of T receiver-covariate matrices   family = "binary", R = 2,   burn = 500, nscan = 4000, odens = 10,   verbose = FALSE )  summary(fit_long)

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: lame expects Y (possibly named) matrix, graph object. package ships one-call as_lame_y() helper handles igraph, network, plain matrices, data.frames correct NA diagonal:
code: # igraph / network / statnet / matrix -> matrix Y <- as_lame_y(g)                              # works for any of the above  # manual idioms still work Y <- igraph::as_adjacency_matrix(g, sparse = FALSE); diag(Y) <- NA Y <- as.matrix(net); diag(Y) <- NA

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: data lives tibble (sender, receiver, tie), pivot matrix align row / column order explicitly: common mistake: pivot_wider() preserve row order, naive pivot_wider() |> column_to_rownames() |> .matrix() can produce Y rownames(Y) != colnames(Y), next diag(Y) <- NA writes NA wrong cells. arrange() + Y[actors, actors] step prevents .
code: library(tidyr); library(tibble); library(dplyr) # get a stable actor universe (sorted; or whatever order you want) actors <- sort(unique(c(df$sender, df$receiver))) Y <- df |>   # ensure every (sender, receiver) cell exists, with NA for absent pairs   complete(sender = actors, receiver = actors) |>   arrange(factor(sender, levels = actors)) |>   pivot_wider(names_from = receiver, values_from = tie) |>   column_to_rownames("sender") |>   as.matrix() Y <- Y[actors, actors]    # final guarantee: row / col order matches diag(Y) <- NA              # unipartite only; skip for bipartite

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: lame ergm substitute two models make different dependence assumptions. ERGM specifies joint distribution whole adjacency matrix uses terms like gwesp, triangle, kstar (via change statistics) encode unconditional higher-order dependence among ties. AME instead assumes conditional dyadic independence: given sender effects a_i, receiver effects b_j, latent positions u_i, v_j, dyadic correlation rho, covariates, dyads independent. Higher-order structure (clustering, transitivity, degree heterogeneity) captured indirectly random effects integrate . Practical consequences: AME suffer model-degeneracy failure modes triangle-heavy ERGMs. AME give change-statistic interpretations beta – coefficients partial associations latent (e.g. probit) scale. AME typically -predicts transitivity friendship-style networks (see GOF sections vignette("cross_sec_ame") vignette("lame-overview")). gwesp substantive target, stay ERGM; want stable estimates explicit actor heterogeneity, dynamic effects, forecasts, AME better tool. GOF section vignette("cross_sec_ame") shows score ERGM-style raw triangle, two-path, clustering-coefficient statistics via gof(fit, custom_gof = ...) can read AME diagnostics put ergm::gof().

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: lame exports nodematch(), absdiff(), nodefactor() – direct analogues ERGM terms name – drop straight Xdyad: single cross-section use ame(Y, ...); panel use lame(list(Y_t1, Y_t2, ...), ...). undirected networks pass symmetric = TRUE; two-mode/rectangular data (countries × international organisations, states × treaties) pass mode = "bipartite". Xrow / Xcol accept either numeric matrix data.frame; Xdyad must 3-D n × n × p numeric array. eight families supported modes; pick family matches tie type (continuous, binary, ordered categories, counts, censored, rank lists). symmetric (undirected) networks pass symmetric = TRUE family table applies. Every family works modes, including rank--count families (ordinal, cbin, frn, rrl, poisson) bipartite networks symmetric ordinal; vignette("bipartite") walk-covers .
code: Xdyad <- array(   c(nodematch(group), absdiff(age)),   dim = c(n, n, 2),   dimnames = list(NULL, NULL, c("same_group", "age_diff")) )

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: key extension lame time-varying network effects: predict(fit_dynamic, h = 3, type = "response") forecasts three periods ahead drawing AR(1) / RW1 hyperparameters posterior; predict(..., interval = "credible") returns per-period $lower / $median / $upper matrices. See vignette("forecasting") full forecasting workflow vignette("dynamic_effects") dynamic_beta_kind decision tree.
code: # Fit model with dynamic effects (builds on fit_long and the panel above) fit_dynamic <- lame(   Y = Y,   Xdyad = Xdyad,   Xrow = Xrow,   Xcol = Xcol,   family = "binary",   dynamic_ab = TRUE,        # time-varying sender/receiver effects   dynamic_uv = TRUE,        # time-varying latent positions   dynamic_beta = "dyad",    # time-varying regression coefficients on dyadic Xs   dynamic_beta_kind = "ar1",# "ar1" / "rw1" / "rw2" / "matern32"   R = 2,   burn = 500, nscan = 4000, odens = 10,   prior = list(     rho_uv_mean = 0.9,     rho_ab_mean = 0.8   ),   verbose = FALSE )

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
code: # Additive effects (sender/receiver) ab_plot(fit, effect = "sender")                    # Static effects ab_plot(fit_dynamic, plot_type = "trajectory")     # Dynamic over time  # Multiplicative effects (latent factors) uv_plot(fit)                                       # Static latent positions uv_plot(fit_dynamic, plot_type = "trajectory")     # Dynamic trajectories  # Diagnostics trace_plot(fit)                                     # MCMC convergence gof_plot(fit)                                       # Goodness-of-fit

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: Cross-sectional: ame() fits AME models single network snapshot Longitudinal: lame() fits AME models networks observed multiple time periods Bipartite: full support two-mode (rectangular) networks ame() lame() 8 families: normal, binary, ordinal, poisson, tobit, censored binary, fixed rank nomination, row-ranked likelihood

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: Time-varying latent positions (dynamic_uv): captures evolving community structure via AR(1) processes Time-varying heterogeneity (dynamic_ab): models changing sender/receiver activity time Time-varying regression coefficients (dynamic_beta): lets dyadic, nodal, intercept coefficients evolve via per-block AR(1) processes; selectable per-coefficient per-block; identifiability preserved via automatic sum--zero constraint additive effects Flexible priors: customizable temporal persistence innovation variance

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: S3 methods: coef(), vcov(), confint(), residuals(), predict(), summary(), update(), simulate(). work MCMC fits (ame / lame) ALS fits (ame_als / lame_als). Visualization: trace_plot(), gof_plot(), uv_plot(), ab_plot(). autoplot() returns coefficient--CI plot either fit class. Broom + modelsummary: tidy() glance() registered generics::tidy / generics::glance, broom::tidy(fit), broom::glance(fit), modelsummary::modelsummary(list(...)) work directly MCMC ALS fits. tidy.boot_ame() exposes standalone ame_als_bootstrap() return path composes modelsummary way. Posterior tools: as_draws(fit) posterior / tidybayes ecosystem; loo::loo(fit) leave-one-CV save_log_lik = TRUE set fit time. Simulation GOF: simulate(fit, nsim = K) gof(fit) posterior predictive checks; gof_temporal() adds temporal PP test longitudinal fits. Multi-chain: ame_parallel() / lame_parallel() run multiple chains parallel pool return list, built-R-hat / ESS diagnostics via compute_mcmc_diagnostics(). Multi-panel: lame_multi() fits K panels shared regression coefficients (e.g., cooperation + conflict + trade networks actors). Checkpoint resume: lame(..., checkpoint_path = "X.rds", max_seconds = T) writes checkpoint wall-clock budget expires; resume either lame(resume_from = "X.rds", nscan = K) lame_resume("X.rds", nscan_more = K).

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks
text: ame_als() lame_als() fast, MCMC-free point estimator additive--multiplicative model, adapted Social Influence Regression (SIR) estimator Minhas & Hoff (2025) — iterative block coordinate descent method introduced Decomposing Network Dynamics: Social Influence Regression implemented sir package. port adaptation estimator AME model, original lame methodology. ame_als() / lame_als(): fast AME point estimates via iterative block coordinate descent (alternating least squares / IRLS), typically much faster MCMC sampler since return point estimates rather posterior sample. Useful data exploration, model screening, starting values, large networks. Family scope: normal, binary, poisson; tobit, ordinal, cbin, frn, rrl use MCMC path. lame(..., method = "als") dynamic effects: normal, binary, Poisson panels, dispatcher can fit dynamic additive effects, selected dynamic regression coefficients, AR(1) t-transition dynamic U/V directed, symmetric, bipartite panels, bipartite dynamic G. penalized point estimates. Student-t dynamic UV fits attach final local transition-weight matrices fit$lambda_u fit$lambda_v. Use als_max_iter, als_tol, als_stability dynamic ALS fit needs longer run start-sensitivity check. Node-covariate coefficients use orthogonal additive-effect decomposition lame_als(); dynamic node coefficients use period-specific node values selected dynamic_beta, static node coefficients use per-actor means. Named changing-composition panels aligned union actor set, smoothing penalties broken across actor-entry gaps. Rank/censored families still use MCMC path. Static ALS fits still use single latent rank R; dynamic bipartite ALS honors separate R_row R_col values. lame_snap_als(): fast dynamic snap-shift point estimator normal unipartite bipartite longitudinal networks. returns dynamic U/V, rho_uv, sigma_uv, pi_snap, snap_prob, transition diagnostics. bipartite fits uses static G matrix reports separate row- column-side snap scores. path available unified front door lame(..., method = "als", dynamic_uv = TRUE, dynamic_uv_kind = "snap"). default uses sequential initialization lower-tail drift updates broad ruptures absorbed high-variance drift. snap_prob ALS snap score, Bayesian posterior probability. Read rankings, heuristic 0.5 classifications, stability diagnostics; fit$convergence$max_snap_stable fit$convergence$unstable_transitions show actor-periods still moving. Snap ALS estimate node covariates, dynamic coefficients, dynamic G. ame_als_bootstrap(): block parametric bootstrap standard errors confidence intervals fast estimator. one-shot path ame_als(..., bootstrap = N) attaches bootstrap fit; tidy(fit) / confint(fit) use bootstrap intervals present, sandwich (vcov.ame_als) otherwise.

Random-seed + package version floor; commit hash + renv.lock ceiling.

title: Visualize sender and receiver random effects — ab_plot
text: additive effects AME models represent: Sender effects () Actor-specific tendencies form outgoing ties. Positive values indicate actors send ties expected; negative values indicate actors send fewer ties. Receiver effects (b) Actor-specific tendencies receive incoming ties. Positive values indicate actors receive ties expected; negative values indicate actors receive fewer ties. static effects, plot displays effects dot plot vertical lines extending zero effect estimate. dynamic effects (fit contains a_dynamic/b_dynamic), additional options available visualize effects evolve time.
