what: Penalised ALS time-varying coefficient estimate — als_dynamic_beta
title: Penalised ALS time-varying coefficient estimate — als_dynamic_beta
text: Computes fast point estimate time-varying coefficient vector \(\beta_t\) longitudinal network model solving first-difference-penalised least-squares problem. MCMC, multiplicative effects, random effects — purely regression point estimate smoothing penalty coefficient path. Useful rapid exploration generating starting values full lame(dynamic_beta = ...) fit.

title: Penalised ALS time-varying coefficient estimate — als_dynamic_beta
code: als_dynamic_beta(Y, Xdyad, lambda = 0, intercept = TRUE)

title: Penalised ALS time-varying coefficient estimate — als_dynamic_beta
text: Y list \(T\) response matrices (3-D array third dimension time). Xdyad list \(T\) dyadic covariate arrays (\(n \times n \times p\), single \(n \times n\) matrix \(p=1\)). lambda Non-negative smoothing parameter. Default 0 (penalty, per-period OLS). intercept Logical; include intercept (default TRUE).

title: Penalised ALS time-varying coefficient estimate — als_dynamic_beta
text: list beta (\(p \times T\) matrix estimates), lambda, residual_ss (sum squared residuals across periods), intercept, call. Class "als_dynamic_beta".

title: Penalised ALS time-varying coefficient estimate — als_dynamic_beta
text: $$\min_{\beta_{1:T}} \sum_t \|y_t - X_t \beta_t\|^2 + \lambda   \sum_{t=2}^{T} \|\beta_t - \beta_{t-1}\|^2$$ \(\lambda = 0\) \(T\) independent per-period OLS fits. \(\lambda \\infty\) solution converges single pooled \(\beta\) (constant time).

title: Penalised ALS time-varying coefficient estimate — als_dynamic_beta
text: als_dynamic_beta regression-smoother; returned object carries time-varying \(\beta_t\) alone, additive (\(, b\)) multiplicative (\(U, V\)) AME components. therefore special case lame_als (always estimates full AME decomposition) special case lame(dynamic_beta = TRUE) (Bayesian AR(1) / RW1 / RW2 / Matern 3/2 state-space prior). Use want fast, point-, deterministic smoother coefficient path; use lame(dynamic_beta = ...) need full posterior uncertainty additive / multiplicative effects.

title: Penalised ALS time-varying coefficient estimate — als_dynamic_beta
code: set.seed(1) n <- 10; T <- 4 X <- replicate(T, array(rnorm(n*n*2), c(n, n, 2)), simplify = FALSE) beta_true <- rbind(seq(-1, 1, length.out = T), seq(0.5, -0.5, length.out = T)) Y <- vector("list", T) for (t in seq_len(T)) {   Yt <- X[[t]][, , 1] * beta_true[1, t] + X[[t]][, , 2] * beta_true[2, t] +         matrix(rnorm(n*n, 0, 0.2), n, n)   diag(Yt) <- NA   Y[[t]] <- Yt } fit_als <- als_dynamic_beta(Y, X, lambda = 0)     # per-period LS fit_smooth <- als_dynamic_beta(Y, X, lambda = 10) # smoother path

title: AME model fitting routine — ame
text: MCMC routine providing fit additive multiplicative effects (AME) regression model cross-sectional relational data various types. function supports unipartite (square) bipartite (rectangular) networks. longitudinal networks, use lame function. Original implementation Peter Hoff.

title: AME model fitting routine — ame
code: ame(   Y,   Xdyad = NULL,   Xrow = NULL,   Xcol = NULL,   rvar = !(family == "rrl"),   cvar = TRUE,   dcor = !symmetric,   nvar = TRUE,   R = 0,   R_row = NULL,   R_col = NULL,   mode = c("unipartite", "bipartite"),   family = "normal",   intercept = !is.element(family, c("rrl", "ordinal")),   symmetric = FALSE,   odmax = rep(max(apply(Y > 0, 1, sum, na.rm = TRUE)), nrow(Y)),   prior = list(),   g = NA,   seed = 6886,   nscan = 10000,   burn = 500,   odens = 25,   verbose = TRUE,   gof = TRUE,   custom_gof = NULL,   plot = FALSE,   start_vals = NULL,   periodic_save = FALSE,   out_file = NULL,   save_interval = 0.25,   model.name = NULL,   posterior_opts = NULL,   n_chains = 1,   cores = 1,   use_sparse_matrices = FALSE,   method = c("mcmc", "als"),   bootstrap = 0L,   bootstrap_type = c("parametric", "block"),   bootstrap_block_length = 1L,   bootstrap_seed = NULL,   save_log_lik = FALSE,   ordinal_cutpoints = c("data_induced", "explicit"),   print,   ... )

title: AME model fitting routine — ame
text: Y unipartite: n x n square relational matrix. bipartite: nA x nB rectangular relational matrix nA number row nodes nB number column nodes. See family various data types. Xdyad unipartite: n x n x pd array dyadic covariates (e.g. distance, shared group). bipartite: nA x nB x pd array. 2-D matrix (single dyadic covariate) must wrapped array(x, dim = c(n, n, 1)); Inf/NaN entries rejected. Xrow unipartite: n x pr matrix sender (row) covariates (e.g. sender's age, group). bipartite: nA x pr matrix row-node covariates. data.frame accepted coerced numeric matrix internally. Xcol unipartite: n x pc matrix receiver (column) covariates. bipartite: nB x pc matrix column-node covariates. data.frame accepted coerced internally. rvar logical: fit row random effects (asymmetric case)? cvar logical: fit column random effects (asymmetric case)? dcor logical: fit dyadic correlation (asymmetric case)? Note: used bipartite networks. nvar logical: fit nodal random effects (symmetric case)? R integer: dimension multiplicative effects (can zero). bipartite networks, used default R_row R_col specified. R_row integer: bipartite networks, dimension row node multiplicative effects (defaults R) R_col integer: bipartite networks, dimension column node multiplicative effects (defaults R) mode character: either "unipartite" (default) square networks "bipartite" rectangular networks. combinations family mode supported – see table Supported family x mode combinations . family character: one "normal","tobit","binary","ordinal","cbin","frn","rrl","poisson". See Supported family x mode combinations table combinations valid; see Details model assumptions behind family. intercept logical: fit model intercept? symmetric logical: sociomatrix symmetric design? odmax scalar integer vector length n giving maximum number nominations node may make - used "frn" "cbin" families prior list containing hyperparameters prior distributions. Available options defaults: Sab0 Prior covariance matrix additive effects (default: diag(2)). 2x2 matrix Sab0\[1,1\] prior variance row effects, Sab0\[2,2\] prior variance column effects, -diagonals control correlation row column effects. eta0 Prior degrees freedom covariance multiplicative effects (default: 4 + 3 \* n/100, n number actors). Higher values impose stronger shrinkage latent factors. Common values: 4-10 weak shrinkage, 10-20 moderate, >20 strong shrinkage. etaab Prior degrees freedom covariance additive effects (default: 4 + 3 \* n/100). Controls shrinkage row/column random effects. Larger values shrink effects toward zero. Used bipartite longitudinal paths; ignored unipartite cross-sectional ame() fit, controls additive prior Sab0 eta0. s20 Prior variance regression coefficients (default: 1). Larger values allow larger coefficient values. Used bipartite longitudinal paths; unipartite ame() fit regression prior controlled g. s2u0 Prior variance multiplicative effects (default: 1). Used bipartite longitudinal paths. Suv0 Prior covariance multiplicative effects (default: identity matrix). Common usage: prior = list(Sab0 = diag(c(2, 2)), eta0 = 10) moderate shrinkage, prior = list(Sab0 = diag(c(0.5, 0.5))) tighter control. unipartite cross-sectional ame() fit prior controlled Sab0, eta0, Suv0 g. g optional scalar Zellner g-prior regression coefficients (beta ~ N(0, g * sigma^2 * solve(XtX)) XtX design cross-product). specified, defaults : normal family, g = n * var(Y); tobit, g = n * var(Y) * 4; families, g = n (number non-missing dyads). Per-coefficient (vector) g currently supported unipartite path – pass scalar. Note: g top-level argument ame(), element prior = list(...); passing prior = list(g = 0.1) -op (warned ). seed random seed nscan number iterations Markov chain (beyond burn-) burn burn Markov chain. Typical use burn far smaller nscan; burn > nscan allowed warns. odens output density (thinning interval) Markov chain. nscan / odens samples stored. verbose logical: print progress running? Default TRUE. gof logical: calculate goodness fit statistics? Setting TRUE adds approximately 2-5% runtime. faster sampling without GOF overhead, set gof=FALSE use gof() model fitting. custom_gof optional function list named functions computing custom goodness--fit statistics. function must accept single matrix Y input return numeric vector. single function provided, return named vector. list functions provided, function return single value named according list names. Custom statistics computed addition default statistics. Example: custom_gof = function(Y) c(density = mean(Y > 0, na.rm = TRUE)) plot accepted signature parity lame; ignored. ame() runs single-period MCMC draw live trace panels. Default FALSE. start_vals List previous model run containing parameter starting values new MCMC periodic_save logical: indicating whether periodically save MCMC results out_file character vector indicating name path file stored periodic_save selected. example, Apple OS out_file="~/Desktop/ameFit.rda". save_interval quantile interval indicating save post-burn-period. model.name optional string model selection output posterior_opts optional list posterior sampling options n_chains integer: number MCMC chains run (default: 1) cores integer: number cores parallel chains (default: 1) use_sparse_matrices logical: use sparse matrix storage large networks? (default: FALSE). Recommended truly sparse networks (< 10% non-zero entries). method character: "mcmc" (default, Bayesian MCMC fit) "als" (fast, MCMC-free iterative block coordinate descent point estimator). method = "als", MCMC-specific arguments (nscan, burn, odens, prior, ...) warned ignored; call forwards ame_als. bootstrap integer (used method = "als"): number bootstrap replicates. 0 (default) skips bootstrap; N > 0 runs N replicates attaches result confint returns bootstrap intervals. bootstrap_type character (used method = "als"): "parametric" (default) "block". bootstrap_block_length integer: block length block bootstrap. bootstrap_seed optional integer seed bootstrap. save_log_lik logical: TRUE, attach per-iteration pointwise log-likelihood matrix fit$log_lik (n_iter x n_obs array) using augmented-Z normal approximation. Required loo::loo(fit) loo::waic(fit). augmented-Z approximation semantics used lame(); non-normal families predictive criterion latent-Z scale, family-specific Y density. Default FALSE (log_lik storage; default fit byte-identical previous releases). ordinal_cutpoints character: cutpoint convention family = "ordinal". "data_induced" (default) uses data-induced cutpoints; "explicit" samples explicit cutpoints via Cowles (1996) Metropolis-Hastings update. Ignored families. print Deprecated. Use verbose instead. ... reserved future use. Passing lame()-arguments (e.g. dynamic_beta, period_exposure) triggers clean abort directing lame; passing unrecognised name warns typos visible rather silently dropped.

title: AME model fitting routine — ame
text: Posterior Samples (full MCMC chains): BETA Regression coefficients (nscan xp matrix) VC Variance components (nscan xk matrix) GOF Goodness--fit statistics (nscan x4 matrix). First row contains observed values, remaining rows contain posterior predictive samples. See gof post-hoc computation gof_plot visualization. Posterior Means (averaged chain): APM Additive row/sender effects (n-vector) BPM Additive column/receiver effects (m-vector); NULL symmetric networks U Multiplicative row/sender factors (n xR matrix) V Multiplicative column/receiver factors (m xR matrix); NULL symmetric networks L Eigenvalue matrix (R xR diagonal); symmetric networks YPM Posterior mean Y response scale (predictions imputing missing values) Metadata: family Model family (normal, binary, etc.) mode Network mode (unipartite bipartite) symmetric Logical indicating network symmetric R Dimension multiplicative effects Optional Posterior Samples (requested via posterior_options): U_samples Samples U (n xR xiterations array) V_samples Samples V (m xR xiterations array) a_samples Samples row effects (n xiterations matrix) b_samples Samples column effects (m xiterations matrix) Note reconstructing removed matrices: save memory, EZ (expected latent network) UVPM/ULUPM (multiplicative products) stored can reconstructed using: reconstruct_EZ(fit) - Returns linear predictor (link scale, response scale) reconstruct_UVPM(fit) - Returns U\ Generating posterior distributions: Use simulate_posterior(fit, component="UV") generate posterior samples components means stored, use posterior_options() model fitting save full posterior samples. model.name Name model (provided)

title: AME model fitting routine — ame
text: command provides posterior inference parameters AME models cross-sectional relational data, assuming one eight possible data types/models. function supports unipartite networks (square adjacency matrices) bipartite networks (rectangular adjacency matrices distinct row column node sets) single time point analysis. Model. dyad \((, j)\) AME linear predictor $$\eta_{ij} = \beta_0 + x_{ij}'\beta + a_i + b_j + u_i' D v_j,$$ \(\beta\) regression coefficients dyadic / nodal covariates, \(a_i\) row (sender) random effect, \(b_j\) column (receiver) random effect, \(u_i' D v_j\) multiplicative latent-factor term (rank R). observation model \(Y_{ij} \sim F(\eta_{ij}, \theta)\) \(F\) specified family (Gaussian "normal", probit "binary" / "cbin", etc.). unipartite (symmetric = FALSE) residual error dyad-level correlation \(\rho\) \((, j)\) \((j, )\); bipartite, dyad correlation fixed 0. Priors (brief). \(\beta\) Zellner-style g-prior (g); \((a_i, b_i)\) jointly Normal covariance \(\Sigma_{ab}\) (Inverse-Wishart prior Sab0 / eta0); \(u_i, v_j\) independent Normal covariance \(\Sigma_{uv}\) (Inverse-Wishart prior Suv0 / kappa0); dyad-correlation \(\rho\) Normal prior \(\mathrm{atanh}(\rho)\). See prior_summary(fit) priors actually used. Choosing R. multiplicative rank R controls dimensionality latent homophily / heterogeneity explained covariates additive effects. R = 0 fits additive-social-relations model; R = 1 2 typical small / medium networks; R > floor(n/3) rarely identifiable issue warning. Latent factors capture unobserved structure (clusters, hub patterns, transitive triangles covariates miss) accessed fit$U, fit$V. Identifiability. latent factor term \(u_i' D v_j\) invariant rotation reflection \(U, V\); package canonicalises SVD successive draws interpretable. visual stability across posterior summaries see procrustes_align latent_positions. Theoretical Foundation: AME model decomposes network structure several components: $$y_{ij} = \beta'x_{ij} + a_i + b_j + u_i'v_j + \epsilon_{ij}$$ : \(\beta'x_{ij}\): Fixed effects dyadic/nodal covariates \(a_i\): Additive sender (row) effect node \(b_j\): Additive receiver (column) effect node j \(u_i'v_j\): Multiplicative interaction latent factors \(\epsilon_{ij}\): Dyadic error term (may correlated) specification generalizes social relations model (Warner et al. 1979) latent space models (Hoff et al. 2002) within unified framework. Prior Distributions: model uses conjugate semi-conjugate priors possible: Regression coefficients: \(\beta \sim N(0, g\sigma^2(X'X)^{-1})\) (g-prior) Additive effects: \((a_i, b_i)' \sim N(0, \Sigma_{ab})\) jointly Covariance: \(\Sigma_{ab} \sim IW(\eta_0, \eta_0 S_{ab0})\) (inverse-Wishart) Multiplicative effects: Hierarchical shrinkage via \(\eta_0\) Dyadic correlation: \(\rho \sim Uniform(-1, 1)\) Metropolis updates inverse-Wishart prior \(\Sigma_{ab}\) allows learning correlation sender receiver effects, capturing reciprocity patterns. Multiplicative Effects (Latent Factors): R > 0, model includes R-dimensional latent factors: Asymmetric case: \(u_i, v_j \\mathbb{R}^R\) \(u_i'v_j\) interaction Symmetric case: \(u_i = v_i\) eigendecomposition \(ULU'\) Captures homophily, transitivity, community structure R chosen via model selection set 2-3 visualization Estimation Algorithm: model uses Gibbs sampler following updates: Sample latent Z given parameters (data augmentation non-normal families) Update regression coefficients \(\beta\) via g-prior conjugate update Update additive effects (,b) jointly \(\beta\) Update covariance \(\Sigma_{ab}\) inverse-Wishart Update multiplicative effects U,V via Gibbs Metropolis-Hastings Update dyadic correlation \(\rho\) via Metropolis-Hastings Update variance \(\sigma^2\) (continuous families) Standard Model Types: following data types/models available: "normal": normal AME model (identity link: \(E[Y] = \eta\)). "tobit": tobit AME model censored continuous data. Values censored zero, appropriate non-negative continuous relational data (identity link censoring). "binary": binary probit AME model (probit link: \(P(Y=1) = \Phi(\eta)\)). "ordinal": ordinal probit AME model (cumulative probit link). intercept identifiable model. "cbin": AME model censored binary data (probit link censoring). value 'odmax' specifies maximum number links row may . "frn": AME model fixed rank nomination networks. higher value rank indicates stronger relationship. value 'odmax' specifies maximum number links row may . "rrl": AME model based row ranks. appropriate relationships across rows directly comparable terms scale. intercept, row random effects row regression effects estimable model. "poisson": overdispersed Poisson AME model count data (log link: \(E[Y] = \exp(\eta)\)). linear predictor \(\eta\) represents \(\log(\lambda)\) \(\lambda\) expected count.

title: AME model fitting routine — ame
text: Every family supported modes. bipartite Z-samplers live R/rZ_bipartite.R dispatch per family; see also inline comment R/lame.R (rectangular samplers live R/rZ_bipartite.R). Symmetric (symmetric = TRUE) fits require symmetric Y. family = "ordinal" symmetric = TRUE supported via dedicated sampler R/rZ_ord_sym_fc.R, uses symmetric-doubled precision mirrors upper-triangle draws lower triangle \(Z = t(Z)\) holds every sweep. rrl + bipartite supported: rectangular row-rank Z-sampler handles full per-row permutation likelihood, ame() recovers regression coefficients comparably unipartite rrl path similarly-sized data. Symmetric input one triangle missing. symmetric = TRUE one triangle Y fully NA (user stored lower upper triangle), symmetry validator (.finite(Y - t(Y))) evaluates FALSE call proceeds: sampler treats populated triangle symmetric data mirrors . usually intended, upper / lower triangles meant differ, model silently fitting half data. Audit anyNA(Y[upper.tri(Y)]) != anyNA(Y[lower.tri(Y)]) calling unsure.

what: Converting a network from another R package
title: AME model fitting routine — ame
text: ame() expects Y (potentially named) matrix, graph object. common conversions: igraph: Y <- igraph::as_adjacency_matrix(g, sparse = FALSE);         diag(Y) <- NA network / statnet: Y <- .matrix(net); diag(Y) <- NA tibble / long-format tidyverse: Y <-         tidyr::pivot_wider(df, names_from = receiver, values_from = tie) |>         tibble::column_to_rownames("sender") |> .matrix(); use convenience helper as_lame_y. undirected/symmetric network, pass symmetric = TRUE; rectangular two-mode network (students x courses, donors x candidates), pass mode = "bipartite". Xrow Xcol accept either numeric matrix data.frame (coerced internally); Xdyad must 3-D array n x n x p numeric covariates Inf/NaN.

what: When to use AME vs ERGM
title: AME model fitting routine — ame
text: AME ERGM complementary tools binary network analysis, direct substitutes. ERGM class exponential-family models built around explicit network statistics (counts edges, mutual ties, triangles, geometrically-weighted shared partners, ...). write statistics think matter, ERGM gives coefficients. ERGM excels substantive theory configurations drive tie formation. AME models latent homophily / heterogeneity directly via sender, receiver, multiplicative latent-factor effects. enumerate triadic terms; multiplicative-effects rank R captures higher-order structure (clustering, transitivity, hub patterns) implicitly. AME excels () dyadic / nodal covariates whose effects want interpret cleanly without ERGM degeneracy, (b) higher-order structure "nuisance" want absorb parameterise, (c) need posterior distribution predictions forecasting imputation. Practical guidance: research question "nodes share attribute X tend form triangles together?", reach ERGM's gwesp. research question "controlling unobserved sender / receiver heterogeneity latent clustering, effect dyadic covariate X?", reach AME. R = 0 additive-case social relations model (Warner, Kenny, Stoto 1979); R >= 1 adds latent space.

code: # \donttest{ data(YX_bin) fit <- ame(YX_bin$Y, Xdyad = YX_bin$X, burn = 10, nscan = 100, odens = 1,            family = "binary", verbose = FALSE) summary(fit) #>  #> === AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> [1] "Y ~ dyad(intercept, rgpa, rsmoke, cgpa, csmoke, igrade, igpa, ismoke) + a[i] + b[j] + rho*e[ji], family = 'binary'" #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>                Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper     #> intercept_dyad   -2.543    0.114 -22.296       0   -2.772   -2.319 *** #> rgpa_dyad         0.178    0.081   2.193   0.028    0.047     0.32   * #> rsmoke_dyad       0.254    0.112   2.279   0.023    0.054    0.474   * #> cgpa_dyad         0.188    0.042   4.472       0    0.107    0.273 *** #> csmoke_dyad       0.167    0.052   3.248   0.001    0.067     0.27  ** #> igrade_dyad        1.13    0.052  21.782       0    1.032    1.229 *** #> igpa_dyad         0.049    0.038   1.284   0.199   -0.045    0.123     #> ismoke_dyad       0.038    0.063   0.601   0.548   -0.084     0.15     #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: stars are a visual hint from posterior mean / SD only; for inference use the credible intervals. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.377    0.088 #> cab    0.045    0.019 #> vb     0.073    0.011 #> rho    0.723    0.085 #> ve     1.000    0.000 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) # Note: you should run the Markov chain much longer in practice # }

what: Fast (MCMC-free) AME estimation for a cross-sectional network — ame_als
title: Fast (MCMC-free) AME estimation for a cross-sectional network — ame_als
text: Fits additive multiplicative effects (AME) model single cross-sectional network iterative block coordinate descent, producing fast point estimate MCMC credible intervals. estimation algorithm adapts iterative block coordinate descent estimator Social Influence Regression (SIR) model Hoff & Minhas (algorithm implemented sir::sir_alsfit() nicknamed "ALS" package) AME model. port adaptation, original lame methodology.

title: Fast (MCMC-free) AME estimation for a cross-sectional network — ame_als

title: Fast (MCMC-free) AME estimation for a cross-sectional network — ame_als
text: Y square (unipartite) rectangular (bipartite) relational matrix. Xdyad n_row x n_col matrix n_row x n_col x pd array dyadic covariates, NULL. Xrow n_row x pr matrix row/sender covariates, NULL. Xcol n_col x pc matrix column/receiver covariates, NULL. R integer dimension multiplicative effects (default 0). covariate coefficients conditional choice. multiplicative term \(u_i'v_j\) flexible high-variance regressor can correlate dyadic covariates, estimated beta can shift – occasionally change sign – R increases. Comparing R = 0 fit useful check whether covariate story driven latent rank. family one "normal", "binary", "poisson". rank censoring families MCMC-. mode "unipartite" (square) "bipartite" (rectangular). symmetric logical; fit symmetric (undirected) model. Unipartite . max_iter maximum number block coordinate descent iterations (default 200). tol convergence tolerance relative change residual sum squares (default 1e-6). lowrank_method inner solver multiplicative (low-rank) block: "mm" (default) weighted majorise-minimise; "als" alternating least squares; "hybrid" runs keeps lower-objective result. "als"/"hybrid" converge faster "mm" strongly unbalanced longitudinal panels available directed bipartite models (symmetric fits always use "mm"). three minimise objective, point estimate unchanged balanced data. non_normal_method non-normal ALS families, "irls" (default binary/poisson) runs iteratively reweighted least squares, giving fast approximate GLM AME fit coefficients requested link scale (Poisson log, binary logit/probit). "transform" fits one fixed Gaussian working response (log(y+1) Poisson, rank-normal scores binary); coefficients uncalibrated working scale mainly useful direction/ranking checks. directed R > 0 IRLS fit uses hybrid low-rank solver internally IRLS weights unbalanced. text: AME decomposition $$z_{ij} = \mu + \beta' x_{ij} + a_i + b_j + u_i' v_j + \epsilon_{ij}$$ conditionally linear: linear regression \((\mu, \beta, , b)\) fixed multiplicative factors \((U, V)\), optimal rank-R \((U, V)\) fixed \((\mu, \beta, , b)\) truncated SVD residual matrix. estimator therefore cycles, residual sum squares stabilises, three blocks — monotone (objective-non-increasing) update: joint least-squares solve intercept \(\mu\) regression coefficients \(\beta\) (ginv pseudoinverse fallback rank-deficient designs); Gauss-Seidel sweeps convergence additive effects \((, b)\); weighted low-rank update multiplicative factors \((U, V)\) via SVD (eigen-decomposition symmetric = TRUE). weighting makes multiplicative step correct unbalanced longitudinal panels (dyads observed unequal numbers time points); severely unbalanced panels may need iterations converge, raise max_iter fit reports non-convergence. family = "normal" least-squares (Gaussian maximum- likelihood) fit — exact global solution R = 0, local optimum non-convex low-rank objective R > 0. "binary" "poisson", estimator uses IRLS working-response path default, coefficients requested link scale (binary probit/logit, Poisson log). fixed-transform path still available faster exploratory score, coefficients calibrated effect sizes. Censoring rank families ("ordinal", "tobit", "cbin", "frn", "rrl") supported ALS raise informative error; use ame lame likelihoods. Row/column (node) covariates. node covariate broadcasts per-actor constant, collinear additive sender/receiver effect, coefficient identified objective alone. identified explicit constraint: additive effects taken orthogonal node covariates, beta_row/beta_col corresponding -actor regression coefficients (additive effects carry residual heterogeneity). standard estimand assumption additive effects uncorrelated node covariates; assumption doubtful coefficient absorbs covariate-correlated part additive heterogeneity. Time-varying node covariates summarised per-actor mean; node covariate varies within actor time discarded within-actor variation triggers warning. Identifiability additive multiplicative terms. R > 0 additive term \(a_i + b_j\) multiplicative term \(u_i' v_j\) separately identified objective alone: broadcast (row- column-constant) component can sit either, since pure sender effect \(1'\) rank one. estimator imposes standard AME gauge — multiplicative term double-centered (zero row column means), broadcast structure carried \(, b\) — makes reported \(, b, U, V\) unique given fitted values independent optimisation path. unipartite network double-centering means taken full matrix, includes structurally unobserved self-tie diagonal model fills low-rank completion; additive/multiplicative split therefore carries \(O(1/n)\) dependence completion. disconnected observed-dyad graph additive effects additionally per-component level shift, pinned precision-weighted minimum-norm gauge , b variance components remain reproducible. dyadic covariate (near) low-rank can still partially aliased multiplicative term, keep R modest relative covariate structure. residual aliasing intrinsic AME model — MCMC estimator resolves priors. Choosing R. automatic order-selection criterion (working-response objective likelihood, AIC/BIC apply). Fit values — e.g. lapply(0:4, function(r) ame_als(Y, R = r, ...)) — inspect deviance (residual sum squares): falls steeply real multiplicative signal captured flattens, “elbow” curve reasonable choice. R > 0 objective non-convex; use multistart guard local optima. Uncertainty obtained separately, bootstrap; see ame_als_bootstrap. (SIR paper's primary standard errors Hessian-based, classical sandwich/robust. AME Hessian-based variance awkward two counts: without explicit gauge fix rotational invariance multiplicative factors leaves joint Hessian rank-deficient, even gauge fixed Gaussian working-response approximation used non-normal families leaves Hessian-based variance miscalibrated. bootstrap side-steps , preferred .)

Regenerating additive effects (rather holding fitted , b fixed) gives intercept node-covariate standard errors actor-level sampling-variability component. Assumptions. block bootstrap treats time slices exchangeable replicates static-effects model — appropriate model, strongly trended serially dependent series (use dynamic lame ); time slices necessarily coarse, intervals correspondingly imprecise somewhat anti-conservative, parametric default. variance component fit R > 0 can still carry small residual parametric-bootstrap bias (low-rank refit re-absorbs simulated noise); summary() flags point estimate falls outside interval. binary IRLS fit can carry finite-sample (incidental-parameters) bias point estimator , bootstrap reproduces rather removes. Block bootstrap on a static-effects fit: SEs for the intercept, #> node-covariate coefficients and additive variance components (va, vb, cab) can #> severely under-cover because slice resampling does not perturb actor-level #> structure. Use `type = "parametric"` for those parameters. # }

title: Extract model coefficients from AME model — coef.ame
text: static fit (default, model dynamic_beta = FALSE), coefficients returned named numeric vector computed colMeans(fit$BETA). dynamic fit (lame(..., dynamic_beta = ...) coefficient time-varying), fit$BETA 3-dimensional array [n_stored, p, T] coef.lame returns [p, T] matrix per-period posterior means. Rownames coefficient names; colnames period labels (names(Y) t1, t2, ...). Static coefficients dynamic fit constant across columns. binary models, probit (latent) scale. Use predict.ame type = "response" get predicted probabilities. coef() return. multiplicative latent positions \(U\), \(V\) part coefficient vector; live fit$U fit$V (3-D arrays [n, R, T] dynamic_uv ). additive sender / receiver effects \(, b\) fit$APM fit$BPM. tidy frame latent positions use latent_positions; sender / receiver lollipops use ab_plot.

what: Detect potential change points in a dynamic_beta posterior path — detect_change_point
title: Detect potential change points in a dynamic_beta posterior path — detect_change_point
text: dynamic coefficient dynamic_beta fit, compares posterior distribution maximum scaled first-difference \(M = \max_t |\beta_t - \beta_{t-1}| / \sigma_\beta\) Monte-Carlo approximation statistic AR(1) prior. returned bf column tail-ratio score \(\Pr(M^{post} > m^*) / 0.05\), \(m^*\) 95\ quantile \(M^{prior}\). kept historical column name compatibility, marginal-likelihood Bayes factor. Use surface posterior temporal jumps AR(1) prior comfortably accommodate.

title: Detect potential change points in a dynamic_beta posterior path — detect_change_point
code: detect_change_point(   fit,   coefs = NULL,   n_prior_sims = 2000L,   threshold_bf = 10,   seed = NULL )

title: Detect potential change points in a dynamic_beta posterior path — detect_change_point
text: fit fitted lame object dynamic_beta. coefs Optional character vector coefficient names check. Defaults every dynamic coefficient fit. n_prior_sims Number prior simulations null distribution. Default 2000. threshold_bf Cutoff "warn" column. Default 10; lower 5 false-positive rate AR(1) data high. seed Optional seed reproducibility.

title: Detect potential change points in a dynamic_beta posterior path — detect_change_point
text: data frame one row per dynamic coefficient: coef, bf (heuristic tail-ratio score), m_post_mean (posterior mean \(M\)), m_prior_q95 (95\ \(M\)), t_hat (period largest scaled jump), warn (logical, bf > threshold_bf).

title: Detect potential change points in a dynamic_beta posterior path — detect_change_point

title: Summarise the implied prior on a time-varying coefficient path — dynamic_beta_prior_summary
text: Draws ndraws sample paths length T dynamic_beta prior (given kind, AR(1) hyperparameters, inverse-gamma innovation variance) reports implied distribution common summary statistics: maximum absolute first difference, roughness (sum squared first differences), path range, correlation time. Use fitting confirm prior loose / tight enough.

title: Summarise the implied prior on a time-varying coefficient path — dynamic_beta_prior_summary
code: dynamic_beta_prior_summary(   T = 10L,   ndraws = 5000L,   kind = c("ar1", "rw1", "rw2", "matern32"),   rho_mean = 0.8,   rho_sd = 0.15,   rho_lower = 0,   rho_upper = 0.999,   sigma_shape = 2,   sigma_scale = 1,   matern32_length_scale = 2,   threshold = 1,   seed = NULL )

title: Summarise the implied prior on a time-varying coefficient path — dynamic_beta_prior_summary
text: T Length path (default 10). ndraws Number paths draw (default 5000). kind One "ar1", "rw1", "rw2", "matern32". Default "ar1". "matern32" path drawn continuous-time Matérn-3/2 GP length scale matern32_length_scale marginal variance \(\sigma_\beta^2\). rho_mean, rho_sd Prior mean/SD \(\rho\) (AR(1) ). Used parameterise Beta prior standardised \(\rho\). rho_lower, rho_upper Truncation bounds (AR(1) ). Default [0, 0.999]. sigma_shape, sigma_scale Inverse-Gamma shape / scale \(\sigma_\beta^2\). Defaults shape = 2, scale = 1. matern32_length_scale Length scale Matérn-3/2 covariance (kind = "matern32" ). Default 2. threshold Cutoff prob_max_diff_gt_threshold summary. Default 1. seed Optional integer seed reproducibility.

title: Summarise the implied prior on a time-varying coefficient path — dynamic_beta_prior_summary
text: list components: summary Data frame quantile rows max-first-diff, roughness, path range, trend correlation. prob_max_diff_gt_threshold Empirical probability maximum absolute first difference exceeds threshold. rho Length ndraws vector sampled rho values (NA kinds fixed rho). sigma Length ndraws vector sampled sigma values. paths ndraws x T matrix sample paths.

title: Summarise the implied prior on a time-varying coefficient path — dynamic_beta_prior_summary
text: kind = "ar1", rho_mean/rho_sd translated Beta prior standardised \((\rho - \rho_{lower}) / (\rho_{upper} - \rho_{lower})\). kind = "rw1", \(\rho = 1\) fixed \(\sigma_\beta^2\) sampled. kind = "rw2", second-difference variance sampled first two values use diffuse \(N(0, 10)\) initial prior.

title: Summarise the implied prior on a time-varying coefficient path — dynamic_beta_prior_summary
code: # \donttest{ # Default prior: AR(1) with rho_mean = 0.8, rho_sd = 0.15 s <- dynamic_beta_prior_summary(T = 10, ndraws = 2000, seed = 1) s$summary #>             quantity        q05        q50        q95 #> 1 max_abs_first_diff  0.7215172 1.46787811  3.7138105 #> 2          roughness  1.4113939 5.56455715 31.4268861 #> 3              range  1.1631870 2.62820813  6.6322426 #> 4          trend_cor -0.8732470 0.01310171  0.8807169 # what's the probability that consecutive beta_t differ by more than 1? s$prob_max_diff_gt_threshold #> [1] 0.799  # Tighter prior on innovation variance s_tight <- dynamic_beta_prior_summary(sigma_scale = 0.1, seed = 1) s_tight$summary #>             quantity        q05        q50       q95 #> 1 max_abs_first_diff  0.2176577 0.46198529 1.1278813 #> 2          roughness  0.1372637 0.55602950 3.0626640 #> 3              range  0.3659379 0.82262847 2.0530328 #> 4          trend_cor -0.8787253 0.02435639 0.8788859 # }

what: Held-out predictive evaluation for an ame / lame fit — evaluate_heldout
title: Held-out predictive evaluation for an ame / lame fit — evaluate_heldout
text: Computes family-appropriate held-predictive scores given fit logical mask cells score. function split data : expects either () refit training subset now want score held-cells matrix, (b) predicted probabilities want scored. AUROC PR-AUC reported applicable, alongside Brier mean log density.

title: Held-out predictive evaluation for an ame / lame fit — evaluate_heldout
code: evaluate_heldout(y_obs, y_pred, mask, family = "binary")

title: Held-out predictive evaluation for an ame / lame fit — evaluate_heldout
text: y_obs Observed outcomes. longitudinal fits, list per-period matrices; cross-sectional, single matrix. Cells mask ignored. y_pred Predicted probabilities / means response scale. shape y_obs. mask Logical mask shape y_obs marking cells score (TRUE = include evaluation). family Family string; used pick scoring rule. Defaults "binary".

title: Held-out predictive evaluation for an ame / lame fit — evaluate_heldout
text: one-row data frame columns appropriate family: n_eval, plus auroc + auprc + brier + logloss (binary / cbin), rmse + mae + mean_logdens (normal / tobit), mean_logdens + rmse (poisson).

title: Held-out predictive evaluation for an ame / lame fit — evaluate_heldout
text: Workflow. typical pattern : mask random sample dyads NA Y, refit (lame() handles NA internally via data augmentation), call predict(fit, type = "response"), pass prediction alongside original Y held-mask function. See examples. Dependencies. AUROC / PR-AUC use precrec available; installed, Brier mean log-density computed one-line note emitted.

title: Held-out predictive evaluation for an ame / lame fit — evaluate_heldout
code: # \donttest{ set.seed(1) n <- 25; Y <- matrix(rbinom(n*n, 1, 0.3), n, n); diag(Y) <- NA rownames(Y) <- colnames(Y) <- paste0("a", sprintf("%02d", 1:n)) # mask 20% of dyads mask <- matrix(FALSE, n, n) obs_idx <- which(!is.na(Y)) set.seed(1) mask[sample(obs_idx, floor(0.2 * length(obs_idx)))] <- TRUE Y_train <- Y; Y_train[mask] <- NA fit <- ame(Y_train, R = 0, family = "binary",            burn = 50, nscan = 200, odens = 5, verbose = FALSE, plot = FALSE) y_pred <- predict(fit, type = "response") evaluate_heldout(Y, y_pred, mask, family = "binary") #>   n_eval     auroc     auprc    brier   logloss #> 1    120 0.5659341 0.2869632 0.190125 0.5682987 # }

what: Forward-filter / backward-sample for vec(G_t) under AR(1) state prior — ffbs_vecG
title: Forward-filter / backward-sample for vec(G_t) under AR(1) state prior — ffbs_vecG
text: One Carter-Kohn sweep per-period observations form vec(E_t) = H_t g_t + eps_t, state transition g_t = rho * g_{t-1} + eta_t.

title: Forward-filter / backward-sample for vec(G_t) under AR(1) state prior — ffbs_vecG

title: Forward-filter / backward-sample for vec(G_t) under AR(1) state prior — ffbs_vecG
text: E_cube nA x nB x T residual cube (Z minus base - - b - UV') U_cube nA x RA x T latent row factor cube V_cube nB x RB x T latent column factor cube s2 scalar observation variance rho_G AR(1) coefficient (-1, 1) (use rho = 1 RW1 limit; forward variance clamped via tiny floor). sigma_G2 state innovation variance

title: Forward-filter / backward-sample for vec(G_t) under AR(1) state prior — ffbs_vecG
text: list G_cube (RA x RB x T) vecG_path (p x T draws vectorised state).

what: Probability-integral-transform calibration check for h-step forecasts — forecast_pit
title: Probability-integral-transform calibration check for h-step forecasts — forecast_pit
text: fit least one dynamic component, evaluates well h-step posterior-predictive distribution covers actually observed outcomes corresponding period(s). well-calibrated forecast produces approximately Uniform(0, 1) PIT values.

title: Probability-integral-transform calibration check for h-step forecasts — forecast_pit
code: forecast_pit(fit, y_future, h = NULL, n_draws = NULL)

title: Probability-integral-transform calibration check for h-step forecasts — forecast_pit
text: fit fitted lame object trained first T - h periods (function refit; user responsible train/forecast split). y_future list length h held-observed matrices, one per forecast period. shape fit$YPM[[t]] elements. h forecast horizon (length y_future). Inferred length(y_future) supplied. n_draws Number posterior draws use forecast. Default uses stored draws.

title: Probability-integral-transform calibration check for h-step forecasts — forecast_pit
text: list class forecast_pit components pit (numeric vector), ks_stat (KS statistic Uniform), ks_p (KS p-value), cover_95 (fraction PIT values [0.025, 0.975]).

title: Probability-integral-transform calibration check for h-step forecasts — forecast_pit
text: Workflow: fit periods 1:(T - h), forecast h steps ahead, score held-T - h + 1 : T observations. Returns numeric vector PIT values (one per observed dyad held-periods), plus one-row summary Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic Uniform(0, 1) fraction--observed-dyads coverage diagnostic. Families. family = "normal" / "tobit", PIT computed analytically per-draw forecast variance. discrete families ("binary", "poisson", "ordinal") randomised PIT Czado, Gneiting & Held (2009) used. rank families ("cbin", "frn", "rrl") PIT implemented; function returns NULL informational note. Plotting. companion plot() method returned object renders histogram uniform reference line. Use ggplot2::ggplot(pit$pit) + geom_histogram() custom plots.

title: Probability-integral-transform calibration check for h-step forecasts — forecast_pit
text: Czado, C., Gneiting, T., & Held, L. (2009). Predictive model assessment count data. Biometrics, 65(4), 1254-1261.

title: Probability-integral-transform calibration check for h-step forecasts — forecast_pit
code: # \donttest{ data(YX_bin_list) Y_train <- YX_bin_list$Y[1:3] Y_test <- YX_bin_list$Y[4] X_train <- YX_bin_list$X[1:3] fit <- lame(Y_train, Xdyad = X_train,             family = "binary", R = 0,             dynamic_beta = "dyad",             nscan = 200, burn = 50, odens = 5, verbose = FALSE) #> Warning: `family` = "binary" but `Y` contains values other than 0/1. #> ℹ `Y` will be thresholded to `1 * (Y > 0)`; if you meant counts, use "poisson", #>   or "ordinal"/"normal" as appropriate. pit <- forecast_pit(fit, y_future = Y_test) #> Warning: ! Posterior 95% interval for `rho_beta` reaches 0.994. #> ℹ Under AR(1) the `h`-step forecast variance saturates at the stationary level #>   `sigma_beta^2 / (1 - rho_beta^2)`, which itself explodes as `rho_beta -> 1`; #>   horizons beyond "3" become uninformative in this regime. #> ℹ Consider refitting with `dynamic_beta_kind = "rw1"` if the underlying process #>   is genuinely unit-root. pit$ks_p #> [1] 7.350344e-288 plot(pit)  # }

title: Compute EZ when beta is time-varying — get_EZ_dynamic_beta_cpp
text: Rebuild (n x n x T) (nA x nB x T) EZ cube using per-period beta vector. Mirrors get_EZ_cpp / get_EZ_bip_cpp accepts (T x p) beta matrix row beta period.

title: Compute EZ when beta is time-varying — get_EZ_dynamic_beta_cpp
code: get_EZ_dynamic_beta_cpp(   Xlist,   beta_full_path,   a_mat,   b_mat,   U_cube,   V_cube,   G,   bipartite,   symmetric )

title: Compute EZ when beta is time-varying — get_EZ_dynamic_beta_cpp
text: Xlist T-length list design arrays (n x n x p nA x nB x p). beta_full_path (T x p) matrix per-period beta. coefficients dynamic block, rows identical (static beta replicated across periods). a_mat (n_a x T) row effects (repmat-ed static effects). b_mat (n_b x T) column effects. U_cube (n_u x R x T) row latent positions (replicated-static). V_cube (n_v x R x T) column latent positions. G (R x R RA x RB) interaction matrix (bipartite); identity unipartite. bipartite TRUE/FALSE. symmetric TRUE/FALSE.

title: Compute EZ when beta is time-varying — get_EZ_dynamic_beta_cpp
text: n_a x n_b x T cube EZ values.

code: get_fit_object(   APS,   BPS,   UVPS,   YPS,   BETA,   VC,   GOF,   Xlist,   actorByYr,   colActorByYr = NULL,   start_vals,   symmetric,   tryErrorChecks,   model.name = NULL,   U = NULL,   V = NULL,   dynamic_uv = FALSE,   dynamic_ab = FALSE,   bip = FALSE,   rho_ab = NULL,   rho_uv = NULL,   family = NULL,   odmax = NULL,   nA = NULL,   nB = NULL,   n_time = NULL,   Y_obs = NULL,   G = NULL,   dynamic_beta = FALSE,   beta_dynamic_mask = NULL,   beta_dynamic_groups = NULL,   rho_beta = NULL,   sigma_beta = NULL,   RHO_BETA = NULL,   SIGMA_BETA = NULL )

title: Get fitted object from MCMC results — get_fit_object
text: APS summed additive sender random effects (matrix dynamic) BPS summed additive receiver random effects (matrix dynamic) UVPS summed multiplicative random effects YPS summed Y posterior predictive values BETA Matrix draws regression coefficient estimates VC Matrix draws variance estimates GOF Matrix draws goodness fit calculations Xlist List based version design array actorByYr List actors time point. bipartite mode per-year list row actors. colActorByYr Bipartite . List column actors time point; defaults NULL (unipartite). start_vals start_vals future model run symmetric logical indicating whether model symmetric tryErrorChecks list counts MCMC errors model.name Name model (optional) U Latent sender positions (optional, dynamic UV) V Latent receiver positions (optional, dynamic UV) dynamic_uv logical indicating whether UV effects dynamic dynamic_ab logical indicating whether additive effects dynamic bip logical indicating whether network bipartite rho_ab temporal correlation parameter additive effects (optional) rho_uv temporal correlation parameter multiplicative effects (optional) family character string specifying model family (e.g., "binary", "normal", "poisson") odmax vector maximum ranks ordinal fixed rank nomination families nA number actors first mode (bipartite networks) nB number actors second mode (bipartite networks) n_time number time periods (longitudinal models) Y_obs original observed network (stored residuals computation) G bipartite interaction matrix mapping row column latent spaces dynamic_beta logical scalar; whether BETA storage 3-D (dynamic_beta path). Default FALSE. beta_dynamic_mask logical vector marking coefficients dynamic. beta_dynamic_groups character vector per-coefficient block labels ("intercept", "dyad", "row", "col"); "" static coefficients. rho_beta named numeric vector per-block AR(1) rho values (one per dynamic block). sigma_beta named numeric vector per-block AR(1) innovation standard deviations. RHO_BETA matrix per-iteration rho_beta draws (rows = MCMC draw, cols = dynamic block). SIGMA_BETA matrix per-iteration sigma_beta draws.

what: Compute GOF statistics from saved posterior samples — gof
title: Compute GOF statistics from saved posterior samples — gof
text: Computes goodness--fit statistics model estimation generating posterior predictive networks saved MCMC samples. useful model fitted gof = FALSE speed MCMC sampling, want evaluate custom GOF statistics without re-running model.

title: Compute GOF statistics from saved posterior samples — gof
code: gof(fit, Y = NULL, custom_gof = NULL, nsim = 100, verbose = TRUE)

title: Compute GOF statistics from saved posterior samples — gof
text: fit ame model object run posterior sampling enabled Y Original data. ame objects, n x n matrix (nA x nB bipartite). lame objects, list matrices (one per time period). NULL, extracted fit object. custom_gof Optional custom GOF function(s) - format ame() nsim Number posterior predictive simulations generate (default 100). NULL, uses available posterior samples. verbose Logical; print progress information

title: Compute GOF statistics from saved posterior samples — gof
text: matrix GOF statistics format gof=TRUE used model estimation. First row contains observed statistics, subsequent rows contain posterior predictive statistics.

title: Compute GOF statistics from saved posterior samples — gof
text: function requires model estimated posterior sampling parameters needed generate posterior predictive datasets. Specifically, needs: BETA: regression coefficients VC: variance components models random effects: samples , b models latent factors: U_samples, V_samples enable posterior sampling model estimation, use: posterior_opts = posterior_options(save_UV = TRUE, save_ab = TRUE) Computing GOF post-hoc several advantages: Faster MCMC sampling (GOF overhead) Can experiment different GOF statistics without re-running model Can control number posterior predictive simulations independently

title: Compute GOF statistics from saved posterior samples — gof
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas, Tosin Salau

title: Compute GOF statistics from saved posterior samples — gof
code: # \donttest{ # Run model without GOF during fitting data(YX_nrm) fit <- ame(YX_nrm$Y, Xdyad = YX_nrm$X, R = 2, gof = FALSE,            nscan = 100, burn = 10, odens = 1, verbose = FALSE)  # Compute GOF post-hoc gof_result <- gof(fit) #> ℹ Computing GOF statistics post-hoc #> ℹ Using 99 posterior predictive simulations #> ✔ GOF computation complete # }

Unipartite names: "sd.row", "sd.col", "dyad.dep", "triad.dep", "trans.dep"; bipartite names: "sd.row", "sd.col", "four.cycles"; custom GOF columns may also named. Unknown names dropped warning. credible.level Numeric 0 1; credible interval level longitudinal plots (default 0.95) ncol Number columns faceted plot layout (default 2) point.size Size points longitudinal plots (default 2) line.size Width lines plots (default 1) title Optional title plot ... title: Visualize goodness-of-fit statistics for AME and LAME models — gof_plot
text: ggplot2 object can customized

title: Visualize goodness-of-fit statistics for AME and LAME models — gof_plot
text: Overview: Goodness--fit (GOF) assessment crucial network models standard residual diagnostics often inadequate capturing network dependencies. function implements posterior predictive checking : Computing key network statistics observed data Generating multiple networks model's posterior predictive distribution Computing statistics simulated networks Visualizing comparison identify model inadequacies Network Statistics Evaluated: unipartite (square) networks: sd.row (-degree heterogeneity) Standard deviation row means. High values indicate substantial variation active nodes senders/initiators. model underestimates , may missing important sender effects covariates. sd.col (-degree heterogeneity) Standard deviation column means. High values indicate substantial variation node popularity receivers. Underestimation suggests missing receiver effects popularity-related covariates. dyad.dep (Reciprocity/Mutuality) Correlation Y[,j] Y[j,]. Positive values indicate reciprocity (mutual ties likely). AME model captures dyadic correlation parameter rho. Poor fit points adjusting dcor parameter. triad.dep (Transitivity/Clustering) Measures tendency triadic closure (friend friend friend). Calculated correlation Y[,j] sum(Y[,k]*Y[k,j])/sqrt(n-2). AME captures multiplicative effects (U,V). Poor fit suggests increasing latent dimension R. bipartite (rectangular) networks: sd.row (Type activity variation) Standard deviation row means Type nodes. Indicates heterogeneity actively Type nodes connect Type B nodes. sd.col (Type B popularity variation) Standard deviation column means Type B nodes. Indicates heterogeneity popular Type B nodes Type nodes. four.cycles (Bipartite clustering) Count 4-cycles (rectangular paths A1-B1-A2-B2-A1). High values indicate pairs Type nodes tend connect Type B nodes. Captured bipartite multiplicative effects appropriate R_row, R_col. Interpretation Guide: Histogram plots (static models): Dashed orange vertical line (Okabe-Ito #D55E00): observed statistic value (dual-encoded colour linetype) Grey histogram: distribution posterior predictive simulations Good fit: orange line falls within bulk histogram Poor fit: orange line tail outside distribution Common model inadequacies solutions: Observed sd.row/sd.col high Model underestimates degree heterogeneity. Solutions: Add row/column covariates (Xrow, Xcol), enable random effects (rvar=TRUE, cvar=TRUE), increase variance. Observed dyad.dep outside distribution Reciprocity captured well. Solutions: positive reciprocity, ensure dcor=TRUE. negative, consider transformation different family. Observed triad.dep high Clustering/transitivity underestimated. Solutions: Increase latent dimension R, add network covariates capture homophily, consider including community structure covariates. Multiple statistics showing poor fit Fundamental model misspecification. Solutions: Change family, add missing covariates, consider different model class. Mathematical Details: posterior predictive p-value statistic s : $$p = P(s(Y_{rep}) \geq s(Y_{obs}) | Y_{obs})$$ Y_rep drawn posterior predictive distribution. Values near 0 1 indicate poor fit. function visualizes full distribution rather just p-values richer diagnostics. Customization: function accepts custom GOF statistics model's custom_gof argument. automatically included plot. Custom statistics capture network features important specific application. Computational Notes: GOF computation involves simulating multiple networks, can computationally intensive. model uses networks simulated MCMC gof=TRUE specified. post-hoc GOF, use gof() function generates new simulations. Good model fit indicated : Observed statistics fall within central 95% posterior predictive distributions systematic patterns /underestimation across statistics Custom statistics (provided) also show adequate fit longitudinal models, observed trajectories track within credible bands

title: Visualize goodness-of-fit statistics for AME and LAME models — gof_plot

title: Goodness of fit statistics for bipartite networks — gof_stats_bipartite
text: named numeric vector containing bipartite-specific goodness--fit statistics: sd.rowmean Standard deviation row means. Measures heterogeneity -degree set nodes (sender effects). Higher values indicate variation active nodes . sd.colmean Standard deviation column means. Measures heterogeneity -degree set B nodes (receiver effects). Higher values indicate variation popular B nodes . four.cycles Count four-cycles (also called 4-paths squares) bipartite network. four-cycle occurs two nodes set (e.g., k) connect two nodes set B (e.g., j l), forming closed path: ->j->k->l->. measures tendency pairs -nodes share multiple common B-node connections, capturing form clustering specific bipartite networks. High four-cycle counts indicate connections random show patterns shared preferences co-occurrence. example, user-item network, many four-cycles suggest users like one item tend also like items co-occur .

title: Posterior-predictive temporal-trend test — gof_temporal
text: fitted lame object, computes network statistic (density, reciprocity, transitivity) observed period, fits least-squares linear trend period index, compares observed slope slopes posterior-predictive replicates. two-sided value p_pp = 2 * min(p_up, 1 - p_up), runs 0 (observed slope extreme tail) 1 (observed slope dead-centre). static fit truly trending data yields p_pp near 0; dynamic fit captures trend yields p_pp near 1.

title: Posterior-predictive temporal-trend test — gof_temporal
code: gof_temporal(   fit,   stat = c("auto", "density", "mean", "reciprocity", "transitivity"),   n_rep = 500,   seed = NULL )

title: Posterior-predictive temporal-trend test — gof_temporal
text: fit fitted lame object. stat One "auto" (default; picks "reciprocity" unipartite directed fits \(T \ge 3\) family normal / poisson / tobit, "density" constant uninformative; otherwise "density"), "density", "mean" (mean -diagonal Y; right "density" analogue continuous outcomes), "reciprocity", "transitivity". n_rep Number posterior-predictive replicates draw (replicate full \(T\)-period network simulate(fit)). seed Optional RNG seed.

title: Posterior-predictive temporal-trend test — gof_temporal
text: list stat (chosen statistic name), slope_obs (observed slope stat period index), slope_rep (length-n_rep vector replicate slopes), p_pp (two-sided posterior-predictive p-value), stat_obs_by_t, stat_rep_by_t (per-period statistics)

title: Posterior-predictive temporal-trend test — gof_temporal
code: # \donttest{ data(YX_bin_list) fit <- lame(YX_bin_list$Y, YX_bin_list$X, family = "binary", R = 0,             dynamic_beta = "dyad",             nscan = 200, burn = 50, odens = 5, verbose = FALSE) #> Warning: `family` = "binary" but `Y` contains values other than 0/1. #> ℹ `Y` will be thresholded to `1 * (Y > 0)`; if you meant counts, use "poisson", #>   or "ordinal"/"normal" as appropriate. gof_temporal(fit, stat = "density", n_rep = 100) #>  #> ── Temporal-trend posterior-predictive check ── #>  #> • Statistic: "density" #> • Observed slope (per-period): -0.00068 #> • Replicates: 100 #> • Posterior-predictive p-value (two-sided): 0.8 #> Observed temporal trend is well covered by the fitted model. # }

what: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks — lame-package
title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks — lame-package
text: extension 'amen' package specifically designed longitudinal network analysis. package provides tools fitting Additive Multiplicative Effects (AME) models longitudinal/replicated relational data several extensions: (1) handling changing actor compositions across time periods, allowing networks different sets actors time point; (2) significant performance improvements C++ implementations via Rcpp RcppArmadillo; (3) specialized functions temporal network dynamics. package supports eight data types: normal (nrm), binary (bin), ordinal (ord), Poisson count (poisson), tobit/censored continuous (tobit), censored binary (cbin), fixed-rank nomination (frn), row-ranked (rrl). Based AME framework originally developed Hoff (2009) Hoff, Fosdick, Volfovsky Stovel (2013).

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks — lame-package
text: Estimators. package offers two estimation routes: ame / lame — Bayesian MCMC estimators, calibrated posterior inference (cross-sectional longitudinal respectively). ame_als / lame_als — fast, MCMC-free point estimator iterative block coordinate descent, bootstrap uncertainty via ame_als_bootstrap. Use rapid model exploration, rank selection starting values; use MCMC estimators final inference.

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks — lame-package
text: Shahryar Minhas, Tosin Salau, Cassy Dorff Maintainer: Shahryar Minhas minhassh@msu.edu

title: Longitudinal Additive and Multiplicative Effects Models for Networks — lame-package
code: # \donttest{ data(YX_nrm) fit <- ame(YX_nrm$Y, Xdyad = YX_nrm$X, burn = 10, nscan = 100, odens = 1,            family = "normal", verbose = FALSE) summary(fit) #>  #> === AME Model Summary === #>  #> Call: #> [1] "Y ~ dyad(intercept, rgpa, rsmoke, cgpa, csmoke, igrade, igpa, ismoke) + a[i] + b[j] + rho*e[ji], family = 'normal'" #>  #> Regression coefficients: #> ------------------------ #>                Estimate StdError z_value p_value CI_lower CI_upper     #> intercept_dyad   -2.709    0.115 -23.527       0   -2.901   -2.515 *** #> rgpa_dyad         0.219    0.101   2.165    0.03    0.025    0.444   * #> rsmoke_dyad       0.263     0.12   2.186   0.029    0.051    0.515   * #> cgpa_dyad         0.164    0.039   4.165       0    0.091    0.241 *** #> csmoke_dyad       0.112    0.056   1.995   0.046        0    0.205   * #> igrade_dyad       1.129    0.037  30.452       0    1.059    1.197 *** #> igpa_dyad         0.055    0.016   3.413   0.001     0.02    0.081 *** #> ismoke_dyad       0.029    0.025   1.166   0.243   -0.018    0.075     #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Note: stars are a visual hint from posterior mean / SD only; for inference use the credible intervals. #>  #> Variance components: #> ------------------- #>     Estimate StdError #> va     0.853    0.120 #> cab    0.094    0.034 #> vb     0.120    0.019 #> rho    0.920    0.002 #> ve     1.053    0.020 #>   (va = sender, cab = sender-receiver covariance, vb = receiver, #>    rho = dyadic correlation, ve = residual variance) # }
